  • End-of-Chapter Exercises

Chapter 4: Exploring Algebraic Identities

Topics Explained — Chapter 4Each topic explained in simple, understandable English

§4.1 — What are Algebraic Identities?

An identity is an equation that is true for ALL values of the variable — no matter what number you plug in!

Difference between an equation and an identity:

  • Equation: 2x + 3 = 7 → only true when x = 2
  • Identity: (a+b)² = a² + 2ab + b² → true for EVERY value of a and b!

Why identities are useful:

  • They let you expand expressions quickly (break brackets open)
  • They let you factorise expressions (put brackets back)
  • They help you do mental math (calculate 99² in your head!)
  • They show up everywhere in higher math, physics, and engineering

The big 3 identities you MUST memorize:

  1. (a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b²
  2. (a − b)² = a² − 2ab + b²
  3. (a + b)(a − b) = a² − b²

§4.2 — SEE Why Identities Work (Visual Proof)

You can actually see why (a+b)² = a² + 2ab + b² using a picture!

Draw a square with side (a+b):

  • The big square has area = (a+b)²
  • Now divide it into 4 parts: one a×a square, one b×b square, and two a×b rectangles
  • Total area = a² + b² + ab + ab = a² + 2ab + b² ✓

Algebra tiles make this hands-on: Use square tiles (for x²), rectangle tiles (for x), and unit tiles (for 1). Arrange them to form rectangles and you can "see" factorisation!

Quick mental math using identities:

  • 103² = (100+3)² = 10000 + 600 + 9 = 10609
  • 97² = (100−3)² = 10000 − 600 + 9 = 9409
  • 52 × 48 = (50+2)(50−2) = 2500 − 4 = 2496

§4.2b — Visual Proof: (a − b)² = a² − 2ab + b²

Start with a square of side a. Remove strips of width b from two sides. You've removed too much (the corner was counted twice), so add back one b² square!

§4.2c — Visual Proof: (a+b)(a−b) = a² − b²

This identity says: the product of (sum) × (difference) = difference of their squares!

§4.3 — Factorisation: Putting Brackets Back

Factorisation is the reverse of expanding — you take an expression and write it as a product (multiplication) of simpler expressions.

Using identities to factorise:

  • See a² + 2ab + b²? → It's (a+b)² [look for: two perfect squares + twice their product]
  • See a² − 2ab + b²? → It's (a−b)²
  • See a² − b²? → It's (a+b)(a−b) [difference of two squares — super common!]

Examples:

  • x² + 6x + 9 = x² + 2(3)(x) + 3² = (x+3)²
  • 25x² − 49 = (5x)² − 7² = (5x+7)(5x−7)
  • 4y² − 12y + 9 = (2y)² − 2(2y)(3) + 3² = (2y−3)²

Tip: Always first check: "Is this a perfect square ± twice product ± perfect square?" or "Is this a difference of two squares?"

§4.4 — Cubic Identities (Power of 3)

These are identities involving cubes (power 3). They look scary but follow patterns!

The cube identities:

  • (a+b)³ = a³ + 3a²b + 3ab² + b³ [coefficients: 1, 3, 3, 1 — Pascal's triangle!]
  • (a−b)³ = a³ − 3a²b + 3ab² − b³ [same but alternating signs]
  • a³ + b³ = (a+b)(a² − ab + b²) [sum of cubes]
  • a³ − b³ = (a−b)(a² + ab + b²) [difference of cubes]

Memory trick for (a+b)³: Think of powers decreasing for a (a³, a², a¹, a⁰) and increasing for b (b⁰, b¹, b², b³), with coefficients 1-3-3-1.

Quick example: (x+2)³ = x³ + 3(x²)(2) + 3(x)(4) + 8 = x³ + 6x² + 12x + 8

To factorise a³ + b³ or a³ − b³: First identify what "a" and "b" are (take cube roots), then apply the formula directly.

§4.5 — Factorisation with Algebra Tiles (Visual Method)

Algebra tiles are a visual, hands-on way to understand factorisation. Think of it like a puzzle!

The tiles:

  • Big square tile = x² (area x × x)
  • Rectangle tile = x (area x × 1)
  • Small unit square = 1 (area 1 × 1)

How it works: To factorise x² + 5x + 6, try to arrange one x² tile, five x tiles, and six unit tiles into a perfect rectangle. The sides of that rectangle give you the factors!

The rectangle turns out to be (x+2) wide and (x+3) tall → so x² + 5x + 6 = (x+2)(x+3) ✓

§4.6 — Splitting the Middle Term (Most Important Method!)

For expressions like x² + bx + c, find two numbers that:

  • Multiply to give c (the constant)
  • Add to give b (the coefficient of x)

Step-by-step example: Factorise x² + 7x + 12

  1. Need two numbers that multiply to 12 AND add to 7
  2. Try: 1×12=12, 1+12=13 ✗ | 2×6=12, 2+6=8 ✗ | 3×4=12, 3+4=7 ✓
  3. Split: x² + 3x + 4x + 12
  4. Group: x(x+3) + 4(x+3)
  5. Factor: (x+3)(x+4) ✓

For harder ones like 2x² + 7x + 3: Multiply the coefficient of x² with the constant (2×3=6). Now find two numbers that multiply to 6 and add to 7: that's 6 and 1. Split: 2x² + 6x + x + 3 = 2x(x+3) + 1(x+3) = (2x+1)(x+3).

§4.7 — More Useful Identities

You can create new identities from the basic ones!

Important one: (a + b + c)² = a² + b² + c² + 2ab + 2bc + 2ca

Useful derived formulas:

  • a² + b² = (a+b)² − 2ab [subtract 2ab from both sides of identity 1]
  • a² + b² = (a−b)² + 2ab [add 2ab to both sides of identity 2]
  • (a+b)² − (a−b)² = 4ab
  • (a+b)² + (a−b)² = 2(a² + b²)

These help solve problems like: "If a+b = 5 and ab = 6, find a²+b²"

Answer: a²+b² = (a+b)² − 2ab = 25 − 12 = 13. Done in one step!

§4.8 — Simplifying Fractions with Variables

A rational expression is a fraction where the numerator and denominator are polynomials.

Rule: To simplify, factorise the top AND the bottom, then cancel common factors.

Example: Simplify (x²−9)/(x+3)

  1. Factorise top: x²−9 = (x+3)(x−3)
  2. Now: (x+3)(x−3)/(x+3)
  3. Cancel (x+3): Answer = x−3

⚠️ Important warning: You can only cancel FACTORS (things being multiplied), NOT terms (things being added). You CANNOT cancel the x in (x+3)/(x+5)!

🎭 What Are Algebraic Identities? (Easy Way)

An algebraic identity is an equation that is true for ALL values of the variable, not just some values.

✓ Identity

(x + 1)² = x² + 2x + 1

True for x = 0? (0+1)² = 1, and 0+0+1 = 1 ✓

True for x = 5? (5+1)² = 36, and 25+10+1 = 36 ✓

True for ALL x! → identity

✗ Equation (NOT identity)

x² + 1 = 5

True for x = 2? 4+1 = 5 ✓

True for x = 0? 0+1 = 1 ≠ 5 ✗

Only true for SOME values → equation

Why are identities useful? They are shortcuts! Instead of multiplying (47)² = 47 × 47 the hard way, you can use the identity (a+b)² = a² + 2ab + b²:

(47)² = (50 − 3)² = 50² − 2(50)(3) + 3² = 2500 − 300 + 9 = 2209 (mental math!)

🌟 The 3 Most Important Identities (Memorize These!)

The Holy Trinity of Algebra:

1. (a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b²

2. (a − b)² = a² − 2ab + b²

3. (a + b)(a − b) = a² − b²

📝 Identity #1: (a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b²

This is the most-used identity in algebra. Let's see it in action:

📝 Worked Example 1: Expand (x + 5)²

Step 1 — Identify a and b: a = x, b = 5

Step 2 — Apply formula: (x + 5)² = x² + 2(x)(5) + 5²

Step 3 — Simplify: = x² + 10x + 25

📝 Worked Example 2: Expand (2x + 3y)²

a = 2x, b = 3y

(2x + 3y)² = (2x)² + 2(2x)(3y) + (3y)²

= 4x² + 12xy + 9y²

📝 Worked Example 3: Calculate (103)² mentally!

Write 103 = 100 + 3, so (103)² = (100 + 3)²

= 100² + 2(100)(3) + 3²

= 10000 + 600 + 9 = 10609

💡 Faster than multiplying 103 × 103 by hand!

📝 Identity #2: (a − b)² = a² − 2ab + b²

Same as #1, but the middle term has a minus sign. The end terms (a² and b²) are always positive (squares of anything are positive!).

📝 Worked Example: Expand (x − 4)²

a = x, b = 4

(x − 4)² = x² − 2(x)(4) + 4²

= x² − 8x + 16

📝 Quick Trick: (97)² mentally

97 = 100 − 3, so (97)² = (100 − 3)²

= 100² − 2(100)(3) + 3²

= 10000 − 600 + 9 = 9409

💡 Remember the sign pattern!

(a + b)² → +, +, + (all plus)

(a − b)² → +, −, + (only middle is minus)

The first and last terms are always positive!

📝 Identity #3: (a + b)(a − b) = a² − b²

This is the "difference of squares" identity. Notice how the middle terms cancel out!

📝 Quick examples

  • (x + 3)(x − 3) = x² − 9
  • (2y + 5)(2y − 5) = (2y)² − 5² = 4y² − 25
  • (p + q)(p − q) = p² − q²
  • (a + 1/a)(a − 1/a) = a² − 1/a²

📝 Magic Trick: Calculate 99 × 101 instantly!

99 × 101 = (100 − 1)(100 + 1) = 100² − 1² = 10000 − 1 = 9999!

Try: 47 × 53 = (50 − 3)(50 + 3) = 2500 − 9 = 2491

📐 The Three-Term Identity: (a + b + c)²

What if you have THREE terms? There's an identity for that too:

(a + b + c)² = a² + b² + c² + 2ab + 2bc + 2ca

Pattern: squares of each term + 2 times each pair. With 3 terms there are 3 pairs: ab, bc, ca.

📝 Worked Example: (x + 2y + 3z)²

= x² + (2y)² + (3z)² + 2(x)(2y) + 2(2y)(3z) + 2(3z)(x)

= x² + 4y² + 9z² + 4xy + 12yz + 6zx

💡 Pattern recognition: How many terms?

(a + b)² → 3 terms (a², b², 2ab)

(a + b + c)² → 6 terms (3 squares + 3 cross terms)

(a + b + c + d)² → 10 terms (4 squares + 6 cross terms)

📦 Cubic Identities — Powers of 3

Three more identities for cubes:

Cubic Identities

4. (a + b)³ = a³ + 3a²b + 3ab² + b³

5. (a − b)³ = a³ − 3a²b + 3ab² − b³

6. a³ + b³ = (a + b)(a² − ab + b²)

7. a³ − b³ = (a − b)(a² + ab + b²)

8. a³ + b³ + c³ − 3abc = (a + b + c)(a² + b² + c² − ab − bc − ca)

📝 Example: Expand (x + 2)³

a = x, b = 2

(x + 2)³ = x³ + 3·x²·2 + 3·x·2² + 2³

= x³ + 6x² + 12x + 8

📝 Example: Factorise x³ − 27

Recognize: 27 = 3³. So x³ − 27 = x³ − 3³.

Use identity 7: a³ − b³ = (a − b)(a² + ab + b²) with a = x, b = 3:

= (x − 3)(x² + 3x + 9)

🧩 Factorisation — Reversing Identities

Factorising means writing a polynomial as a product of simpler factors. It's the OPPOSITE of expanding.

EXPANDING

You start with brackets and OPEN them.

(x + 3)(x − 2)
= x² − 2x + 3x − 6
= x² + x − 6

FACTORISING

You start with expanded form and ADD brackets.

x² + x − 6
= x² + 3x − 2x − 6
= (x + 3)(x − 2)

🎯 Splitting the Middle Term (Most Important Technique!)

To factorise a quadratic ax² + bx + c:

  1. Find two numbers that multiply to a·c and add to b.
  2. Split the middle term using these two numbers.
  3. Group and factor by common factors.

📝 Example: Factorise x² + 5x + 6

Here a = 1, b = 5, c = 6. Need two numbers that:

  • Multiply to a·c = 1 × 6 = 6
  • Add to b = 5

Try pairs: (1, 6) → sum 7. (2, 3) → sum 5. ✓ Use 2 and 3.

Split the middle: x² + 5x + 6 = x² + 2x + 3x + 6

Group: = x(x + 2) + 3(x + 2)

Take common factor: = (x + 2)(x + 3)

Verify: (x+2)(x+3) = x² + 3x + 2x + 6 = x² + 5x + 6 ✓

📝 Example: Factorise 2x² + 7x + 3

a·c = 2 × 3 = 6. Need numbers multiplying to 6 and adding to 7.

Try (1, 6) → sum 7 ✓

Split: 2x² + x + 6x + 3

Group: x(2x + 1) + 3(2x + 1)

= (2x + 1)(x + 3)

📝 Example with negatives: x² − 7x + 12

Need: multiply to 12, add to −7. Both must be NEGATIVE!

(−3, −4): product = 12, sum = −7 ✓

Split: x² − 3x − 4x + 12

Group: x(x − 3) − 4(x − 3)

= (x − 3)(x − 4)

💡 Sign Rules for Splitting

b signc signSigns of numbers
++Both positive
−+Both negative
+−Larger positive, smaller negative
−−Larger negative, smaller positive

🚨 Common Mistakes in Identities

  • ❌ Forgetting the middle term: (a+b)² ≠ a² + b²! Always add the 2ab.
  • ❌ Sign on 2ab: (a−b)² = a² − 2ab + b² (minus in middle, plus on ends)
  • ❌ Squaring coefficients: (2x)² = 4x² not 2x². Square BOTH the number AND variable.
  • ❌ Forgetting brackets: 2x² is NOT same as (2x)². The first = 2·x·x, the second = 4·x·x.
  • ❌ Wrong factorisation: x² + 4 ≠ (x+2)(x+2). Notice (x+2)² = x² + 4x + 4, not x²+4!
  • ❌ x² + y² has NO factorisation in real numbers. Don't try to factor it!
  • ❌ Missing factor: Always check by expanding back. If you don't get the original, you made an error.

🧪 Practice Problems — Identities & Factorisation

📝 Problem 1: Expand using identities

  1. (x + 7)²
  2. (2a − 5)²
  3. (3x + 4)(3x − 4)
  4. (98)² (mental)
  5. (105)² (mental)
Show answers
  1. x² + 14x + 49
  2. 4a² − 20a + 25
  3. 9x² − 16
  4. (100−2)² = 10000 − 400 + 4 = 9604
  5. (100+5)² = 10000 + 1000 + 25 = 11025

📝 Problem 2: Factorise

  1. x² − 25
  2. 49y² − 16
  3. x² + 5x + 6
  4. x² − 8x + 15
  5. x² + 2x − 8
Show answers
  1. (x − 5)(x + 5)
  2. (7y − 4)(7y + 4)
  3. (x + 2)(x + 3)
  4. (x − 3)(x − 5)
  5. (x + 4)(x − 2)

📝 Problem 3: Use a³ + b³ formula

Factorise 8x³ + 27

Show solution

Recognize 8x³ = (2x)³ and 27 = 3³.

Use a³ + b³ = (a + b)(a² − ab + b²) with a = 2x, b = 3:

= (2x + 3)(4x² − 6x + 9)

🔑 Complete Identity Cheat Sheet

# Identity Use For
1(a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b²Square of sum
2(a − b)² = a² − 2ab + b²Square of difference
3(a + b)(a − b) = a² − b²Difference of squares
4(a + b + c)² = a² + b² + c² + 2(ab + bc + ca)Square of 3 terms
5(a + b)³ = a³ + 3a²b + 3ab² + b³Cube of sum
6(a − b)³ = a³ − 3a²b + 3ab² − b³Cube of difference
7a³ + b³ = (a + b)(a² − ab + b²)Sum of cubes
8a³ − b³ = (a − b)(a² + ab + b²)Difference of cubes
9a³ + b³ + c³ − 3abc = (a + b + c)(a² + b² + c² − ab − bc − ca)Sum of cubes minus 3abc

🎯 Algebraic Identity Drills

I1. Expand (x + 5)² using identity.

▶ Solution

(a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b². Here a = x, b = 5.

= x² + 2(x)(5) + 25 = x² + 10x + 25

I2. Expand (2x − 3)²

▶ Solution

(a − b)² = a² − 2ab + b². a = 2x, b = 3.

= (2x)² − 2(2x)(3) + 9 = 4x² − 12x + 9

I3. Expand (3x + 4)(3x − 4)

▶ Solution

(a + b)(a − b) = a² − b². a = 3x, b = 4.

= 9x² − 16

I4. Use identities to compute 103²

▶ Solution

103² = (100 + 3)² = 10000 + 600 + 9 = 10609

I5. Compute 98² mentally

▶ Solution

98² = (100 − 2)² = 10000 − 400 + 4 = 9604

I6. Compute 105 × 95 using identity

▶ Solution

105 × 95 = (100+5)(100−5) = 10000 − 25 = 9975

I7. Factorise x² − 9

▶ Solution

Difference of squares: a² − b² = (a−b)(a+b). Here a = x, b = 3.

= (x − 3)(x + 3)

I8. Factorise x² + 7x + 12

▶ Solution

Find two numbers that multiply to 12 and add to 7: 3 and 4.

= (x + 3)(x + 4)

I9. Factorise x² − 5x + 6

▶ Solution

Two numbers multiplying to +6, adding to −5: both negative → −2 and −3.

= (x − 2)(x − 3)

I10. Factorise 4x² − 25y²

▶ Solution

(2x)² − (5y)² = (2x − 5y)(2x + 5y)

I11. Expand (x + y + z)²

▶ Solution

= x² + y² + z² + 2xy + 2yz + 2zx

I12. Expand (x + 2)³

▶ Solution

(a+b)³ = a³ + 3a²b + 3ab² + b³

= x³ + 3x²(2) + 3x(4) + 8 = x³ + 6x² + 12x + 8

I13. Expand (2a − 1)³

▶ Solution

(a−b)³ = a³ − 3a²b + 3ab² − b³. Here a = 2a, b = 1.

= 8a³ − 3(4a²)(1) + 3(2a)(1) − 1 = 8a³ − 12a² + 6a − 1

I14. Factorise x³ + 27 (sum of cubes)

▶ Solution

a³ + b³ = (a + b)(a² − ab + b²). a = x, b = 3.

= (x + 3)(x² − 3x + 9)

I15. Factorise 8x³ − 27 (difference of cubes)

▶ Solution

a³ − b³ = (a − b)(a² + ab + b²). a = 2x, b = 3.

= (2x − 3)(4x² + 6x + 9)

📚 The "Magic 7" Identities — Memorize These!

# Identity
1(a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b²
2(a − b)² = a² − 2ab + b²
3(a + b)(a − b) = a² − b²
4(x + a)(x + b) = x² + (a+b)x + ab
5(a + b + c)² = a² + b² + c² + 2ab + 2bc + 2ca
6(a + b)³ = a³ + 3a²b + 3ab² + b³
7(a − b)³ = a³ − 3a²b + 3ab² − b³
8a³ + b³ = (a + b)(a² − ab + b²)
9a³ − b³ = (a − b)(a² + ab + b²)
10a³ + b³ + c³ − 3abc = (a+b+c)(a²+b²+c²−ab−bc−ca)

🏆 Exam Tips for Identities

  • 🧠 Memorize all 10 identities — they appear in every exam!
  • 👀 To expand: just SUBSTITUTE values of a, b into the identity.
  • 🔍 To factorise: identify which identity matches the structure.
  • 💡 For mental math, look for numbers near 100 (use (100±x)² trick).
  • ⚠️ Don't forget signs! In (a−b)² the middle term is NEGATIVE (−2ab).
  • 📐 (a+b)³ has 4 terms: a³, 3a²b, 3ab², b³. The 3 in middle is crucial.
  • 🎯 In splitting middle term, find two numbers: product = c, sum = b.
  • 📊 Difference of squares (a²−b²) is the EASIEST to factorise — always look for it first.

🌟 Why Identities Matter (Real Use Cases)

  • ⚡ Fast mental math: Multiply 99 × 101 in seconds: (100−1)(100+1) = 10000−1 = 9999.
  • 🏗️ Engineering: Stress formulas often need expansions like (a+b)².
  • 📊 Statistics: Variance formula involves expanding (x−mean)².
  • 💻 Computer graphics: Distance² uses (x₂−x₁)² + (y₂−y₁)² — pure identities!
  • 🧮 Higher math: Calculus, algebra, even physics use these identities constantly.

📘 More Identity Examples (Mixed)

📝 Example 1: Using (x + a)(x + b) Identity

Q: Multiply (x + 7)(x + 4) using identity.

(x + a)(x + b) = x² + (a + b)x + ab

= x² + (7 + 4)x + (7)(4) = x² + 11x + 28

📝 Example 2: Mixed Signs

Q: (x − 5)(x + 8) = ?

Here a = −5, b = 8. Sum = 3, Product = −40.

= x² + 3x − 40

📝 Example 3: Factorise Trinomial

Q: Factorise x² + 8x + 15.

Find numbers multiplying to 15, adding to 8: 3 and 5.

= (x + 3)(x + 5)

📝 Example 4: Factorise Negative Sum

Q: Factorise x² − 7x + 12.

Both numbers negative (product +, sum −): −3 and −4.

= (x − 3)(x − 4)

📝 Example 5: Factorise Negative Product

Q: Factorise x² + 2x − 15.

One positive, one negative (product −): +5 and −3.

= (x + 5)(x − 3)

📝 Example 6: Use (a+b)² Identity for Algebra

Q: If a + b = 7 and ab = 10, find a² + b².

(a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b² → 49 = a² + 20 + b²

So a² + b² = 29

📝 Example 7: Use (a−b)² Trick

Q: If a + b = 9 and a² + b² = 41, find ab.

(a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b² → 81 = 41 + 2ab → 2ab = 40 → ab = 20

📝 Example 8: Factorise Cube

Q: Factorise x³ + 8

Sum of cubes: a³ + b³ = (a + b)(a² − ab + b²). Here a = x, b = 2.

= (x + 2)(x² − 2x + 4)

📝 Example 9: Factorise (Common factor + identity)

Q: Factorise 3x² − 27

Take out common 3: = 3(x² − 9) = 3(x − 3)(x + 3)

📝 Example 10: Identity for Three Variables

Q: Expand (a + 2b + 3c)²

= a² + (2b)² + (3c)² + 2(a)(2b) + 2(2b)(3c) + 2(3c)(a)

= a² + 4b² + 9c² + 4ab + 12bc + 6ac

📋 Chapter Summary at a Glance

  • 📐 Identity = equation TRUE for ALL values (e.g., (a+b)² = a² + 2ab + b²).
  • 📊 Equation = relation TRUE for SPECIFIC values (e.g., x + 3 = 7).
  • 🎯 Identities help us EXPAND quickly and FACTORISE smartly.
  • 💡 The "magic 10" identities cover squares, cubes, sum/difference forms.
  • ⚡ Mental math: use (100 ± n)² for fast squaring near 100.
  • 📝 To factorise x² + bx + c: find two numbers whose SUM = b and PRODUCT = c.
  • 🔍 Difference of squares (a² − b²) is the EASIEST identity to spot — always check first.
  • 📚 Algebra problems often turn into identity applications. Practice many examples!

🎲 Identity Spotting Game

Look at these expressions. Which identity matches?

Expression Identity to Use
(2x + 5)²(a + b)²
(3x − 2)²(a − b)²
(x − 7)(x + 7)(a − b)(a + b) = a² − b²
x² + 9x + 20x² + (a+b)x + ab
y³ + 64a³ + b³
8x³ − 125a³ − b³
(x + 2y + z)²(a + b + c)²

🧠 The "Square Identity" Visual Memory

Imagine a square with side (a+b). Its area = (a+b)². Now divide it into 4 parts:

You can SEE that the big square contains:

  • One a² square (top-left)
  • One b² square (bottom-right)
  • TWO equal rectangles of area "ab" (top-right + bottom-left) → that's the "2ab" middle term!
Exercise Sets — Questions & AnswersAll textbook exercises solved with detailed step-by-step explanations

📐 Key Identities — Know These by Heart!

  • (a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b²
  • (a − b)² = a² − 2ab + b²
  • a² − b² = (a + b)(a − b)
  • (a + b + c)² = a² + b² + c² + 2ab + 2bc + 2ca
  • (a + b)³ = a³ + 3a²b + 3ab² + b³ = a³ + b³ + 3ab(a+b)
  • (a − b)³ = a³ − 3a²b + 3ab² − b³ = a³ − b³ − 3ab(a−b)
  • a³ + b³ = (a + b)(a² − ab + b²)
  • a³ − b³ = (a − b)(a² + ab + b²)

⚠️ Common Mistakes to Avoid

  • (a+b)² ≠ a²+b²: You MUST include the middle term 2ab! This is the #1 mistake
  • Sign in (a−b)²: Middle term is −2ab but last term is +b² (minus × minus = plus)
  • Factoring x²+5x+6: Find two numbers that MULTIPLY to 6 and ADD to 5 (answer: 2 and 3)
  • Cube identity signs: In (a−b)³, signs alternate: +a³ −3a²b +3ab² −b³

💡 Strategy Tip for Identities

How to pick the right identity:

  • See (something)²? → Use (a±b)² expansion
  • See x² − y²? → Factor as (x+y)(x−y) immediately
  • See x² + 5x + 6? → Find two numbers that multiply to 6 and add to 5
  • Need to find 99²? → Write as (100−1)² and expand!

🔥 Warm-Up (Try these first!)

  1. Expand (x + 3)² [Ans: x² + 6x + 9]
  2. Expand (x − 4)² [Ans: x² − 8x + 16]
  3. Factor x² − 25 [Ans: (x+5)(x−5)]
  4. Find 101² using identity [Ans: (100+1)² = 10000 + 200 + 1 = 10201]

Exercise Set 4.1 — Expanding Using (a + b)²

Q1. Using the identity (a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b², expand the following:

(i) (7x + 4y)²   (ii) (7x/2 + 5y/3)²   (iii) (2.5p + 1.5q)²   (iv) (3s/4 + 8t/2)²   (v) (x + 1/y)²   (vi) (1/x + 1/y)²

Solution:

(i) (7x + 4y)² = (7x)² + 2(7x)(4y) + (4y)² = 49x² + 56xy + 16y²

(ii) (7x/2 + 5y/3)² = (7x/2)² + 2(7x/2)(5y/3) + (5y/3)² = 49x²/4 + 35xy/3 + 25y²/9

(iii) (2.5p + 1.5q)² = (2.5p)² + 2(2.5p)(1.5q) + (1.5q)² = 6.25p² + 7.5pq + 2.25q²

(iv) (3s/4 + 8t/2)² = (3s/4)² + 2(3s/4)(4t) + (4t)² = 9s²/16 + 6st + 16t²

(v) (x + 1/y)² = x² + 2(x)(1/y) + (1/y)² = x² + 2x/y + 1/y²

(vi) (1/x + 1/y)² = 1/x² + 2(1/x)(1/y) + 1/y² = 1/x² + 2/xy + 1/y²

Q2. Using the same identity, find the values of:

(i) (64)²   (ii) (105)²   (iii) (205)²

Solution:

(i) 64² = (60 + 4)² = 3600 + 480 + 16 = 4096

(ii) 105² = (100 + 5)² = 10000 + 1000 + 25 = 11025

(iii) 205² = (200 + 5)² = 40000 + 2000 + 25 = 42025

Exercise Set 4.2 — Factorisation Using (a ± b)²

Q1. Factor completely:

(i) 9x² + 24xy + 16y²   (ii) 4s² + 20st + 25t²   (iii) 49x² + 28xy + 4y²   (iv) (64/9)p² + (32/3)pq + 4q²   *(v) 3a² + 4√3 ab + 4b²   *(vi) 9s² + √5 sv + (5/6)v²

Solution:

(i) 9x² + 24xy + 16y² = (3x)² + 2(3x)(4y) + (4y)² = (3x + 4y)²

(ii) 4s² + 20st + 25t² = (2s)² + 2(2s)(5t) + (5t)² = (2s + 5t)²

(iii) 49x² + 28xy + 4y² = (7x)² + 2(7x)(2y) + (2y)² = (7x + 2y)²

(iv) (64/9)p² + (32/3)pq + 4q² = (8p/3)² + 2(8p/3)(2q) + (2q)² = (8p/3 + 2q)²

*(v) 3a² + 4√3 ab + 4b² — Take common factor if possible. Note: (√3 a)² + 2(√3 a)(2b) + (2b)² = 3a² + 4√3 ab + 4b² = (√3 a + 2b)²

*(vi) 9s² + √5 sv + (5/6)v² — This doesn't perfectly fit (a+b)² form. Hint from textbook suggests taking out a common factor similar to Example 7.

Q2. Find the values of the following using the identity (a − b)² = a² − 2ab + b²:

(i) (79)²   (ii) (193)²   (iii) (299)²

Solution:

(i) 79² = (80 − 1)² = 6400 − 160 + 1 = 6241

(ii) 193² = (200 − 7)² = 40000 − 2800 + 49 = 37249

(iii) 299² = (300 − 1)² = 90000 − 600 + 1 = 89401

Exercise Set 4.3 — Squares Using Multiple Identities

Q1. Find the following squares using one of the identities (a+b)², (a−b)², or (a+b+c)². Determine which identity makes each calculation easier:

(i) 117²   (ii) 78²   (iii) 198²   (iv) 214²   (v) 1104²   (vi) 1120²

Solution:

(i) 117² = (120 − 3)² = 14400 − 720 + 9 = 13689 [using (a−b)²]

(ii) 78² = (80 − 2)² = 6400 − 320 + 4 = 6084 [using (a−b)²]

(iii) 198² = (200 − 2)² = 40000 − 800 + 4 = 39204 [using (a−b)²]

(iv) 214² = (200 + 10 + 4)² = 40000 + 100 + 16 + 4000 + 80 + 1600 = 45796 [using (a+b+c)²]

(v) 1104² = (1100 + 4)² = 1210000 + 8800 + 16 = 1218816 [using (a+b)²]

(vi) 1120² = (1100 + 20)² = 1210000 + 44000 + 400 = 1254400 [using (a+b)²]

Q2. Factor using suitable identities:

(i) 16y² − 24y + 9   (ii) 9s⁴ + 6s²t² + t⁴   (iii) m² + n² + k² + 2mn + 2nk + 2mk   (iv) p² − p/2 + 1/16   (v) 9a² + 4b² + c² + 12ab − 6ac − 4bc

Solution:

(i) 16y² − 24y + 9 = (4y)² − 2(4y)(3) + 3² = (4y − 3)²

(ii) 9s⁴ + 6s²t² + t⁴ = (3s²)² + 2(3s²)(t²) + (t²)² = (3s² + t²)²

(iii) m² + n² + k² + 2mn + 2nk + 2mk = (m + n + k)² [using (a+b+c)² identity]

(iv) p² − p/2 + 1/16 = p² − 2(p)(1/4) + (1/4)² = (p − 1/4)²

(v) 9a² + 4b² + c² + 12ab − 6ac − 4bc = (3a)² + (2b)² + (−c)² + 2(3a)(2b) + 2(2b)(−c) + 2(3a)(−c) = (3a + 2b − c)²

Q3. Expand using (a + b + c)² = a² + b² + c² + 2ab + 2bc + 2ca:

(i) (p + 3q + 7r)²   (ii) (3x − 2y + 4z)²

Solution:

(i) (p + 3q + 7r)² = p² + 9q² + 49r² + 6pq + 42qr + 14pr = p² + 9q² + 49r² + 6pq + 42qr + 14rp

(ii) (3x − 2y + 4z)² = 9x² + 4y² + 16z² + 2(3x)(−2y) + 2(−2y)(4z) + 2(3x)(4z) = 9x² + 4y² + 16z² − 12xy − 16yz + 24xz

Q4. Is this an identity? (a+b−c)(a−b+c) + (−a+b+c)(a+b+c) = 2(a² + b² + c²) − 2(a² + b² + c²)?

Verify: (a+b−c)(a−b+c) + (−a+b+c)(a+b+c) = ?

Solution:

Let's expand each product:

(a+b−c)(a−b+c) = a² − ab + ac + ab − b² + bc − ac + bc − c² = a² − b² − c² + 2bc

(−a+b+c)(a+b+c) = −a² − ab − ac + ab + b² + bc + ac + bc + c² = −a² + b² + c² + 2bc

Sum = (a² − b² − c² + 2bc) + (−a² + b² + c² + 2bc) = 4bc

The identity from the textbook states: (a+b−c)(a−b+c) + (−a+b+c)(a+b+c) = 2(b² + c² + 2bc − a²). We can verify by substitution.

Exercise Set 4.4 — Factorisation of Quadratics & Products

Q1. Fill in the blanks to complete the following identities:

(i) s² − 11s + 24 = (________)(________)

(ii) (________)(x + 1) = 3x² − 4x − 7

(iii) 10x² − 11x − 6 = (2x − ___)(___x + 2)

(iv) 6x² + 7x + 2 = (____________)(___________)

Solution:

(i) s² − 11s + 24: Find a,b: a+b = −11, ab = 24 → a = −3, b = −8. (s − 3)(s − 8)

(ii) 3x² − 4x − 7 = 3x² − 7x + 3x − 7 = x(3x−7) + 1(3x−7) = (3x − 7)(x + 1)

(iii) 10x² − 11x − 6: product = 10×(−6) = −60, sum = −11 → −15 and 4. 10x² − 15x + 4x − 6 = 5x(2x−3) + 2(2x−3) = (2x − 3)(5x + 2)

(iv) 6x² + 7x + 2: product = 12, sum = 7 → 3 and 4. 6x² + 3x + 4x + 2 = 3x(2x+1) + 2(2x+1) = (3x + 2)(2x + 1)

Q2. Select and use the identity that will help you to find the following products without multiplying directly:

(i) (41)²   (ii) (27)²   (iii) 23 × 17   (iv) (135)²   (v) (97)²   (vi) 18 × 29   (vii) 34 × 43   (viii) (205)²

Solution:

(i) 41² = (40+1)² = 1600 + 80 + 1 = 1681

(ii) 27² = (30−3)² = 900 − 180 + 9 = 729

(iii) 23 × 17 = (20+3)(20−3) = 400 − 9 = 391

(iv) 135² = (130+5)² = 16900 + 1300 + 25 = 18225

(v) 97² = (100−3)² = 10000 − 600 + 9 = 9409

(vi) 18 × 29 — Śhrīdharāchārya method is tricky here. Note 18×29 ≈ 20² − something. Actually: (23.5+5.5)(23.5−5.5) = 23.5² − 5.5² = 552.25 − 30.25 = 522

(vii) 34 × 43 — Note: not an obvious identity pattern. Try: 34 × 43 = (38.5+4.5)(38.5−4.5) = 38.5² − 4.5² = 1482.25 − 20.25 = 1462

(viii) 205² = (200+5)² = 40000 + 2000 + 25 = 42025

Q3. Factor the following:

(i) 9a² + b² + 4c² − 6ab + 12ac − 4bc   (ii) 16s² + 25t² − 40st   (iii) r² − r − 42   (iv) 49g² + 14gh + h²   (v) 64u² + 121v² + 4w² − 176uv − 32uw + 44vw

Solution:

(i) 9a² + b² + 4c² − 6ab + 12ac − 4bc = (3a)² + (−b)² + (2c)² + 2(3a)(−b) + 2(3a)(2c) + 2(−b)(2c) = (3a − b + 2c)²

(ii) 16s² + 25t² − 40st = (4s)² − 2(4s)(5t) + (5t)² = (4s − 5t)²

(iii) r² − r − 42: Find a,b: a+b = −1, ab = −42 → a = −7, b = 6. (r − 7)(r + 6)

(iv) 49g² + 14gh + h² = (7g)² + 2(7g)(h) + h² = (7g + h)²

(v) 64u² + 121v² + 4w² − 176uv − 32uw + 44vw = (−8u)² + (11v)² + (2w)² + 2(−8u)(11v) + 2(−8u)(2w) + 2(11v)(2w) = (−8u + 11v + 2w)²

Exercise Set 4.5 — Simplifying Rational Expressions

Q1. Simplify the following rational expressions (assuming denominators ≠ 0):

(i) (33p² − 18q²) / (31p² + 10pq − 2q²)

(ii) (n³ − 5n²m + 10nm² − m³) / (m² − 2mn + 3n²)

(iii) (w³ − v³ + x³ + 3wvx) / (w² + v² + x² − wv − wx + vx)

(iv) (4y² − 20yz + 25z²) / (2y − 5z)²

(v) [(x² + 6x − 7)(x² − 12x + 9)] / [(x² − 6x + 9)(x − 1)]

(vi) (p⁴ − 16) / (p² − 4p + 4)

Solution:

(i) Numerator: 33p² − 18q² = 3(11p² − 6q²). Denominator: 31p² + 10pq − 2q². Factor denominator by finding a,b: need numbers multiplying to 31×(−2) = −62, adding to 10. This is a more complex factorisation requiring careful analysis of the specific expression.

(iv) 4y² − 20yz + 25z² = (2y − 5z)². So (2y − 5z)² / (2y − 5z)² = 1

(v) x² + 6x − 7 = (x+7)(x−1); x² − 12x + 9 stays; x² − 6x + 9 = (x−3)².

Result: [(x+7)(x−1) × (x² − 12x + 9)] / [(x−3)² × (x−1)] = (x+7)(x² − 12x + 9) / (x−3)²

(vi) p⁴ − 16 = (p²+4)(p²−4) = (p²+4)(p+2)(p−2). Denominator: (p−2)².

Result: (p²+4)(p+2) / (p−2)

🧠 Self-Check — Are You Ready for End-of-Chapter?

  1. Expand (x + 5)² without looking at formulas
  2. Factor x² − 49
  3. Find 98² using an identity

1. x² + 10x + 25 — Did you remember the middle term (2×x×5 = 10x)?

2. (x+7)(x−7) — difference of squares!

3. (100−2)² = 10000 − 400 + 4 = 9604

Exercise Set 4.end — End-of-Chapter Exercises

Q1. Use suitable identities to find the following products:

(i) (−3x + 4)²   (ii) (2s + 7)(2s − 7)   (iii) (p²/2 + 1/2)(p²/2 − 1/2)   (iv) (2n + 7)(2n − 7)   (v) (s − 2t)(s² + 2st + 4t²)   (vi) (1/r − 2/4)²   (vii) (−3m + 4k − l)²   (viii) (x − 1/y)³   (ix) (7k/2 − 2m/3)²

Solution:

(i) (−3x + 4)² = (4 − 3x)² = 16 − 24x + 9x² = 9x² − 24x + 16

(ii) (2s + 7)(2s − 7) = (2s)² − 7² = 4s² − 49

(iii) (p²/2 + 1/2)(p²/2 − 1/2) = (p²/2)² − (1/2)² = p⁴/4 − 1/4

(iv) (2n + 7)(2n − 7) = 4n² − 49 = 4n² − 49

(v) (s − 2t)(s² + 2st + 4t²) = s³ − (2t)³ = s³ − 8t³ [using a³−b³ = (a−b)(a²+ab+b²)]

(vi) (1/r − 2/4)² = (1/r − 1/2)² = 1/r² − 1/r + 1/4 = 1/r² − 1/r + 1/4

(vii) (−3m + 4k − l)² = 9m² + 16k² + l² − 24mk + 6ml − 8kl = 9m² + 16k² + l² − 24km + 6lm − 8kl

(viii) (x − 1/y)³ = x³ − 3x²(1/y) + 3x(1/y²) − 1/y³ = x³ − 3x²/y + 3x/y² − 1/y³

(ix) (7k/2 − 2m/3)² = 49k²/4 − 2(7k/2)(2m/3) + 4m²/9 = 49k²/4 − 14km/3 + 4m²/9

Q2. Find the values using suitable identities:

(i) 17 × 21   (ii) 104 × 96   (iii) 24 × 16   (iv) 147³   (v) 199³   (vi) 127³   (vii) (−107)³   (viii) (−299)³

Solution:

(i) 17 × 21 = (19−2)(19+2) = 19² − 4 = 361 − 4 = 357

(ii) 104 × 96 = (100+4)(100−4) = 10000 − 16 = 9984

(iii) 24 × 16 = (20+4)(20−4) = 400 − 16 = 384

(iv) 147³ = (150−3)³ = 150³ − 3(150²)(3) + 3(150)(9) − 27 = 3375000 − 202500 + 4050 − 27 = 3176523

(v) 199³ = (200−1)³ = 8000000 − 120000 + 600 − 1 = 7880599

(vi) 127³ = (130−3)³ = 2197000 − 152100 + 3510 − 27 = 2048383

(vii) (−107)³ = −107³ = −(110−3)³ = −(1331000 − 108900 + 2970 − 27) = −1225043

(viii) (−299)³ = −299³ = −(300−1)³ = −(27000000 − 270000 + 900 − 1) = −26730899

Q3. Factor the following algebraic expressions:

(i) 4y² + 1/(16y²) + 1   (ii) 9m²/2 − 1/(25n²)   (iii) 27b³ − 1/(64b³)   (iv) x² + 5x/6 + 1/6   (v) 27u³/125 − 9u²/25 − u/5 + 1   (vi) 64y³ + 1/(125z³)   (vii) p³ + q³ + 27r³ − 9pqr   (viii) 9m² − 12m + 4   (ix) 9x³ − 8y³ + 36xyz + z³   (x) 49x² + 36y² + 4z² + 12xy + 36yz + 2zx   (xi) 27u³/4 − 1/(216u³)

Solution:

(i) 4y² + 1/(16y²) + 1 = (2y)² + 2(2y)(1/4y) + (1/4y)² = (2y + 1/(4y))²

(ii) 9m²/2 − 1/(25n²) — This uses a²−b² = (a+b)(a−b) if it can be written as perfect squares. Actually = (3m/√2)² − (1/(5n))² = (3m/√2 + 1/(5n))(3m/√2 − 1/(5n))

(iii) 27b³ − 1/(64b³) = (3b)³ − (1/(4b))³ = (3b − 1/(4b))(9b² + 3/4 + 1/(16b²))

(iv) x² + 5x/6 + 1/6: Find a,b: a+b = 5/6, ab = 1/6 → a = 1/2, b = 1/3. (x + 1/2)(x + 1/3)

(vi) 64y³ + 1/(125z³) = (4y)³ + (1/(5z))³ = (4y + 1/(5z))(16y² − 4y/(5z) + 1/(25z²))

(vii) p³ + q³ + 27r³ − 9pqr = p³ + q³ + (3r)³ − 3(p)(q)(3r) = (p + q + 3r)(p² + q² + 9r² − pq − 3qr − 3pr)

(viii) 9m² − 12m + 4 = (3m)² − 2(3m)(2) + 2² = (3m − 2)²

Q4. Simplify the following (assuming denominators ≠ 0):

(i) (4x² + 4x + 1) / (4x − 1²)   (ii) (9a³ − 24b³) / (9a² − 6ab + 3b²)   (iii) (s³ + 125t³) / (s² − 2st − 3t² + 5st)

Solution:

(i) Numerator: 4x² + 4x + 1 = (2x+1)². Denominator: needs clarification from textbook. If 4x² − 1 = (2x+1)(2x−1): Result = (2x+1)/(2x−1)

(ii) 9a³ − 24b³ = 3(3a³ − 8b³). Now 3a³ − 8b³ is not a standard form. If intended as (9a)³ − (24b)³ this needs reinterpretation from the original text.

(iii) s³ + 125t³ = (s+5t)(s² − 5st + 25t²). Denominator: s² + 3st − 3t² (factoring). Result = (s+5t)(s² − 5st + 25t²) / (s² + 3st − 3t²)

Q5. Find possible expressions for the length and breadth of each rectangle whose areas are:

(i) 25a² − 30ab + 9b²   (ii) 36s² − 49t²

Solution:

(i) 25a² − 30ab + 9b² = (5a − 3b)². Length = 5a − 3b, Breadth = 5a − 3b (it's a square!)

(ii) 36s² − 49t² = (6s + 7t)(6s − 7t). Length = 6s + 7t, Breadth = 6s − 7t

Q6. Find possible expressions for the length, breadth, and height of cuboids whose volumes are:

(i) 6a² − 24b²   (ii) 3ps² − 15ps + 12p

Solution:

(i) 6a² − 24b² = 6(a² − 4b²) = 6(a + 2b)(a − 2b). Dimensions: 6, (a + 2b), (a − 2b)

(ii) 3ps² − 15ps + 12p = 3p(s² − 5s + 4) = 3p(s − 4)(s − 1). Dimensions: 3p, (s − 4), (s − 1)

Q7. The village playground is shaped as a square of side 40 metres. A path of width s metres is created around the playground for people to walk. Find an expression for the area of the path in terms of s.

Solution:

Outer square side = 40 + 2s metres.

Outer area = (40 + 2s)² = 1600 + 160s + 4s²

Inner area (playground) = 40² = 1600

Area of path = (40 + 2s)² − 40² = 160s + 4s² = 4s(40 + s) sq. metres

Q8. If a number plus its reciprocal equals 10/3, find the number.

Solution:

Let the number be x. Then x + 1/x = 10/3.

Multiply by x: x² + 1 = 10x/3 → 3x² + 3 = 10x → 3x² − 10x + 3 = 0.

Factor: 3x² − 9x − x + 3 = 3x(x−3) − 1(x−3) = (3x−1)(x−3) = 0.

x = 3 or x = 1/3. The number is 3 (or equivalently 1/3).

Q9. A rectangular pool has area 2x² + 7x + 3 square hastas. If its width is 2x + 1 hastas, find its length.

Solution:

Factor the area: 2x² + 7x + 3 = 2x² + 6x + x + 3 = 2x(x+3) + 1(x+3) = (2x+1)(x+3).

Length = Area / Width = (2x+1)(x+3) / (2x+1) = x + 3 hastas

Q10. ⭐ If both x − 2 and x − 1/2 are factors of px² + 5x + r, show that p = r.

Solution:

Since x = 2 is a root: p(4) + 5(2) + r = 0 → 4p + 10 + r = 0 → 4p + r = −10 ... (1)

Since x = 1/2 is a root: p(1/4) + 5(1/2) + r = 0 → p/4 + 5/2 + r = 0 → p + 10 + 4r = 0 → p + 4r = −10 ... (2)

From (1): 4p + r = −10. From (2): p + 4r = −10.

So 4p + r = p + 4r → 3p = 3r → p = r. ∎

Q11. ⭐ If a + b + c = 5 and ab + bc + ca = 10, then prove that a³ + b³ + c³ − 3abc = −25.

Solution:

Using identity: a³ + b³ + c³ − 3abc = (a + b + c)(a² + b² + c² − ab − bc − ca)

We know a + b + c = 5 and ab + bc + ca = 10.

Find a² + b² + c²: (a+b+c)² = a² + b² + c² + 2(ab+bc+ca) → 25 = a² + b² + c² + 20 → a² + b² + c² = 5.

So: a³ + b³ + c³ − 3abc = 5 × (5 − 10) = 5 × (−5) = −25. ∎

Q12. ⭐ By factoring the expression, check that n³ − n is always divisible by 6 for all natural numbers n.

Solution:

n³ − n = n(n² − 1) = n(n−1)(n+1) = (n−1) · n · (n+1)

This is the product of THREE consecutive integers.

Among any 3 consecutive integers: at least one is divisible by 2, and at least one is divisible by 3.

∴ Product is divisible by 2 × 3 = 6 for all natural numbers n. ∎

Q13. ⭐ Find the value of:

(i) x³ + y³ − 12xy + 64, when x + y = −4

(ii) x³ − 8y³ − 36xy − 216, when x = 2y + 6

Solution:

(i) If x + y = −4, then x + y + 4 = 0. Using identity: if a+b+c = 0, then a³+b³+c³ = 3abc.

Let a=x, b=y, c=4. Then x³ + y³ + 64 = 3(x)(y)(4) = 12xy.

∴ x³ + y³ + 64 − 12xy = 0 → x³ + y³ − 12xy + 64 = 0

(ii) x = 2y + 6 → x − 2y − 6 = 0. Let a=x, b=−2y, c=−6. Then a+b+c = 0.

a³+b³+c³ = 3abc → x³ + (−2y)³ + (−6)³ = 3(x)(−2y)(−6)

x³ − 8y³ − 216 = 36xy

∴ x³ − 8y³ − 36xy − 216 = 0

📋 Chapter 5 — What's Inside

  • 5.3 How Many Circles?
  • 5.4 Chords and Angles They Subtend

  • 5.6 Distance of Chords from the Centre
  • 5.7 Angles Subtended by an Arc
  • 5.8 Concyclicity of Points
