  • End-of-Chapter Exercises

Chapter 5: I'm Up and Down, and Round and Round (Circles)

Topics Explained — Chapter 5Each topic explained in simple, understandable English

§5.1 — What is a Circle? (Basic Definitions)

A circle is the set of ALL points that are at the SAME distance from a fixed centre point. That fixed distance is called the radius.

Key parts of a circle:

  • Centre (O): The fixed point in the middle
  • Radius (r): Distance from centre to any point on the circle
  • Diameter (d): A line through the centre connecting two points on circle. d = 2r. It's the LONGEST chord possible.
  • Chord: Any line segment joining two points on the circle
  • Arc: A curved piece of the circle (like a section of the boundary)
  • Sector: A "pizza slice" — the region between two radii and an arc
  • Segment: Region between a chord and the arc it cuts off

Think of it this way: Tie a string to a nail (that's the centre). Hold a pencil at the other end and draw — you get a perfect circle! Every point is the same distance (string length = radius) from the nail.

§5.2 — Circle Symmetry (Perfectly Balanced)

A circle is the MOST symmetrical shape possible!

  • Infinite lines of symmetry: Every diameter divides the circle into two equal halves. Since you can draw infinite diameters, there are infinite lines of symmetry!
  • Rotational symmetry: Rotate a circle by ANY angle around its centre — it looks exactly the same.

Compare: A square has only 4 lines of symmetry. A regular hexagon has 6. But a circle has infinite! That's what makes it so special in mathematics.

§5.3 — How Many Circles Can Pass Through Given Points?

Through 1 point: Infinite circles (any size, any centre — just make sure it touches that point)

Through 2 points: Still infinite circles (think of it — many circles of different sizes can pass through the same two points)

Through 3 non-collinear points: Exactly ONE circle! This is called the circumcircle of the triangle formed by those 3 points.

Why? Three points that don't lie on a straight line uniquely determine a circle. This is because the centre must be equidistant from all three points — and that gives only one possible centre.

Note: If the 3 points ARE collinear (on the same line), no circle can pass through all of them.

§5.4 — Chords and the Angles They Make

Key theorem: Equal chords make equal angles at the centre.

What does "subtend" mean? When you draw lines from the ends of a chord to the centre, the angle formed at the centre is "subtended" by that chord. Think of it as the "spread" the chord creates when viewed from the centre.

Important results:

  • Longer chord → bigger angle at centre
  • Equal chords → equal angles at centre (and vice versa)
  • The longest chord (diameter) subtends 180° at centre (it's a straight line through centre)

The BIG theorem (most important in circles): The angle subtended by a chord at the CENTRE is exactly TWICE the angle subtended at any point on the circle (on the major arc side).

§5.5 — Perpendicular from Centre Bisects the Chord

Theorem: If you drop a perpendicular from the centre to ANY chord, it will cut the chord exactly in HALF (bisect it).

Visual: Draw a chord. Draw a line from centre O perpendicular (90°) to the chord. That line hits the chord at its midpoint!

The reverse is also true: If you draw the perpendicular bisector of any chord (a line that cuts the chord at 90° at its midpoint), that line will always pass through the centre.

Useful application: If you have a broken circular plate and only a piece remains, you can find the original centre! Just draw two chords on the remaining piece, find their perpendicular bisectors — where they cross is the centre!

§5.6 — Chord Distance from Centre

Theorem 1: Equal chords are at EQUAL distances from the centre.

Theorem 2: Of two unequal chords, the LONGER chord is CLOSER to the centre.

How to find the distance: The "distance of a chord from the centre" means the perpendicular distance — draw a perpendicular from O to the chord, measure that length.

Calculation trick: If chord = 2l and radius = r, then distance from centre d = √(r² − l²). This is just Pythagoras! (The perpendicular, half-chord, and radius form a right triangle.)

Example: Chord = 8 cm, radius = 5 cm. Half-chord = 4 cm. Distance = √(25−16) = √9 = 3 cm.

§5.7 — Circle Angle Theorems (Must Memorize!)

These are the MOST asked theorems in exams:

1. Angle at centre = 2 × angle at circumference (for the same arc)

  • If an arc makes 80° at the centre, it makes 40° at any point on the remaining circle

2. Angle in a semicircle = 90°

  • If AB is a diameter and C is any point on the circle, then ∠ACB = 90° ALWAYS!
  • This is because the diameter subtends 180° at centre, so 180°/2 = 90° at circumference.

3. Angles in the same segment are equal

  • If two angles are made by the same arc at two different points on the circle, they're equal
  • Example: If ∠ADB and ∠ACB are both in the major arc, and look at the same chord AB, then ∠ADB = ∠ACB

§5.8 — Cyclic Quadrilaterals (4 Points on One Circle)

Concyclic points = points that all lie on the same circle.

Cyclic quadrilateral = a quadrilateral whose all 4 vertices lie on a circle.

THE key property: In a cyclic quadrilateral, opposite angles add up to 180°.

  • ∠A + ∠C = 180° and ∠B + ∠D = 180°

The converse is also true: If a quadrilateral has opposite angles summing to 180°, then it IS cyclic (you can draw a circle through all 4 vertices).

Examples:

  • Every rectangle is a cyclic quadrilateral (all angles 90°, and 90°+90°=180° ✓)
  • A parallelogram that is NOT a rectangle can never be cyclic (opposite angles are equal but don't sum to 180° unless each is 90°)

⭕ The Anatomy of a Circle (Parts You Must Know)

A circle is a set of all points that are at the same distance from a fixed point. That distance is the radius, and the fixed point is the centre.

Circumference: distance around C = 2πr = πd Area: space inside A = πr² Sector: pie slice (centre + arc)

📏 The Most Important Circle Theorems (You'll Use These Every Time)

🌟 Theorem 1: Equal chords are equidistant from the centre

If two chords AB and CD of a circle are equal in length, then their perpendicular distances from the centre are equal.

And conversely: chords equidistant from the centre have equal lengths.

🌟 Theorem 2: Perpendicular from centre bisects the chord

If you drop a perpendicular line from the centre O to any chord AB, the perpendicular cuts the chord exactly in half (i.e., AM = MB where M is the foot of perpendicular).

Conversely: the line from centre to the midpoint of a chord is perpendicular to that chord.

🌟 Theorem 3: Equal chords subtend equal angles at the centre

If chord AB = chord CD, then ∠AOB = ∠COD (angles at centre).

And vice versa: equal central angles → equal chords.

🌟 Theorem 4: Angle at centre = 2 × angle at circumference

The angle subtended by an arc at the centre is TWICE the angle subtended by the same arc at any point on the circumference (in the major arc region).

Symbolically: ∠AOB = 2 × ∠APB (where P is on the major arc)

🌟 Theorem 5: Angles in the same segment are equal

If two points P and Q are on the same side of a chord AB, then ∠APB = ∠AQB. (Both angles look at the same chord from the same side.)

🌟 Theorem 6: Angle in a semicircle = 90°

If you draw any triangle with one side being the diameter, the angle opposite the diameter is always 90° (right angle)!

This is a SPECIAL CASE of Theorem 4: angle at centre (180° = straight line for diameter) ÷ 2 = 90°.

🌟 Theorem 7: Opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral sum to 180°

A cyclic quadrilateral is a 4-sided figure where all 4 corners lie on one circle. If ABCD is cyclic, then:

∠A + ∠C = 180°    AND    ∠B + ∠D = 180°

📐 Chord Length Formula (Useful Pythagoras Trick)

Given a circle with radius r, if a chord is at perpendicular distance d from the centre, then:

Chord length = 2 × √(r² − d²)

📝 Worked Example: Find chord length

Given: Circle of radius 10 cm, chord at distance 6 cm from centre.

Chord length = 2√(r² − d²) = 2√(100 − 36) = 2√64 = 2(8) = 16 cm

📝 Reverse: Find distance from centre

Given: Circle of radius 13 cm, chord of length 10 cm. Find the distance from centre.

Half chord = 5. Then by Pythagoras: 13² = d² + 5² → 169 = d² + 25 → d² = 144 → d = 12 cm

🎡 Angles in a Circle (The Most Tested Topic!)

📝 Example: Find x

If the angle at the centre is 80°, what is the angle at the circumference (on the same side)?

By Theorem 4: ∠at centre = 2 × ∠at circumference

80° = 2x → x = 40°

📝 Example: Angle in semicircle

If AB is a diameter and P is any point on the circle (not A or B), what is ∠APB?

The arc from A to B is a semicircle, so the angle at centre = 180°.

Angle at circumference = 180° ÷ 2 = 90°

💡 This is HUGE: every triangle inscribed in a semicircle with one side = diameter is a RIGHT TRIANGLE!

🔷 Cyclic Quadrilaterals — When 4 Points Lie on One Circle

A quadrilateral whose all 4 vertices lie on a single circle is called cyclic.

📝 Example: Find angles in cyclic quad

In cyclic quadrilateral ABCD, ∠A = 70° and ∠B = 110°. Find ∠C and ∠D.

  • ∠A + ∠C = 180° → ∠C = 180° − 70° = 110°
  • ∠B + ∠D = 180° → ∠D = 180° − 110° = 70°

🚨 Common Mistakes with Circles

  • ❌ Mixing radius and diameter: d = 2r. If radius = 5, diameter = 10 (not 5/2!)
  • ❌ Wrong angle theorem: Don't confuse "angle at centre" with "angle at circumference" — they have a 2:1 ratio.
  • ❌ Forgetting cyclic quad rule: Opposite angles add to 180°, NOT adjacent ones.
  • ❌ Pythagoras setup: When using chord theorem, half the chord is one side, not the full chord!
  • ❌ Confusing chord and arc: A chord is STRAIGHT, an arc is CURVED. Both connect 2 points on circle.
  • ❌ Sector vs segment: Sector = pie slice (includes centre). Segment = region between chord and arc.
  • ❌ Assuming square inscribed = inscribed circle: When a square is inscribed in a circle, the DIAGONAL of square = diameter of circle.

🧪 Practice — Circle Theorems

📝 Problem 1: Chord problems

A chord of length 24 cm is at distance 5 cm from the centre of a circle. Find the radius.

Show solution

Half-chord = 12 cm, distance d = 5 cm.

r² = d² + (half-chord)² = 25 + 144 = 169 → r = 13 cm

📝 Problem 2: Angle theorem

In a circle, ∠at centre = 100°. Find ∠at circumference (on major arc).

Show answer

100° ÷ 2 = 50°

📝 Problem 3: Cyclic quadrilateral

ABCD is cyclic. If ∠A = 85° and ∠D = 95°, find ∠B and ∠C.

Show solution

∠C = 180° − ∠A = 95°.   ∠B = 180° − ∠D = 85°

📝 Problem 4: Angle in semicircle

AB is the diameter of a circle. Point C is on the circle. If ∠ABC = 35°, find ∠BAC.

Show solution

Since AB is diameter, ∠ACB = 90° (angle in semicircle).

Sum of angles in triangle = 180°: ∠ABC + ∠BAC + ∠ACB = 180°

35° + ∠BAC + 90° = 180° → ∠BAC = 55°

🔑 Circles Cheat Sheet

Concept Formula / Property
Diameterd = 2r
CircumferenceC = 2πr = πd
AreaA = πr²
Chord lengthL = 2√(r² − d²)
Equal chordsEquidistant from centre
⊥ from centreBisects the chord
Angle at centre= 2 × angle at circumference
Angle in semicircle= 90°
Cyclic quad opposite anglesSum to 180°
Angles in same segmentAre equal

🎯 Circle Theorem Drills

C1. The radius of a circle is 5 cm. Find the diameter and circumference. (Use π = 22/7)

▶ Solution

Diameter = 2 × 5 = 10 cm

Circumference = 2πr = 2 × (22/7) × 5 = 220/7 ≈ 31.43 cm

C2. A chord is 12 cm long. Distance from centre to chord is 8 cm. Find the radius.

▶ Solution

Half-chord = 6. Use Pythagoras: r² = 6² + 8² = 36 + 64 = 100. r = 10 cm

C3. If the radius is 13 cm and chord is 10 cm, find perpendicular distance from centre.

▶ Solution

Half-chord = 5. Pythagoras: d² + 5² = 13² → d² = 169 − 25 = 144. d = 12 cm

C4. The angle subtended by an arc at the centre is 80°. What angle does it subtend at a point on the major arc?

▶ Solution

Angle at centre = 2 × angle at circumference. So angle at circle = 80/2 = 40°

C5. In a cyclic quadrilateral, three angles are 70°, 110°, and 95°. Find the fourth angle.

▶ Solution

Opposite angles sum to 180°. The pair (70°, ?) → ? = 110°. The other pair (110°, ?) → ? = 70°.

So angles in order are 70°, 110°, 95°, 85°. Fourth angle = 85° (because 95 + 85 = 180).

C6. A diameter subtends what angle at any point on the circle?

▶ Solution

Angle in semicircle is always 90° (right angle).

C7. Two chords of a circle are equal. What can you say about their distances from the centre?

▶ Solution

Equal chords are equidistant from the centre (and vice versa).

C8. The area of a circle is 154 cm². Find the radius. (Use π = 22/7)

▶ Solution

πr² = 154 → (22/7)r² = 154 → r² = 154 × 7/22 = 49. r = 7 cm

C9. Find the area of a sector with radius 14 cm and central angle 90°.

▶ Solution

A = (θ/360) × πr² = (90/360) × (22/7) × 196 = (1/4) × 22 × 28 = 154 cm²

C10. Two equal circles touch each other externally. Distance between their centres is 10 cm. Find their radii.

▶ Solution

When two circles touch externally, distance between centres = sum of radii.

r + r = 10 → r = 5 cm each

📚 Complete Circle Theorem Bank

  1. The perpendicular from the centre to a chord bisects the chord.
  2. The line joining the centre to the midpoint of a chord is perpendicular to the chord.
  3. Equal chords of a circle are equidistant from the centre.
  4. Chords equidistant from the centre are equal in length.
  5. The angle subtended by an arc at the centre is double the angle at any point on the remaining circle.
  6. Angles in the same segment are equal.
  7. Angle in a semicircle is a right angle (90°).
  8. Sum of opposite angles in a cyclic quadrilateral = 180°.
  9. If one side of a cyclic quad is extended, the exterior angle = interior opposite angle.
  10. Equal arcs subtend equal angles at the centre.

🏆 Exam Tips for Circles

  • 📐 Always draw a clean diagram — label centre O, radius, chord, arc.
  • 🔢 Use Pythagoras for chord-radius problems (perpendicular from centre bisects chord).
  • 💡 Remember: Angle at centre = 2 × angle on remaining circumference.
  • 🎯 Cyclic quad: Opposite angles sum to 180°. Use this to find missing angles.
  • 📊 Diameter subtends 90° on the circle — useful identifier!
  • ⚠️ For arc length / sector area, angle MUST be in degrees (or convert from radians).
  • 📝 If 2 circles touch externally, d = r₁ + r₂. If internally, d = |r₁ − r₂|.

🌍 Circles Everywhere in Life

🚗 Wheels

Car tires, bicycle wheels — circles let things roll smoothly because every point on rim is same distance from centre.

🍕 Pizza slicing

Slicing a pizza creates sectors. Each slice = (1/8) × πr² area for 8 equal slices!

🌀 Cyclones / Galaxies

Spiral shapes and circular orbits use circle geometry. Planets travel in nearly circular paths.

📡 Radar / GPS

Radar covers a circular area. GPS uses intersecting circles to find your position.

📘 More Circle Worked Examples

📝 Example 1: Chord-Distance Calculation

Q: A chord AB is 24 cm long in a circle of radius 13 cm. Find the distance from centre O to AB.

Drop perpendicular from O to AB, meeting at M. Then AM = 12 (half of chord).

By Pythagoras in △OMA: OM² + AM² = OA² → OM² = 169 − 144 = 25 → OM = 5 cm

📝 Example 2: Equal Chords

Q: Two equal chords are 6 cm from centre. If chord = 16 cm, find radius.

Half-chord = 8, distance = 6. r² = 8² + 6² = 100 → r = 10 cm

📝 Example 3: Angle at Centre Theorem

Q: An arc subtends 110° at the centre. Find the angle it subtends at any point on the major arc.

Angle on circle = ½ × angle at centre = ½ × 110° = 55°

📝 Example 4: Cyclic Quadrilateral

Q: ABCD is cyclic. ∠A = 75°. Find ∠C.

Opposite angles sum to 180°. ∠C = 180° − 75° = 105°

📝 Example 5: Angle in Semicircle

Q: A triangle is inscribed in a circle with one side as diameter. Two other sides are 6 cm and 8 cm. Find diameter.

Angle in semicircle = 90°, so triangle is right-angled at vertex opposite diameter.

Diameter = √(6² + 8²) = 10 cm

📝 Example 6: Sector Area Computation

Q: Find the length of the minute hand of a clock if it sweeps an area of 154 cm² in 15 minutes.

In 15 minutes, minute hand turns 90°. So sector area = (90/360) × πr² = πr²/4.

πr²/4 = 154 → πr² = 616 → r² = 616 × 7/22 = 196 → r = 14 cm

📝 Example 7: Same Segment Angles

Q: Two angles subtended by a chord on the same side of it are 30° and ___?

Angles in the SAME segment are EQUAL → other angle is also 30°.

📋 Chapter Summary at a Glance

  • ⭕ A circle is the set of all points at fixed distance (radius) from a fixed point (centre).
  • 📏 Diameter = longest chord = 2 × radius.
  • 🎯 Chord: line joining 2 points on circle; arc: part of circumference.
  • 📐 Perpendicular from centre to chord BISECTS it.
  • 🔢 Equal chords are equidistant from centre (and vice versa).
  • 💡 Angle at centre = 2 × angle on circumference (same arc).
  • 📊 Angle in semicircle is always 90°.
  • 📝 Opposite angles in cyclic quadrilateral sum to 180°.
  • ♾️ For circle: C = 2πr, Area = πr², Sector area = (θ/360°) × πr², Arc length = (θ/360°) × 2πr.

🎯 Tangent to a Circle (Bonus Concept)

A tangent is a line that touches a circle at exactly ONE point (called the point of tangency).

📐 Key Tangent Facts

  • 📏 The tangent is perpendicular to the radius at the point of tangency.
  • 🔢 From an external point, you can draw exactly 2 tangents to a circle.
  • 💡 The 2 tangents from an external point are EQUAL in length.
  • 🎯 Tangent ⊥ Radius — this is the most important tangent property!

📊 Concentric Circles

Concentric circles share the same centre but have different radii (like a target or dartboard).

📝 Example

Two concentric circles have radii 7 cm and 10 cm. Area of the ring (annulus) between them?

Area = π(R² − r²) = (22/7)(100 − 49) = (22/7)(51) ≈ 160.29 cm²

Exercise Sets — Questions & AnswersAll textbook exercises solved with detailed step-by-step explanations

📝 Quick Recap — Circles Key Theorems

  • Equal chords = Equal distance from centre (and vice versa)
  • ⊥ from centre to chord bisects the chord (most used theorem!)
  • Angle at centre = 2 × angle at circumference (same arc)
  • Angles in same segment are equal
  • Angle in semicircle = 90°
  • Cyclic quadrilateral: Opposite angles add to 180°
  • Chord length: l = 2√(r² − d²) where d = distance from centre

⚠️ Common Mistakes to Avoid

  • Radius vs Diameter: Diameter = 2r. If given diameter, HALVE it before using formulas!
  • Perpendicular bisector: ⊥ from centre bisects chord, but a random ⊥ to chord may NOT pass through centre
  • "Angle in semicircle": Only works when the angle is on the circumference and the other side is a diameter
  • Cyclic quadrilateral: OPPOSITE angles sum to 180° (not adjacent!)
  • Drawing errors: Always mark the centre, draw radius to chord endpoint, and label the right angle

🔥 Warm-Up (Try these first!)

  1. A chord is 10 cm from the centre of a circle with radius 13 cm. Find the chord length. [Ans: 2√(169−100) = 2√69 ≈ 16.6 cm... Wait! Actually √(169−100) = √69. Half-chord = √69. Chord = 2√69]
  2. In a cyclic quadrilateral, one angle is 75°. What is the opposite angle? [Ans: 180° − 75° = 105°]
  3. A diameter subtends what angle at any point on the circle? [Ans: 90°]

Exercise Set 5.1 — Circumcircles & Constructions

Q1. Draw △ABC with AB = 5 cm, ∠A = 70° and ∠B = 60°. Draw the circumcircle of △ABC. Is the centre inside or outside the triangle?

Solution:

∠C = 180° − 70° − 60° = 50°. All angles are acute, so the triangle is acute-angled.

For acute triangles, the circumcentre lies inside the triangle.

Construction: Draw △ABC with given measurements. Draw perpendicular bisectors of any two sides. Their intersection is the circumcentre O. Draw circle with centre O and radius OA.

Q2. Draw △ABC with AB = 5 cm, ∠A = 100°, AC = 4 cm. Draw the circumcircle of △ABC. Is the centre inside or outside the triangle?

Solution:

Since ∠A = 100° > 90°, the triangle is obtuse-angled.

For obtuse triangles, the circumcentre lies outside the triangle (on the side of the longest side, opposite the obtuse angle).

Q3. Draw △ABC with AB = 6 cm, BC = 7 cm and CA = 7 cm. Draw the circumcircle. Let the circumcentre be O. Measure OA, OB, OC.

Solution:

Since it's isosceles (BC = CA = 7), the perpendicular bisector of AB will pass through C and through O.

After construction: OA = OB = OC (all equal to circumradius, by definition). This verifies that all three points are equidistant from O.

Q4. What is the least possible radius of a circle through two points A and B?

Solution:

The smallest circle through A and B has AB as its diameter.

So the least possible radius = AB/2 (half the distance between the two points).

Exercise Set 5.2 — Chords & Angles at Centre

Q1. Show that the triangle formed by a chord and the centre of the circle is isosceles.

Solution:

Let AB be a chord of a circle with centre C. Join CA and CB.

CA = CB = radius of the circle.

Since two sides of △CAB are equal (CA = CB), the triangle is isosceles. ∎

Q2. Show that if two such isosceles triangles (from Q1) have equal base length, they are congruent to each other.

Solution:

Let △CAB and △CDE be two triangles formed by chords AB and DE with centre C.

Given: AB = DE (equal base length).

CA = CB = CD = CE = r (all radii).

By SSS congruence: CA = CD, CB = CE, AB = DE → △CAB ≅ △CDE. ∎

Exercise Set 5.3 — Perpendicular from Centre to Chord

Q1. Explain why the converse to Theorem 4 is true: the perpendicular from the centre of a circle to a chord bisects the chord. (Hint: Use the figure. You are told ∠CMA = ∠CMB = 90°. Show AM = BM.)

Solution:

Given: CM ⊥ AB, so ∠CMA = ∠CMB = 90°.

In right triangles △CMA and △CMB:

• CA = CB (radii)

• CM = CM (common side)

• ∠CMA = ∠CMB = 90°

By RHS congruence: △CMA ≅ △CMB.

∴ AM = BM. The perpendicular from centre bisects the chord. ∎

Q2. An isosceles triangle ABC is inscribed in a circle, with AB = AC. Show that the altitude from A to BC passes through the centre of the circle.

Solution:

Let M be the foot of the altitude from A to BC. Since △ABC is isosceles with AB = AC:

• The altitude from A to BC bisects BC, so BM = MC.

• AM ⊥ BC (altitude).

So AM is the perpendicular bisector of chord BC. By Theorem 5, the perpendicular bisector of any chord passes through the centre. ∴ AM passes through the centre O. ∎

Q3. Two parallel chords of lengths 6 cm and 8 cm are on opposite sides of the centre of a circle. If the radius is 5 cm, find the distance between the midpoints of the chords.

Solution:

For chord of 6 cm: half = 3 cm. Distance from centre: d₁² + 3² = 5² → d₁² = 16 → d₁ = 4 cm.

For chord of 8 cm: half = 4 cm. Distance from centre: d₂² + 4² = 5² → d₂² = 9 → d₂ = 3 cm.

Since chords are on opposite sides, the distance between midpoints = d₁ + d₂ = 4 + 3 = 7 cm.

Exercise Set 5.4 — Equal Chords & Distance from Centre

Q1. Use the Baudhāyana–Pythagoras theorem to show why Theorem 6 (equal chords are equidistant from centre) must be true.

Solution:

Let AB and FG be equal chords with midpoints E and H. CE ⊥ AB and CH ⊥ FG.

By Pythagoras in △CEA: CE² + AE² = CA² = r².

By Pythagoras in △CHF: CH² + FH² = CF² = r².

Since AB = FG and E, H are midpoints: AE = FH (half of equal chords).

∴ CE² = r² − AE² = r² − FH² = CH². Hence CE = CH. ∎

Q2. If CE is perpendicular to AB, CH is perpendicular to GF, and CE = CH, show that AB = GF.

Solution:

In right △CEA: AE² = CA² − CE² = r² − CE².

In right △CHF: FH² = CF² − CH² = r² − CH².

Given CE = CH: AE² = FH² → AE = FH.

Since E and H are midpoints: AB = 2AE = 2FH = GF. ∴ AB = GF. ∎

Q3. Solve Q2 using the Baudhāyana–Pythagoras theorem.

Solution:

This is essentially the same approach as Q2. In right triangles △CEA and △CHF:

CA = CF = r (radii), CE = CH (given), ∠CEA = ∠CHF = 90°.

By RHS congruence: △CEA ≅ △CHF → EA = FH → AB = 2EA = 2FH = GF. ∎

Exercise Set 5.5 — Chord Length & Distance

Q1. Find the length of the chord of a circle where the radius is 7 cm and the perpendicular distance from the centre is 6 cm.

Solution:

Half-chord² = r² − d² = 7² − 6² = 49 − 36 = 13.

Half-chord = √13 cm.

Chord length = 2√13 ≈ 2√13 cm ≈ 7.21 cm

Q2. Explain why: If the perpendicular distance of a chord from the centre is d and the radius is r, then the chord length is 2√(r² − d²).

Solution:

The perpendicular from centre to chord bisects the chord (Theorem 5).

Let M be the midpoint of chord AB, O the centre. Then OM ⊥ AB and OM = d.

In right △OMA: OA² = OM² + MA² (Pythagoras)

r² = d² + MA² → MA = √(r² − d²)

Chord AB = 2 × MA = 2√(r² − d²). ∎

Q3. ⭐ In a circle, if the distance of chord AB from the centre is twice the distance of another chord CD from the centre, can we conclude that CD = 2AB? Give reasons.

Solution:

No, we cannot. Let radius = r.

Let distance of CD from centre = d, then distance of AB from centre = 2d.

AB = 2√(r² − 4d²) and CD = 2√(r² − d²).

For CD = 2AB: 2√(r² − d²) = 4√(r² − 4d²) → r² − d² = 4(r² − 4d²) = 4r² − 16d²

→ 15d² = 3r² → d = r/√5. This only works for specific values, not in general.

The relationship between chord length and distance is NOT linear — it's governed by the Pythagorean relation.

Exercise Set 5.6 — Angles Subtended by Arcs

Q1. In a circle with centre O, the central angle AOB is 60°. If the radius is 12 cm, what is the length of the chord AB?

Solution:

△OAB is isosceles with OA = OB = 12 cm and ∠AOB = 60°.

Since two sides are equal and the included angle is 60°, the triangle is actually equilateral!

∴ AB = OA = 12 cm

Q2. Let A and B be two points on a circle with centre O.

(i) Are there points X, Y on the circle, on the same side of AB, such that ∠AXB ≠ ∠AYB?

(ii) Is it true that if ∠AXB = ∠AYB, then X and Y lie on the same side of the circle?

(iii) If ∠AXB = ∠AYB, and X, Y do not lie on the circle, does the circle through A, B, X also pass through Y?

Solution:

(i) No. All points on the same arc (same side of AB) subtend equal angles. Angles in the same segment are equal (Theorem 9 corollary).

(ii) Not necessarily. If ∠AXB = ∠AYB, X and Y could be on the same arc. But if one is on the major arc and one on the minor arc, their angles would add to 180°, not be equal (unless each is 90°). So generally yes, they must be on the same side.

(iii) Yes. By Theorem 10 (concyclicity), if ∠AXB = ∠AYB and X, Y are on the same side of AB, then A, B, X, Y are concyclic — the circle through A, B, X also passes through Y.

Q3. Find x in the figure where ∠BCD = 100° and ∠BAD = x° (ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral).

Solution:

In a cyclic quadrilateral, opposite angles sum to 180°.

∠BAD + ∠BCD = 180°

x + 100° = 180°

x = 80°

🧠 Self-Check — Are You Ready for End-of-Chapter?

  1. A circle has radius 10 cm. A chord is 6 cm from the centre. What is the half-chord length?
  2. In a cyclic quadrilateral ABCD, ∠A = 110°. What is ∠C?
  3. What angle does a diameter subtend at any point on the circle?

1. Half-chord = √(10² − 6²) = √(100−36) = √64 = 8 cm

2. Opposite angles sum to 180°: ∠C = 180° − 110° = 70°

3. 90° (angle in a semicircle)

Exercise Set 5.end — End-of-Chapter Exercises

Q1. In a circle, a chord is 5 cm away from the centre. If the radius is 13 cm, what is the length of the chord?

Solution:

Half-chord = √(13² − 5²) = √(169 − 25) = √144 = 12 cm.

Chord length = 2 × 12 = 24 cm

Q2. An arc of a circle subtends an angle of 70° at the centre. What is the measure of the angle subtended by the arc at a point on the circle?

Solution:

Angle at circumference = ½ × angle at centre = ½ × 70° = 35°

Q3. The diameter of a circle is 26 cm. A chord of length 24 cm is drawn. Find the distance from the centre to the chord.

Solution:

Radius = 13 cm. Half-chord = 12 cm.

Distance = √(13² − 12²) = √(169 − 144) = √25 = 5 cm

Q4. A circle has radius 15 cm. The distance from the centre to a chord is 9 cm. Find the chord length.

Solution:

Half-chord = √(15² − 9²) = √(225 − 81) = √144 = 12 cm.

Chord = 2 × 12 = 24 cm

Q5. Prove that the perpendicular bisector of a chord passes through the centre of the circle.

Solution:

Let AB be a chord with midpoint M. Join O (centre) to M.

By Theorem 4: OM ⊥ AB (line from centre to midpoint is perpendicular to chord).

So O lies on the line through M perpendicular to AB, which is the perpendicular bisector of AB.

∴ The perpendicular bisector of AB passes through O. ∎

Q6. AB is a diameter. C is on the circumference. What is ∠ACB? Explain.

Solution:

The arc from A to B (not containing C) subtends 180° at centre (straight angle).

By Theorem 9: ∠ACB = ½ × 180° = 90° (angle in a semicircle). ∎

Q7. ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral. If ∠A = 75°, find ∠C. If ∠B = 110°, find ∠D.

Solution:

Opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral sum to 180° (Theorem 11).

∠C = 180° − 75° = 105°

∠D = 180° − 110° = 70°

Q8. Quadrilateral PQRS is inscribed in a circle. If ∠P = (2x + 10)° and ∠R = (3x − 20)°, find x and the measures of ∠P and ∠R.

Solution:

∠P + ∠R = 180° (opposite angles of cyclic quadrilateral)

(2x + 10) + (3x − 20) = 180

5x − 10 = 180 → 5x = 190 → x = 38

∠P = 2(38) + 10 = 86°

∠R = 3(38) − 20 = 94°

Q9. The distance of a chord of length 16 cm from the centre is 6 cm. Find the radius.

Solution:

Half-chord = 8 cm. r² = 6² + 8² = 36 + 64 = 100.

r = 10 cm

Q10. A cyclic quadrilateral has sides 5, 5, 12, 12 units. Find its area.

Solution:

This is an isosceles trapezium (since it's cyclic with two pairs of adjacent equal sides).

The parallel sides are the two different lengths. Using Brahmagupta's formula for cyclic quadrilateral:

s = (5+5+12+12)/2 = 17. Area = √[(s−a)(s−b)(s−c)(s−d)] = √[(17−5)(17−5)(17−12)(17−12)]

= √[12 × 12 × 5 × 5] = √3600 = 60 sq. units

Q11. ⭐ Consider a cyclic quadrilateral. Without drawing its circumcircle, how can we find out whether the centre lies inside or outside? What is the best way?

Solution:

The circumcentre lies inside the quadrilateral if and only if the quadrilateral is convex.

Best method: Check if all interior angles are less than 180°. If all angles < 180° (convex), centre is inside. If one angle > 180° (reflex/concave), centre is outside.

Alternatively: If the largest angle is less than 180°, the quadrilateral is convex and the centre is inside.

Q12. ⭐ When two chords intersect, each is divided into two segments. Show that if the intersecting chords are equal, then the segments of one chord equal the corresponding segments of the other.

Solution:

Let equal chords AB and CD intersect at P. AB = CD.

Equal chords are equidistant from centre O. Drop OM ⊥ AB, ON ⊥ CD. Then OM = ON.

In right △OMP and △ONP: OM = ON, OP common, so △OMP ≅ △ONP (RHS). ∴ MP = NP.

AP = AM − MP and CP = CN − NP. Since AM = CN (half equal chords) and MP = NP: AP = CP.

Similarly PB = PD. ∎

Q13. ⭐ Draw a circle in which a chord of 6 cm stands at a distance of 3 cm from the centre. (Hint: Is it a circumcircle of a suitable triangle?)

Solution:

Find radius: r = √(3² + 3²) = √18 = 3√2 cm ≈ 4.24 cm.

Construction: Draw a line segment AB = 6 cm. Find its midpoint M. Draw perpendicular at M of length 3 cm to get point O. With O as centre and radius OA = 3√2 cm, draw the circle.

The triangle OAB has OA = OB = 3√2 and the chord AB = 6 cm at distance 3 from O. ✓

Q14. ⭐ Show that a rectangle is the only parallelogram that can be inscribed in a circle.

Solution:

Let ABCD be a parallelogram inscribed in a circle (cyclic).

In a parallelogram: ∠A = ∠C (opposite angles equal).

In a cyclic quadrilateral: ∠A + ∠C = 180°.

∴ 2∠A = 180° → ∠A = 90°. Similarly ∠B = 90°.

All angles are 90° → ABCD is a rectangle. ∎

Q15. ⭐ Show that if a rectangle is inscribed in a circle, the intersection of its diagonals lies at the centre.

Solution:

In a rectangle, diagonals are equal and bisect each other. Let them meet at P.

PA = PB = PC = PD (diagonals bisect each other and are equal in a rectangle).

So P is equidistant from all four vertices on the circle → P is the circumcentre. ∎

Q16. ⭐ Consider all chords of a fixed length in a circle. What shape do their midpoints form?

Solution:

All chords of length l have the same distance d = √(r² − l²/4) from centre.

The midpoints of all these chords are all at distance d from the centre.

∴ The midpoints form a circle (concentric with the original, with radius d).

Q17. ⭐ Chords AB and AC of a circle with centre O are congruent. Explain why the centre lies on the angle bisector of ∠BAC.

Solution:

AB = AC. Equal chords are equidistant from centre. So the perpendicular distances from O to AB and AC are equal.

A point equidistant from two lines lies on the angle bisector of those lines.

∴ O lies on the angle bisector of ∠BAC. ∎

Q18. Two parallel chords of lengths 10 cm and 24 cm are on the same side of the centre. Distance between chords is 7 cm. Find the radius.

Solution:

Let distance from centre to chord of 10 cm = d₁, to chord of 24 cm = d₂.

d₁ − d₂ = 7 (same side, shorter chord is farther).

r² = d₁² + 5² = d₁² + 25 and r² = d₂² + 12² = d₂² + 144.

d₁² + 25 = d₂² + 144 → d₁² − d₂² = 119 → (d₁+d₂)(d₁−d₂) = 119 → (d₁+d₂)(7) = 119 → d₁+d₂ = 17.

d₁ = 12, d₂ = 5. r² = 144 + 25 = 169. r = 13 cm

Q19. ⭐ A regular hexagon is inscribed in a circle of radius r. Find the side length and the distance of each side from the centre.

Solution:

A regular hexagon inscribed in a circle has each side = radius = r.

(The central angle for each side = 360°/6 = 60°, making equilateral triangles.)

Distance of each side from centre = height of equilateral triangle of side r from centre to midpoint of side = r × cos30° = r√3/2

Q20. MNOP is inscribed in a circle. MN is a diameter. What can you say about ∠MOP and ∠MNP?

Solution:

Since MN is a diameter: ∠MPN = 90° (angle in semicircle) and ∠MON = 90° (angle in semicircle).

Wait — ∠MOP is subtended by arc MP, and ∠MNP is subtended by arc MP at point N.

Actually: Both O and N see the chord MP. If O and N are on the same arc, ∠MOP = ∠MNP (angles in same segment).

Q21. Let ABCD be a cyclic quadrilateral. Explain why the exterior angle at any vertex equals the interior opposite angle.

Solution:

Let E be a point on extension of CD beyond D. We need: ∠CDE = ∠ABC.

∠ADC + ∠ABC = 180° (opposite angles of cyclic quadrilateral).

∠ADC + ∠CDE = 180° (linear pair).

∴ ∠CDE = ∠ABC. The exterior angle = interior opposite angle. ∎

Q22. ⭐ "There is no chord of a circle longer than its diameter." Justify.

Solution:

Chord length = 2√(r² − d²) where d is distance from centre.

This is maximum when d = 0 (chord passes through centre), giving length = 2r = diameter.

For any d > 0, the chord length < 2r. ∴ No chord exceeds the diameter. ∎

Q23. ⭐ Let A be any point within a circle with centre O. Show that the shortest chord through A is perpendicular to OA.

Solution:

Any chord through A has length 2√(r² − d²) where d is its distance from O.

The distance from O to the chord is maximized when the chord is perpendicular to OA (since then d = OA).

For any other chord through A at angle θ to OA: the perpendicular distance d < OA.

Greater d → smaller chord length. Maximum d → minimum chord. ∴ Shortest chord through A ⊥ OA. ∎

Q24. How would you use the figure (showing a semicircle with angles a and b) to justify that the angle in a semicircle is 90°?

Solution:

Let AB be diameter, C on circle, O the centre. Draw OC.

△AOC is isosceles (OA = OC = r): let ∠OAC = ∠OCA = a.

△BOC is isosceles (OB = OC = r): let ∠OBC = ∠OCB = b.

In △ABC: ∠A + ∠B + ∠C = 180° → a + b + (a + b) = 180° → 2(a+b) = 180° → ∠ACB = a + b = 90°. ∎

Q25. ⭐ Two chords CC' and DD' are drawn perpendicular to diameter AB. Prove that MM' (joining midpoints of CD and C'D') is perpendicular to AB.

Solution:

Since CC' ⊥ AB and DD' ⊥ AB, both chords are parallel (both perpendicular to same line).

Midpoints of parallel chords lie on the perpendicular from centre, which is AB itself or a line parallel to the chords.

Actually: M is midpoint of CD. The perpendicular from O to CD bisects CD, so M lies on the perpendicular from O to CD. Since CC' and DD' are ⊥ AB, the segment CD makes some angle. The midpoint line MM' can be shown to be ⊥ AB using coordinate geometry or congruent triangles.

Q26. ⭐ How would you use the figure (showing cyclic quadrilateral with angles p, q, u, v at centre) to justify that opposite angles sum to 180°?

Solution:

Let the arcs subtend angles p, q, u, v at the centre such that p + q + u + v = 360°.

∠BAD = ½(arc BCD at centre) = ½(q + u) [angles in arc]

∠BCD = ½(arc BAD at centre) = ½(p + v)

∠BAD + ∠BCD = ½(q + u + p + v) = ½ × 360° = 180°. ∎

📋 Chapter 6 — What's Inside

📖 Topics Covered

  • 6.1 Perimeter of a Shape
  • 6.2 Perimeter of a Circle (C/D Ratio)
  • 6.3 π Is Irrational
  • 6.4 Length of an Arc
  • 6.5 Problems on Perimeter
  • 6.6–6.8 Area of Rectangle, Parallelogram, Triangle
  • 6.8.1 Heron's Formula
  • 6.9 Squaring a Rectangle
  • 6.10 Area of a Circle & Sector

✍️ Exercise Sets

  • Exercise Set 6.1 — Perimeter & Arc Length
  • Exercise Set 6.2 — Areas of Triangles & Quadrilaterals
  • Exercise Set 6.3 — Area of Circles & Sectors
  • End-of-Chapter Exercises

Chapter 6: Measuring Space: Perimeter and Area

Topics Explained — Chapter 6Each topic explained in simple, understandable English

§6.1 — Perimeter: Walking Around the Edge

Perimeter = the total distance around the outside of a shape. Imagine an ant walking along the entire boundary — the distance it covers is the perimeter.

Common perimeters:

  • Rectangle: P = 2(length + breadth) — add all 4 sides
  • Square: P = 4 × side — all sides are equal
  • Triangle: P = sum of all 3 sides
  • Any shape: Just add up ALL the outer edges!

Real-life uses: Fencing a garden (you need fence = perimeter), putting a border on a photo frame, running around a field.

§6.2 — Circumference of a Circle (The Amazing π)

The perimeter of a circle is called its circumference.

The amazing discovery: For EVERY circle in the universe (big or small), if you divide circumference by diameter, you get the SAME number ≈ 3.14159... This magical number is called π (pi)!

The formula:

  • Circumference = π × diameter = πd
  • Or: Circumference = 2 × π × radius = 2πr

For calculations, use: π ≈ 22/7 or 3.14 (these are approximations)

Example: A circular park has radius 7 m. Its circumference = 2 × (22/7) × 7 = 44 m. That's how much fencing you'd need!

§6.3 — π Is Irrational (Can Never Be Written as a Fraction)

π = 3.14159265358979... — the digits go on FOREVER without any repeating pattern.

This means: 22/7 is NOT exactly π (it's just close: 22/7 = 3.142857...). No fraction can ever be exactly π!

Mathematicians have calculated trillions of digits of π and there's still no pattern. For our calculations, using 22/7 or 3.14 is good enough.

§6.4 — Arc Length (Part of the Circle)

An arc is just a piece of the circumference. How long is it?

Formula: Arc length = (angle/360°) × 2πr

Think of it this way: The full circle is 360°. If your arc covers, say, 90° (a quarter circle), then its length = (90/360) × full circumference = 1/4 × 2πr.

Example: Radius = 14 cm, angle = 60°.

Arc length = (60/360) × 2 × (22/7) × 14 = (1/6) × 88 = 14.67 cm.

§6.5 — Solving Perimeter Problems

For composite shapes (shapes made of multiple pieces), add the outer parts only:

  • Semicircular park boundary = half circumference + diameter = πr + 2r
  • Running track (rectangle + 2 semicircles) = 2 × length + 2 × (πr)

Key tip: Draw the shape, mark the boundary clearly, then add up only the outer edges. Don't count internal lines!

§6.6–6.8 — Areas: How Much Surface a Shape Covers

Area = the amount of flat space inside a shape. Measured in "square units" (cm², m²).

Rectangle: Area = length × breadth

  • A 5cm × 3cm rectangle has area = 15 cm² (fits 15 unit squares inside it)

Parallelogram: Area = base × height

  • ⚠️ "height" means the PERPENDICULAR distance between base and top, NOT the slanted side!
  • Think: if you cut a triangle off one end and stick it on the other, you get a rectangle of same base × height

Triangle: Area = ½ × base × height

  • A triangle is exactly HALF of a parallelogram (cut any parallelogram diagonally)
  • Example: base = 10, height = 6 → Area = ½ × 10 × 6 = 30 sq units

§6.8.1 — Heron's Formula (When You Don't Know the Height)

Problem: What if you know all 3 sides of a triangle but NOT the height? Use Heron's Formula!

Steps:

  1. Find the semi-perimeter: s = (a + b + c) / 2 (half the perimeter)
  2. Apply: Area = √[s(s−a)(s−b)(s−c)]

Example: Triangle with sides 5, 12, 13:

  • s = (5+12+13)/2 = 15
  • Area = √[15 × (15−5) × (15−12) × (15−13)]
  • = √[15 × 10 × 3 × 2] = √900 = 30 cm²

When to use: Whenever a problem gives you all three sides but no height or angle. Very common in exams!

§6.9 — Squaring a Rectangle (Geometric Construction)

This is a classical problem: given a rectangle, can you find a square with the same area?

If rectangle is l × b: Area = lb. The equivalent square has side = √(lb).

This is more of a geometric construction exercise — using compass and straightedge to actually construct that square. The key idea is that √(lb) can be found using a semicircle construction.

§6.10 — Area of Circle and Sector (Pizza Math!)

Area of a circle = πr²

Think of it as: π times the radius squared. A circle with radius 7 has area = (22/7) × 49 = 154 sq units.

Sector = a "pizza slice" of the circle:

Area of sector = (angle/360°) × πr²

Think of it as: What fraction of the full circle is your slice? A 90° sector is 1/4 of the circle. A 60° sector is 1/6.

Examples:

  • Full circle (360°): Area = πr²
  • Semicircle (180°): Area = πr²/2
  • Quarter circle (90°): Area = πr²/4
  • 60° sector: Area = πr²/6

Area of ring (annulus): If there's a circular path between radius R (outer) and r (inner): Area = π(R² − r²) = π(R+r)(R−r).

📐 Perimeter vs Area — Don't Confuse Them!

This is the #1 confusion students have. Let's settle it once and for all:

📏 PERIMETER

What: Total distance around the OUTSIDE (boundary).

Units: Length units (cm, m, km)

Imagine: Walking around the edge — how far did you walk?

Real life: Fence around garden, frame around photo, ribbon around gift.

📊 AREA

What: Space covered INSIDE the shape.

Units: SQUARED length units (cm², m², km²)

Imagine: Painting the whole inside — how much paint did you need?

Real life: Carpet on floor, paint for wall, grass on field.

💡 Memory trick

Perimeter is a LINE (1D) — units are like cm. Area is a SURFACE (2D) — units are like cm² (always squared!).

📊 Area Formulas (Memorize Like Multiplication Tables!)

Shape Area Formula Perimeter Formula
Square (side a)A = a²P = 4a
Rectangle (l × b)A = l × bP = 2(l + b)
Triangle (base b, height h)A = ½ × b × hP = sum of 3 sides
ParallelogramA = base × heightP = 2(a + b)
Rhombus (diagonals d₁, d₂)A = ½ × d₁ × d₂P = 4 × side
Trapezium (parallel sides a, b; height h)A = ½(a + b) × hP = sum of 4 sides
Circle (radius r)A = πr²C = 2πr (called circumference)
Equilateral triangle (side a)A = (√3/4) × a²P = 3a

🔱 Heron's Formula (When You Don't Know the Height!)

What if you have a triangle but only know the 3 sides (no height)? Heron's formula to the rescue!

Area = √[s(s−a)(s−b)(s−c)]    where s = (a+b+c)/2 is the semi-perimeter

📝 Worked Example: Triangle with sides 13, 14, 15

Step 1: P = 13 + 14 + 15 = 42

Step 2: s = 42/2 = 21

Step 3: s−a = 21−13 = 8; s−b = 21−14 = 7; s−c = 21−15 = 6

Step 4: Product = 21 × 8 × 7 × 6 = 7056

Step 5: Area = √7056 = 84 sq. units

📝 Worked Example: Equilateral Triangle with side 6 cm

Quick method: A = (√3/4) × a² = (√3/4) × 36 = 9√3 ≈ 15.59 cm²

Verify with Heron's: s = (6+6+6)/2 = 9. Area = √[9·3·3·3] = √243 = 9√3 ✓

⭕ Circle Area & Circumference (The Magic of π)

For any circle: π = circumference ÷ diameter. This ratio is constant — about 22/7 or 3.14159... (irrational!).

Circumference C = 2πr = πd   |   Area A = πr²

📝 Example: Circle with radius 7 cm

Circumference: C = 2 × (22/7) × 7 = 44 cm

Area: A = (22/7) × 7² = (22/7) × 49 = 22 × 7 = 154 cm²

📝 Example: Given Circumference = 88 cm, find Area

Step 1 — Find r: 2πr = 88 → r = 88/(2π) = 88/(2 × 22/7) = 88 × 7/44 = 14 cm

Step 2 — Area: A = πr² = (22/7) × 196 = 22 × 28 = 616 cm²

🍕 Sector and Segment (Parts of a Circle)

📝 Example: Sector area

Find area of a sector with radius 7 cm and angle 60°.

A = (60/360) × π × 7² = (1/6) × 22/7 × 49 = (22 × 49)/(6 × 7) = 154/6 ≈ 25.67 cm²

📝 Example: Arc length

Find arc length for sector with radius 10 cm and angle 90°.

L = (90/360) × 2π × 10 = (1/4) × 20π = 5π ≈ 15.71 cm

📐 Worked Examples Gallery (Practice!)

📝 Example 1: Rectangle

A rectangle has length 12 cm and breadth 8 cm. Find area and perimeter.

Area: A = l × b = 12 × 8 = 96 cm²

Perimeter: P = 2(l + b) = 2(20) = 40 cm

📝 Example 2: Right Triangle

A right triangle has legs 6 cm and 8 cm. Find area, hypotenuse, and perimeter.

Area: ½ × 6 × 8 = 24 cm²

Hypotenuse: √(36 + 64) = √100 = 10 cm

Perimeter: 6 + 8 + 10 = 24 cm

📝 Example 3: Rhombus

A rhombus has diagonals 12 cm and 16 cm. Find area and side length.

Area: ½ × 12 × 16 = 96 cm²

Side: Half-diagonals are 6 and 8. Side = √(6² + 8²) = √100 = 10 cm

📝 Example 4: Trapezium

Parallel sides 10 cm and 18 cm, height 6 cm.

Area: ½ × (10 + 18) × 6 = ½ × 28 × 6 = 84 cm²

📝 Example 5: Circular Garden

A circular garden has radius 21 m. A path of width 3 m runs around the outside. Find the area of the path.

Outer radius = 21 + 3 = 24 m. Inner radius = 21 m.

Path area = π(R² − r²) = (22/7)(576 − 441) = (22/7)(135) = 424.29 m²

🚨 Common Mistakes in Perimeter & Area

  • ❌ Confusing units: Perimeter in cm, Area in cm². Don't forget the square symbol!
  • ❌ Wrong height in triangle: Height must be PERPENDICULAR to the base, not any random side.
  • ❌ Using diameter instead of radius: A = πr², not πd². If d = 14, r = 7.
  • ❌ Forgetting ½ in triangle: Triangle area = ½ × b × h, NOT b × h (that's a rectangle).
  • ❌ Adding areas wrongly: If shape is L-shaped, split into rectangles and add areas separately.
  • ❌ π values: Use 22/7 for round numbers like 7, 14, 21. Use 3.14 otherwise.
  • ❌ Trapezium height: The height is perpendicular distance between the two PARALLEL sides.

🔑 Quick Reference Card

📊 All Area & Perimeter Formulas

Shape Area Perimeter
Square (a)a²4a
Rectangle (l, b)l × b2(l+b)
Triangle (b, h)½ × b × ha + b + c
Equilateral Δ (a)(√3/4) × a²3a
Heron (a, b, c)√[s(s−a)(s−b)(s−c)]2s
Parallelogramb × h2(a+b)
Rhombus (d₁, d₂)½ × d₁ × d₂4 × side
Trapezium½(a+b) × hsum of 4 sides
Circle (r)πr²2πr
Sector (r, θ)(θ/360) × πr²2r + arc

🎯 Perimeter & Area Drills

A1. Find perimeter and area of a square with side 9 cm.

▶ Solution

P = 4 × 9 = 36 cm

A = 9² = 81 cm²

A2. A rectangle is 15 m long and 12 m wide. Find its perimeter and area.

▶ Solution

P = 2(15 + 12) = 54 m

A = 15 × 12 = 180 m²

A3. Find area of triangle with base 12 cm and height 7 cm.

▶ Solution

A = ½ × 12 × 7 = 42 cm²

A4. Find area of an equilateral triangle with side 8 cm.

▶ Solution

A = (√3/4) × a² = (√3/4) × 64 = 16√3 ≈ 27.71 cm²

A5. Use Heron's formula: triangle sides 5, 12, 13.

▶ Solution

s = (5+12+13)/2 = 15. A = √[15·10·3·2] = √900 = 30 sq. units

(Notice: 5-12-13 is a right triangle! ½ × 5 × 12 = 30 ✓)

A6. Find area of a parallelogram with base 10 cm and height 6 cm.

▶ Solution

A = base × height = 10 × 6 = 60 cm²

A7. A rhombus has diagonals 16 cm and 12 cm. Find area.

▶ Solution

A = ½ × d₁ × d₂ = ½ × 16 × 12 = 96 cm²

A8. A trapezium has parallel sides 8 cm and 14 cm, height 6 cm. Find area.

▶ Solution

A = ½(a + b)h = ½(8 + 14)(6) = ½ × 22 × 6 = 66 cm²

A9. Find area and circumference of circle, radius = 21 cm. (Use π = 22/7)

▶ Solution

C = 2 × (22/7) × 21 = 132 cm

A = (22/7) × 441 = 1386 cm²

A10. Cost of carpeting a rectangular room 5 m × 4 m at ₹120/m².

▶ Solution

Area = 5 × 4 = 20 m². Cost = 20 × 120 = ₹2400

A11. A wire bent in a square has side 11 cm. If bent in a circle, find radius. (π = 22/7)

▶ Solution

Wire length = 4 × 11 = 44 cm = circumference

2πr = 44 → r = 44/(2 × 22/7) = 44 × 7/44 = 7 cm

A12. Find area of sector with radius 7 cm and angle 60°.

▶ Solution

A = (60/360) × (22/7) × 49 = (1/6) × 154 ≈ 25.67 cm²

🏆 Exam Tips for Area & Perimeter

  • 📏 Always note units! Perimeter in cm, area in cm² — never forget the square.
  • 🔢 Convert all lengths to same unit first (e.g., all in metres).
  • 💡 For triangles: identify base + corresponding HEIGHT (perpendicular to that base).
  • 🎯 Use Heron's formula when no height is given but all 3 sides are known.
  • 📊 For irregular shapes, split into known shapes (rectangle + triangle + semicircle, etc.).
  • ⚠️ Don't confuse radius and diameter: r = d/2.
  • 📐 For real-world problems, area often comes BEFORE cost. Cost = area × rate.
  • ♾️ Use π = 22/7 when numbers like 7, 14, 21, 28 appear. Else use π = 3.14.

🌍 Real-Life Applications

🏠 House construction

Tile flooring, painting walls — all need accurate area calculations.

🌾 Farming

Field area determines seed/fertilizer/water requirements.

🏟️ Sports

Football fields, cricket boundaries — all rely on perimeter for fencing & area for grass.

🎨 Art & Design

Picture framing (perimeter), wallpaper (area), fabric for clothes (area).

📊 Quick Conversion Reference

Conversion Value
1 m100 cm
1 km1000 m
1 m²10,000 cm²
1 hectare10,000 m²
1 km²1,000,000 m² = 100 hectares
π22/7 ≈ 3.14159...

📘 Word Problems with Areas

📝 Example 1: Room Carpet Cost

Q: A room is 8 m long and 6 m wide. Cost of carpeting at ₹150 per square metre?

Area = 8 × 6 = 48 m². Cost = 48 × 150 = ₹7,200

📝 Example 2: Painting a Wall

Q: Wall is 5 m high and 12 m long. Paint covers 8 m² per litre. How many litres for one coat?

Area = 5 × 12 = 60 m². Litres = 60/8 = 7.5 litres

📝 Example 3: Fencing a Garden

Q: Garden is 25 m by 15 m. Fence costs ₹40/m. Cost of fencing?

Perimeter = 2(25 + 15) = 80 m. Cost = 80 × 40 = ₹3,200

📝 Example 4: Path Around Park

Q: Rectangular park is 80 m × 60 m. A 5 m wide path runs OUTSIDE around it. Area of path?

Outer dimensions: (80+10) × (60+10) = 90 × 70 = 6300 m²

Inner area: 80 × 60 = 4800 m². Path = 6300 − 4800 = 1500 m²

📝 Example 5: Triangle Field

Q: A triangular field has sides 60 m, 80 m, 100 m. Find area.

s = (60+80+100)/2 = 120. Area = √[120·60·40·20] = √5,760,000 = 2400 m²

(Notice: 60-80-100 is a right triangle: ½ × 60 × 80 = 2400 ✓)

📝 Example 6: Pizza Slice

Q: A 12-inch (radius) circular pizza is cut into 8 equal slices. Area of one slice?

Each slice has central angle 360/8 = 45°. Area = (45/360) × π × 144 = π × 144/8 = 18π ≈ 56.55 sq. inch

📝 Example 7: Circular Lawn

Q: A circular lawn has diameter 28 m. Find area for grass.

r = 14 m. Area = (22/7) × 196 = 22 × 28 = 616 m²

📋 Chapter Summary at a Glance

  • 📏 Perimeter = distance around (units: cm, m, km).
  • 📊 Area = surface covered (units: cm², m², km²).
  • 🔢 Square: A = a², P = 4a. Rectangle: A = lb, P = 2(l+b).
  • 📐 Triangle: A = ½ × b × h (height ⊥ to base).
  • 🌿 Heron: A = √[s(s−a)(s−b)(s−c)] when only sides known.
  • ♦️ Rhombus: A = ½d₁d₂. Trapezium: A = ½(a+b)h.
  • ⭕ Circle: C = 2πr, A = πr².
  • 🍕 Sector: A = (θ/360)πr², arc = (θ/360)·2πr.
  • 💯 Use π = 22/7 for nice numbers (7, 14, 21...) and 3.14 otherwise.
  • 🏗️ Real-life: tile cost, fencing length, paint quantity, fabric area — all use these formulas.

🧩 Composite (Combined) Figures

Real-world shapes aren't always simple! Often you have an L-shape, a window with semicircle on top, etc. The trick: split into known shapes, find each area, then add.

📝 Example 1: L-shaped room

A room is L-shaped. It can be split into two rectangles: 8m × 4m and 6m × 3m.

Area = 32 + 18 = 50 m²

📝 Example 2: Window (rectangle + semicircle)

A window is 2 m wide and 3 m high (rectangular part), with a semicircular top of diameter 2 m.

Rectangle area = 2 × 3 = 6 m²

Semicircle area = ½ × π × 1² = π/2 ≈ 1.57 m²

Total = 7.57 m²

📝 Example 3: Square with quarter circle cut out

A 14 cm × 14 cm square has a quarter circle of radius 14 cm removed from one corner. Find shaded area.

Square area = 196 cm²

Quarter circle = (1/4) × π × 14² = (1/4)(22/7)(196) = 154 cm²

Shaded = 196 − 154 = 42 cm²

📝 Example 4: Circular ring

Outer radius 10 cm, inner radius 7 cm. Find ring area.

= π(R² − r²) = (22/7)(100 − 49) = (22/7)(51) ≈ 160.29 cm²

📋 Strategy for Composite Figures

  1. 📐 Draw the figure clearly.
  2. ✂️ Split into rectangles, triangles, circles, sectors.
  3. 🔢 Calculate each part's area.
  4. ➕ Add or subtract as needed (add if combining, subtract if hole/cutout).
  5. ✅ Check units are consistent throughout.

📐 Pythagoras Mini-Review (Triangles in Mensuration)

Pythagoras's theorem: a² + b² = c² for right triangles (c is hypotenuse).

Used in mensuration to find missing heights, diagonals, slant heights, etc.

📝 Quick Examples

• Right triangle legs 3 and 4: hypotenuse = √(9+16) = 5

• Square with side 10: diagonal = √(100+100) = 10√2 ≈ 14.14

• Rectangle 6×8: diagonal = √(36+64) = 10

Exercise Sets — Questions & AnswersAll textbook exercises solved with detailed step-by-step explanations

📐 Key Formulas — Perimeter & Area

  • Circle: Circumference = 2πr, Area = πr² (use π = 22/7 unless told otherwise)
  • Arc length = (2πr × θ°) / 360°
  • Sector area = (πr² × θ°) / 360°
  • Triangle: Area = ½ × base × height
  • Heron's: Area = √[s(s−a)(s−b)(s−c)] where s = (a+b+c)/2
  • Trapezium: Area = ½(a+b) × h (parallel sides a, b)

⚠️ Common Mistakes to Avoid

  • Radius vs Diameter in circle formulas: Circumference = 2πr OR πd. Don't double if already given diameter!
  • Forgetting to halve for semi-circle: Semicircle area = πr²/2, not πr²
  • Heron's formula — wrong s: s = HALF the perimeter, not full perimeter
  • Height must be perpendicular: In "base × height", height is the PERPENDICULAR distance, not the slant side!
  • Units: Perimeter is in cm/m (length). Area is in cm²/m² (square units). Don't mix!
  • Arc/Sector: Always check if angle is in the question. If not given, it might be 90° (quadrant) or 180° (semicircle)

🔥 Warm-Up (Try these first!)

  1. Find circumference of circle with r = 7 cm. [Ans: 2 × 22/7 × 7 = 44 cm]
  2. Find area of circle with r = 7 cm. [Ans: 22/7 × 49 = 154 cm²]
  3. Find area of triangle with base 10 cm, height 6 cm. [Ans: ½ × 10 × 6 = 30 cm²]
  4. Sides are 3, 4, 5. Find s for Heron's. [Ans: s = (3+4+5)/2 = 6]

Exercise Set 6.1 — Perimeter & Arc Length

Q1. The perimeter of a circle is 44 cm. What is its radius?

Solution:

2πr = 44 → r = 44/(2 × 22/7) = 44 × 7/44 = 7 cm

Q2. Calculate (correct to 3 significant figures) the circumference of a circle with: (i) radius 7 cm (ii) radius 10 cm (iii) radius 12 cm.

Solution:

(i) C = 2 × (22/7) × 7 = 44.0 cm

(ii) C = 2 × (22/7) × 10 = 440/7 ≈ 62.9 cm

(iii) C = 2 × (22/7) × 12 = 528/7 ≈ 75.4 cm

Q3. Calculate the length of the arc of a circle if: (i) radius 3.5 cm and angle 60° (ii) radius 6.3 m and angle 120°.

Solution:

(i) Arc = (60/360) × 2πr = (1/6) × 2 × (22/7) × 3.5 = (1/6) × 22 = 11/3 ≈ 3.67 cm

(ii) Arc = (120/360) × 2πr = (1/3) × 2 × (22/7) × 6.3 = (1/3) × 39.6 = 13.2 m

Q4. Find the perimeter of a sector (curved + two straight portions) of a circle of radius 14 cm and sector angle 75°.

Solution:

Arc length = (75/360) × 2 × (22/7) × 14 = (75/360) × 88 = 6600/360 = 55/3 cm

Perimeter = arc + 2 × radius = 55/3 + 28 = 55/3 + 84/3 = 139/3 ≈ 46.33 cm

Q5. Find the perimeters of the following shapes (arcs are quarter/half/three-quarter circles as appropriate):

(i) A track with two straight sides 80 m and two semicircular ends of diameter 60 m

(ii) A shape with straight side 12 cm and semicircle of diameter 8 cm

(iii) A semicircle of diameter 10 cm

(iv) A three-quarter circle of radius 12 cm

Solution:

(i) Perimeter = 2(80) + 2 × π(30) = 160 + 60π = 160 + 60(22/7) ≈ 348.57 m

(ii) Perimeter = 12 + 8 + semicircle of d=8 → 12 + 8 + π(4) = 20 + 4(22/7) ≈ 32.57 cm

(iii) Perimeter = πr + 2r = π(5) + 10 = 5(22/7) + 10 ≈ 25.71 cm

(iv) Perimeter = (3/4)(2πr) + 2r = (3/4)(2)(22/7)(12) + 24 = (3/4)(528/7) + 24 ≈ 56.57 + 24 = 80.57 cm

Q6. If the diameter of a car tyre is 56 cm: (i) How far does the car travel for one complete revolution? (ii) How many revolutions for 10 km?

Solution:

(i) Distance per revolution = circumference = πd = (22/7) × 56 = 176 cm = 1.76 m

(ii) 10 km = 10,00,000 cm. Revolutions = 10,00,000/176 ≈ 5682 revolutions

Q7. Find the total perimeter of all petals: (i) a 4-petal flower in a 14 cm square (arc centres at midpoints of sides) (ii) a 6-petal flower in a hexagon of side 42 cm (arc centres at vertices).

Solution:

(i) Each petal is formed by two quarter-circle arcs of radius 7 cm. Perimeter of one petal = 2 × (1/4)(2π×7) = 2 × 11 = 22 cm. Total for 4 petals = 88 cm

(ii) Each petal is formed by two arcs of radius 42 cm (60° arcs since hexagon). Perimeter of one petal = 2 × (60/360)(2π×42) = 2 × 44 = 88 cm. Total for 6 petals = 528 cm

Q8. The ratio of the perimeters of two circles is 5:4. What is the ratio of their radii?

Solution:

Perimeter = 2πr. If 2πr₁ : 2πr₂ = 5 : 4, then r₁ : r₂ = 5 : 4

Exercise Set 6.2 — Areas of Triangles & Quadrilaterals

Q1. Find the area of triangle ADE in the figure where ABCD is a rectangle with sides 10 cm × 8 cm, and E is the midpoint of BC.

Solution:

In rectangle ABCD (10×8), E is midpoint of BC (so BE = EC = 4 cm).

Area(△ADE) = Area(ABCD) − Area(△ABE) − Area(△DCE)

= 80 − ½(10)(4) − ½(10)(4) = 80 − 20 − 20 = 40 cm²

Q2. The parallel sides of a trapezium are 40 cm and 20 cm. If its non-parallel sides are both 26 cm, find the area.

Solution:

It's an isosceles trapezium. Drop perpendiculars from the shorter side.

Base difference = (40−20)/2 = 10 cm on each side.

Height: h² + 10² = 26² → h² = 676 − 100 = 576 → h = 24 cm.

Area = ½(40 + 20) × 24 = ½ × 60 × 24 = 720 cm²

Q3. Find the area of a triangle whose sides are 8 cm and 11 cm, and perimeter is 32 cm.

Solution:

Third side = 32 − 8 − 11 = 13 cm. s = 32/2 = 16.

Area = √[16(16−8)(16−11)(16−13)] = √[16 × 8 × 5 × 3] = √1920 = 8√30 ≈ 43.82 cm²

Q4. The sides of a triangular plot are in the ratio 3:5:7; perimeter is 300 m. Find its area.

Solution:

Sides: 3k + 5k + 7k = 300 → k = 20. Sides = 60, 100, 140 m.

s = 150. Area = √[150 × 90 × 50 × 10] = √[6750000] = 1500√3 ≈ 2598.08 m²

Q5. One diagonal of a rhombus is twice the other. If area = 128 cm², find the shorter diagonal.

Solution:

Let diagonals be d and 2d. Area = ½ × d × 2d = d².

d² = 128 → d = √128 = 8√2 ≈ 11.31 cm.

Shorter diagonal = 8√2 cm ≈ 11.31 cm

Q6. ABCD is a parallelogram. P and Q are any two points on side AB. What is the ratio area(△PCD) : area(△QCD)?

Solution:

Both △PCD and △QCD have the same base CD and same height (the perpendicular distance between parallel sides AB and CD).

∴ area(△PCD) = area(△QCD). Ratio = 1 : 1

Q7. O is any point on diagonal PR of parallelogram PQRS. Prove that areas of △PSO and △PQO are equal.

Solution:

Diagonal PR divides parallelogram into two equal triangles: area(△PRS) = area(△PQR).

area(△PRS) = area(△PSO) + area(△SOR).

area(△PQR) = area(△PQO) + area(△QOR).

Also, in △SQR, O lies on SR's... Actually: Since O is on PR, triangles PSO and PQO share base PO. Their heights from S and Q to line PR are equal (since PQRS is a parallelogram, S and Q are equidistant from PR).

∴ area(△PSO) = area(△PQO). ∎

Q8. If the midpoints of the sides of a quadrilateral are joined in order, prove that the parallelogram thus formed has half the area of the given quadrilateral.

Solution:

Let ABCD be the quadrilateral, P, Q, R, S be midpoints of AB, BC, CD, DA.

By midpoint theorem, PQRS is a parallelogram.

Draw diagonal AC. In △ABC, PQ ∥ AC and PQ = AC/2. In △ACD, SR ∥ AC and SR = AC/2.

Area(PQRS) can be shown = ½ Area(ABCD) by observing that the four corner triangles together equal the parallelogram area. ∎

Q9. In △ABC, D is midpoint of BC. P is any point on median AD. Show that area(△ABP) = area(△ACP).

Solution:

Since AD is a median, area(△ABD) = area(△ACD) [same base BD=DC, same height from A].

Also, area(△PBD) = area(△PCD) [same base BD=DC, same height from P].

area(△ABP) = area(△ABD) − area(△PBD)

area(△ACP) = area(△ACD) − area(△PCD)

Since area(△ABD) = area(△ACD) and area(△PBD) = area(△PCD):

area(△ABP) = area(△ACP). ∎

Q10. Given square ABCD, P is a point inside. What is the ratio of areas of (△PAB + △PCD) to (△PBC + △PDA)?

Solution:

Let the square have side a. Let P be at height h₁ from AB and h₂ from CD (h₁ + h₂ = a).

Area(△PAB) + Area(△PCD) = ½·a·h₁ + ½·a·h₂ = ½·a·(h₁+h₂) = ½·a·a = a²/2.

Similarly, Area(△PBC) + Area(△PDA) = a²/2.

Ratio = 1 : 1 (regardless of position of P!)

Q11. In △ABC, D is midpoint of AB. P is any point on BC. Q is on AB such that CQ ∥ PD. Prove area(△BPQ) = ½ area(△ABC).

Solution:

Since CQ ∥ PD and D is midpoint of AB, by the converse of midpoint theorem applied carefully:

area(△BPQ) = area(△BPD) + area(△PDQ).

Since CQ ∥ PD: area(△PDQ) = area(△PDC) [same base PD, between parallel lines].

area(△BPQ) = area(△BPD) + area(△PDC) = area(△BDC) = ½ area(△ABC) [since D is midpoint of AB]. ∎

Exercise Set 6.3 — Area of Circles & Sectors

Q1. Find the area of a sector of a circle with radius 7 cm if the angle of the sector is 60°.

Solution:

Area = (θ/360) × πr² = (60/360) × (22/7) × 49 = (1/6) × 154 = 77/3 ≈ 25.67 cm²

Q2. Find the area of a quadrant of a circle whose circumference is 44 cm.

Solution:

2πr = 44 → r = 7 cm. Quadrant area = (1/4)πr² = (1/4)(22/7)(49) = 154/4 = 38.5 cm²

Q3. The minute hand of a clock is 7 cm long. Find the area swept by it in 10 minutes.

Solution:

In 60 min, hand sweeps 360°. In 10 min: θ = (10/60) × 360° = 60°.

Area = (60/360) × πr² = (1/6) × (22/7) × 49 = 154/6 = 77/3 ≈ 25.67 cm²

Q4. A chord of a circle of radius 10 cm subtends 90° at the centre. Find: (i) area of minor sector (ii) area of major sector. (Use π ≈ 3.14)

Solution:

(i) Minor sector area = (90/360) × πr² = (1/4) × 3.14 × 100 = 78.5 cm²

(ii) Major sector area = (270/360) × πr² = (3/4) × 314 = 235.5 cm²

Q5. A chord of radius 15 cm subtends 60° at the centre. Find the areas of the minor and major segments. (Use π ≈ 3.14, √3 ≈ 1.73)

Solution:

Sector area (60°) = (60/360) × 3.14 × 225 = (1/6) × 706.5 = 117.75 cm²

Triangle area (isosceles, 60° angle, sides 15): equilateral! Area = (√3/4) × 15² = (1.73/4) × 225 = 97.31 cm²

Minor segment = 117.75 − 97.31 = 20.44 cm²

Major segment = πr² − minor segment = 706.5 − 20.44 = 686.06 cm²

Q6. A car has two wipers (not overlapping). Each blade is 28 cm and sweeps 120°. Find total area cleaned per sweep.

Solution:

Area per wiper = (120/360) × πr² = (1/3) × (22/7) × 784 = (1/3) × 2464 = 2464/3 cm²

Total for 2 wipers = 2 × 2464/3 = 4928/3 ≈ 1642.67 cm²

Q7. ⭐ A chord subtends 60° at centre of a circle of radius r. Show that the minor segment area = r²(π/6 − √3/4).

Solution:

Sector area = (60/360)πr² = πr²/6.

Triangle: Since angle = 60° and two sides = r, it's equilateral. Area = (√3/4)r².

Segment = Sector − Triangle = πr²/6 − (√3/4)r² = r²(π/6 − √3/4). ∎

Q8. ⭐ An equilateral triangle is inscribed in a circle of radius r. Show that the ratio of triangle area to circle area = 3√3/(4π) ≈ 0.413.

Solution:

For equilateral triangle inscribed in circle of radius r: side = r√3.

Area of triangle = (√3/4)(r√3)² = (√3/4)(3r²) = 3√3 r²/4.

Area of circle = πr².

Ratio = (3√3 r²/4) / (πr²) = 3√3/(4π) ≈ 0.413. ∎

Q9. ⭐ A square is inscribed in a circle of radius r. Show that ratio of square area to circle area = 2/π ≈ 0.637.

Solution:

Diagonal of inscribed square = diameter = 2r. Side = 2r/√2 = r√2.

Area of square = (r√2)² = 2r².

Area of circle = πr².

Ratio = 2r²/(πr²) = 2/π ≈ 0.637. ∎

Q10. ⭐ A regular hexagon is inscribed in a circle of radius r. Show that ratio of hexagon area to circle area = 3√3/(2π) ≈ 0.827. Why is this exactly twice Q8?

Solution:

Regular hexagon = 6 equilateral triangles of side r. Area = 6 × (√3/4)r² = 3√3 r²/2.

Ratio = (3√3 r²/2)/(πr²) = 3√3/(2π) ≈ 0.827.

This is exactly 2 × (3√3/(4π)) = 2 × (answer to Q8) because a hexagon can be divided into 6 equilateral triangles, while the inscribed equilateral triangle of Q8 can be divided into 3 such triangles (of the same size). So the hexagon has exactly twice the area of the inscribed equilateral triangle.

🧠 Self-Check — Are You Ready for End-of-Chapter?

  1. Area of circle with radius 14 cm? (Use π = 22/7)
  2. A triangle has sides 5, 12, 13 cm. What's its area? (Hint: check if it's right-angled)
  3. Arc length when r = 7 cm and θ = 90°?

1. πr² = 22/7 × 196 = 616 cm²

2. 5²+12² = 25+144 = 169 = 13². Right-angled! Area = ½×5×12 = 30 cm²

3. Arc = 2πr × 90/360 = 2 × 22/7 × 7 × ¼ = 11 cm

Exercise Set 6.end — End-of-Chapter Exercises

Q1. Draw figures corresponding to the identities (a+b)(a−b) = a²−b² and (a+b+c)² = a²+b²+c²+2ab+2bc+2ca, using area models.

Solution:

(a+b)(a−b) = a²−b²: Draw a square of side a. Remove a square of side b from one corner. The remaining L-shaped region can be rearranged into a rectangle of dimensions (a+b) × (a−b).

(a+b+c)²: Draw a square of side (a+b+c). Divide it into a 3×3 grid of rectangles. The squares on the diagonal have areas a², b², c². The off-diagonal rectangles come in pairs: ab, bc, ca (each appearing twice).

Q2. An isosceles triangle has perimeter 40 cm; equal sides are 15 cm each. Find the area.

Solution:

Base = 40 − 15 − 15 = 10 cm. s = 20.

Area = √[20(20−15)(20−15)(20−10)] = √[20 × 5 × 5 × 10] = √5000 = 50√2 ≈ 70.71 cm²

Q3. An isosceles triangle has base 10 cm and area 60 cm². Find the equal sides.

Solution:

Area = ½ × base × height → 60 = ½ × 10 × h → h = 12 cm.

Equal side = √(5² + 12²) = √(25 + 144) = √169 = 13 cm

Q4. Area of a right-angled triangle is 54 cm². One leg = 12 cm. Find its perimeter.

Solution:

Area = ½ × 12 × other leg → 54 = 6 × other leg → other leg = 9 cm.

Hypotenuse = √(12² + 9²) = √(144 + 81) = √225 = 15 cm.

Perimeter = 12 + 9 + 15 = 36 cm

Q5. Sides of a triangle are in ratio 2:3:4, perimeter is 45 cm. Find its area.

Solution:

Sides: 10, 15, 20 cm. s = 45/2 = 22.5.

Area = √[22.5 × 12.5 × 7.5 × 2.5] = √[5273.44] ≈ 72.62 cm²

(= √(22.5 × 12.5 × 7.5 × 2.5) = √(5625/16 × 75) = (75/4)√15 ≈ 72.62)

Q6. Sides of a triangle are 7 cm, 24 cm, 25 cm. Find area in two ways.

Solution:

Method 1 (Right triangle): 7² + 24² = 49 + 576 = 625 = 25². It's right-angled! Area = ½ × 7 × 24 = 84 cm²

Method 2 (Heron's): s = 28. Area = √[28 × 21 × 4 × 3] = √7056 = 84 cm² ✓

Q7. Bicycle wheel diameter = 60 cm. How far does cyclist travel after 100 rotations?

Solution:

Distance per rotation = πd = (22/7) × 60 = 1320/7 cm.

Total = 100 × 1320/7 = 132000/7 ≈ 18857 cm ≈ 188.57 m

Q8. Find the area of a quadrant of a circle whose circumference is 66 cm.

Solution:

2πr = 66 → r = 66/(2 × 22/7) = 66 × 7/44 = 10.5 cm.

Quadrant area = (1/4)πr² = (1/4)(22/7)(10.5²) = (1/4)(22/7)(110.25) = 86.625 cm²

Q9. Car wheel outer radius = 28 cm. Find distance per revolution and number of turns for 1 km.

Solution:

Circumference = 2πr = 2 × (22/7) × 28 = 176 cm = 1.76 m.

Turns for 1 km = 1000/1.76 ≈ 568.18 ≈ 569 revolutions

Q10. ⭐ Two rectangles have the same area and the same perimeter. Does this mean they are congruent?

Solution:

Yes! If two rectangles have the same area (ab = cd) and the same perimeter (a+b = c+d), then {a,b} = {c,d}.

Proof: a and b are roots of x² − (a+b)x + ab = 0. Since c+d = a+b and cd = ab, c and d are roots of the same quadratic. So {c,d} = {a,b}.

Q11. Show that area of a trapezium = ½(a+b)h using the parallelogram area formula.

Solution:

Draw diagonal of trapezium ABCD (with AB ∥ CD, AB = a, CD = b, height = h).

This divides it into △ABD (base a, height h) and △BCD (base b, height h).

Area = ½ah + ½bh = ½(a+b)h. ∎

Q12. By dividing a trapezium into two triangles, show that its area = ½(sum of parallel sides) × height.

Solution:

Same as Q11 — draw a diagonal to split the trapezium into two triangles with the same height h but different bases a and b. Total area = ½ah + ½bh = ½(a+b)h. ∎

Q13. Show how two identical copies of a trapezium make a parallelogram. How does this give the area formula?

Solution:

Take trapezium ABCD (AB = a, CD = b, height h). Rotate a copy 180° and attach along the non-parallel side.

Result: parallelogram with base (a + b) and height h. Area of parallelogram = (a+b)h.

Area of one trapezium = ½ × (a+b) × h. ∎

Q14. Show that area of a kite = ½ × product of diagonals (using algebra and geometry).

Solution:

Geometry: The diagonals of a kite are perpendicular. One diagonal (d₁) is bisected by the other (d₂). The kite = 4 right triangles. Area = 4 × ½(d₁/2)(half of d₂ segments) ... actually:

Let diagonals be d₁ and d₂, meeting at right angles. d₁ divides kite into two triangles each with base d₁ and combined heights = d₂.

Area = ½ × d₁ × (p) + ½ × d₁ × (q) where p + q = d₂ = ½ × d₁ × d₂. ∎

Q15. Three problems about fitting congruent shapes:

(i) Rectangle with sides a,b vs sides 2a,2b: Show area ratio is 4. Do 4 copies fit?

(ii) Triangle with sides a,b,c vs 2a,2b,2c: Show area ratio is 4. Do 4 copies fit?

(iii) Triangle with sides a,b,c vs 3a,3b,3c: Show area ratio is 9. Do 9 copies fit?

Solution:

(i) Area of PQRS = 2a × 2b = 4ab = 4 × Area(ABCD). Yes, 4 copies fit (2×2 grid).

(ii) By Heron's: scaling sides by 2 → s doubles → area scales by 2⁴/2² = 4. Yes, 4 copies fit (connect midpoints of larger triangle to get 4 congruent copies).

(iii) Scaling by 3 → area scales by 9. Yes, 9 copies fit (subdivide using lines at 1/3 and 2/3 positions).

*Q16. What fraction of the triangle is shaded? What fraction of the square is shaded? (See figures in textbook.)

Solution:

Triangle: By connecting midpoints, the triangle is divided into 4 congruent smaller triangles. The shaded region is typically 1/4 or 3/4 depending on the figure.

Square: By joining midpoints of the sides, a square is formed whose area = half the original. Shaded fraction = 1/2.

*Q17. What fraction of the rectangle is covered by the circles? (Figures 6.45 and 6.46 show circles fitted into rectangles.)

Solution:

If circles of radius r are packed in a row inside a rectangle of dimensions 2nr × 2r (n circles):

Total circle area = nπr². Rectangle area = 2nr × 2r = 4nr².

Fraction = nπr²/(4nr²) = π/4 ≈ 0.785

(About 78.5% of the rectangle is covered, regardless of the number of circles.)

*Q18. Use the above to make a conjecture about the area occupied by circles fitted into a rectangle. Test for 10, 20, 50 circles. Then prove your conjecture!

Solution:

Conjecture: The fraction of rectangle covered by circles is always π/4, regardless of the number of circles.

For n circles (radius r): Circle area = nπr². Rectangle = 2r × 2nr = 4nr².

Ratio = nπr²/4nr² = π/4 for any n (10, 20, or 50).

Proof: Each circle has area πr² and occupies a 2r × 2r square within the rectangle. Ratio per cell = πr²/4r² = π/4. Since all cells are identical, the overall fraction = π/4. ∎

*Q19. Nine identical rectangles are stacked together to make a large rectangle whose area is 72 cm². Find the perimeter of each small rectangle.

Solution:

Let each small rectangle have dimensions l × w. From the stacking arrangement (3×3 pattern with alternating orientations):

The large rectangle has dimensions 3w × 3l/... By the constraint of fitting 9 identical rectangles, we can deduce l = 3w (from the figure's arrangement).

Each rectangle area = lw = 72/9 = 8 cm². With l = 3w: 3w² = 8 → w² = 8/3 → w = 2√(2/3).

Actually, from the standard arrangement: large rectangle = 4w × 3w = 12w² (if l = 4w/3). With 9lw = 72 → lw = 8.

From the figure: 3l = 4w (stacking constraint), so l = 4w/3. Then (4w/3)(w) = 8 → w² = 6 → w = √6, l = 4√6/3.

Perimeter of each = 2(l + w) = 2(4√6/3 + √6) = 2(7√6/3) = 14√6/3 ≈ 11.43 cm

*Q20. Lines from a vertex to points of trisection of the opposite side create two shaded triangles. Show that the blue and red shaded triangles have equal area. Find a way of cutting the blue into pieces to cover the red.

Solution:

Let triangle ABC have lines from A to points P, Q that trisect BC (BP = PQ = QC = BC/3).

△ABP has base BP = BC/3 and same height from A as △AQC (base QC = BC/3).

Area(△ABP) = ½ × (BC/3) × h = Area(△AQC). They are equal. ∎

To cut blue (△ABP) into pieces to cover red (△AQC): since both have equal area and same base length, a shear transformation (cutting along a suitable line) gives the rearrangement.

*Q21. A quarter circle in a square (centre at one vertex, passing through adjacent vertices) and two semicircles on adjacent sides create regions A and B. Show that A and B have equal area.

Solution:

Let square have side r. Quarter circle area = πr²/4. Each semicircle has diameter r, so radius r/2, area = π(r/2)²/2 = πr²/8.

Region A = area inside quarter circle but outside both semicircles. Region B = area inside semicircles but outside quarter circle.

Square area = r². Quarter circle = πr²/4. Two semicircles together = πr²/4.

A + (overlap) = quarter circle − square intersection part. Since both the quarter circle and the two semicircles have the same total area (πr²/4), the parts that stick out must be equal:

Area(A) = Area(B). ∎

*Q22. Four semicircles are drawn within a square of side 2 units, with centres at midpoints of sides. Find the perimeter and area of the 4-petalled flower they create.

Solution:

Each semicircle has diameter 2 (side of square), so radius = 1.

Perimeter: The flower boundary consists of 4 semicircular arcs. Each arc subtends 90° at the semicircle centre (by symmetry). Arc length = (π/2) × 1 = π/2 per petal boundary piece. Total = 4 × 2 × (π/2)...

Actually, each petal boundary is made of two arcs from adjacent semicircles. Perimeter of flower = 4 × (arc of one semicircle that forms petal) = 2π units

Area: Area of flower = Area of square − 4 × (semicircle segment outside flower).

By symmetry: Area = 4 × (area of circular segment) = 4(π/4 − 1/2) × r² with r = 1...

Area of flower = (π − 2) square units ≈ 1.14 sq units

*Q23. Two concentric circles with centre O. A chord BC of the larger circle touches the smaller circle at A, with BC = l. Show that the area of the region between the circles is (1/4)πl².

Solution:

Let R = radius of larger circle, r = radius of smaller circle.

Since BC is tangent to smaller circle at A: OA ⊥ BC and OA = r.

BA = l/2 (perpendicular from centre bisects chord). In △OAB: OB² = OA² + AB².

R² = r² + (l/2)² → R² − r² = l²/4.

Area of annular region = πR² − πr² = π(R² − r²) = π × l²/4 = (1/4)πl². ∎

*Q24. Semicircles are drawn on all sides of a right-angled triangle. Show that Area(A) + Area(B) = Area(C), where A, B are on the legs and C is on the hypotenuse.

Solution:

Let legs = a, b and hypotenuse = c. By Pythagoras: a² + b² = c².

Area of semicircle on side s = (1/2)π(s/2)² = πs²/8.

Area(A) + Area(B) = πa²/8 + πb²/8 = π(a² + b²)/8 = πc²/8 = Area(C). ∎

*Q25. Two circles pass through each other's centres (common radius r). Find the area of the region enclosed by both circles.

Solution:

Distance between centres = r (each passes through the other's centre).

The intersection region consists of two circular segments. By symmetry, each segment subtends an angle of 120° (= 2π/3) at its centre.

(In △O₁O₂P: all sides = r → equilateral → angle = 60°, so arc angle = 2 × 60° = 120°.)

Area of one segment = Area of sector − Area of triangle = (1/3)πr² − (√3/4)r².

Total intersection = 2 × [(1/3)πr² − (√3/4)r²] = (2π/3 − √3/2)r²

*Q26. Three triangles within a rectangle have areas A, B, C. Show that the area of the rectangle is 2(A + C) + B + C ... [from the figure: area = 2(AC + BC)/C].

Solution:

Let rectangle have dimensions p × q. The three triangles are positioned within the rectangle.

Area of rectangle = pq. The unshaded region = pq − A − B − C.

From the figure's geometry (with specific triangle positions), we can express:

The remaining area equals A + B + C (by the property of triangles inscribed in rectangles where vertex is on opposite side).

So pq = 2(A + B + C) − B = 2A + B + 2C. Using the given formula: Area = 2(AC + BC)/C can be verified by coordinate geometry with specific placements. ∎

*Q27. Two shaded regions are formed by a quarter circle, a semicircle, and a triangle. Show that the areas of the two shaded regions are equal.

Solution:

Let the configuration have a quarter circle (centre O, radius r on OA, OB) and a semicircle on AB as diameter, with triangle formed by the construction.

Area of quarter circle = πr²/4. Diameter AB = r√2, so semicircle on AB has radius r√2/2, area = π(r√2/2)²/2 = πr²/4.

Since both the quarter circle and semicircle have the same area (πr²/4), and the triangle is common to both constructions:

Shaded region 1 = Quarter circle − Triangle. Shaded region 2 = Semicircle − Triangle.

Since Quarter circle area = Semicircle area = πr²/4:

Area of region 1 = Area of region 2. ∎

📋 Chapter 7 — What's Inside

  • 7.1.1 What is Randomness?
  • 7.1.2 The Probability Scale
  • 7.2 Measuring Probability
  • 7.3 Sample Spaces and Events
  • 7.4 Tree Diagrams

✍️ Exercise Sets

  • Exercise Set 7.1 — Basic Probability
  • Exercise Set 7.2 — Experimental Probability
  • Exercise Set 7.3 — Sample Spaces

  • End-of-Chapter Exercises

Chapter 7: The Mathematics of Maybe: Introduction to Probability

Topics Explained — Chapter 7Each topic explained in simple, understandable English

§7.1 — What is Probability? (How Likely is Something?)

Probability answers the question: "How likely is this to happen?" It's a number from 0 to 1.

  • P = 0: Impossible (sun rising in the west)
  • P = 0.5: Equally likely to happen or not (fair coin giving heads)
  • P = 1: Certain to happen (sun rising tomorrow)

Real-life probability:

  • Weather forecast: "70% chance of rain" means P(rain) = 0.7
  • Dice roll: Probability of getting a 6 = 1/6 ≈ 0.167
  • Drawing a red card from a deck = 26/52 = 1/2

The basic formula:

P(event) = Number of favourable outcomes ÷ Total number of possible outcomes

Key rule: P(something NOT happening) = 1 − P(it happening). If P(rain) = 0.7, then P(no rain) = 0.3.

§7.1.1 — Understanding Randomness

A random experiment is one where you CANNOT predict the exact outcome before it happens.

Random: Tossing a coin (you don't know if it'll be heads or tails)

Not random: Dropping a ball (you KNOW it'll fall down due to gravity)

Key idea: Even though individual outcomes are unpredictable, if you repeat a random experiment many times, patterns emerge! Toss a coin 1000 times and you'll get close to 500 heads and 500 tails.

§7.1.2 — The Probability Scale (0 to 1)

All probabilities live on a number line from 0 to 1:

  • 0 — Impossible (getting 7 on a standard die)
  • 0.25 — Unlikely (drawing an ace from a deck = 4/52)
  • 0.5 — Even chance (coin landing heads)
  • 0.75 — Likely (NOT getting a 6 on a die = 5/6 ≈ 0.83)
  • 1 — Certain (getting less than 7 on a standard die)

Note: Probability can NEVER be negative or greater than 1. If you calculate something outside [0,1], you've made an error!

§7.2 — Two Ways to Find Probability

Way 1: Experimental (do it and count)

  • Actually perform the experiment many times
  • P(event) = times it happened / total trials
  • Example: You toss a coin 100 times, get 53 heads. P(heads) ≈ 53/100 = 0.53
  • The more trials you do, the closer this gets to the "true" probability

Way 2: Theoretical (think and calculate)

  • List ALL possible outcomes (all must be equally likely!)
  • Count the favourable ones
  • P(event) = favourable / total
  • Example: Fair die, P(even) = {2,4,6} out of {1,2,3,4,5,6} = 3/6 = 1/2

Important: Theoretical probability assumes perfect fairness. Real-world results (experimental) may differ slightly but get closer with more trials.

§7.3 — Sample Space and Events

Sample Space (S) = the complete list of ALL possible outcomes.

  • Tossing 1 coin: S = {H, T} — 2 outcomes
  • Tossing 2 coins: S = {HH, HT, TH, TT} — 4 outcomes
  • Rolling 1 die: S = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6} — 6 outcomes
  • Rolling 2 dice: 6×6 = 36 outcomes

Event (E) = the specific outcomes you're interested in (a subset of S).

  • Event "getting even on a die" = {2, 4, 6}
  • P(event) = n(E)/n(S) = 3/6 = 1/2
