When water enters guard cells, they swell and curve apart, OPENING the pore. When water leaves, they go limp and CLOSE the pore — saving water in dry conditions.

Q16. Explain how bone supports body movement.

▶ Answer

Bone is a hard connective tissue with a matrix rich in calcium and phosphorus salts. Bones form the SKELETON, which gives shape, protects organs, and acts as RIGID levers. Tendons attach muscles to bones; when a muscle contracts, it pulls the bone, producing movement at a joint (where bones meet, cushioned by cartilage and held by ligaments).

🎯 Section S: Quick Recall Quiz (Fill in the Blanks)

  1. The tissue at the tip of a root that helps it grow longer is called __________ meristem. (Apical)
  2. __________ tissue stores fat under your skin. (Adipose)
  3. The matrix of blood is called __________. (Plasma)
  4. Cartilage at joints reduces __________. (Friction)
  5. __________ tissue carries water from roots to leaves. (Xylem)
  6. The fatty layer around an axon is called __________ sheath. (Myelin)
  7. The hard chemical in sclerenchyma walls is __________. (Lignin)
  8. Plant epidermis has tiny pores called __________. (Stomata)
  9. Heart muscle is technically called __________ muscle. (Cardiac)
  10. __________ connect bone to bone. (Ligaments)

🌟 Section T: Real-World Applications of Tissue Knowledge

  • 🏥 Doctors use biopsies (small tissue samples) to diagnose diseases like cancer.
  • 👩‍🌾 Farmers understand parenchyma storage to choose crops (potatoes, sugarcane).
  • 🪵 Carpenters work with wood (xylem) — knowing grain direction matters.
  • 👕 Textile industry uses sclerenchyma fibres (jute, hemp, flax).
  • 🏗️ Engineers mimic plant structures — bamboo (rich in sclerenchyma) inspires lightweight strong materials.
  • 💊 Pharmacists extract medicines from plant tissues (e.g., aspirin from willow bark).
  • 🩺 Sports doctors treat tendon and ligament injuries common in athletes.

🧬 Chapter 3 mastered! Understanding tissues helps you appreciate how every living thing is beautifully organised.
Now let's leap into Chapter 4 — the world of MOTION! 🏃

📊 Quick-Recall Map: All Tissues in One Glance

PLANTS (2 main groups):

  • 1. Meristematic — Apical / Lateral / Intercalary
  • 2. Permanent
    • Simple — Parenchyma / Collenchyma / Sclerenchyma
    • Complex — Xylem / Phloem
    • Protective — Epidermis / Cork

ANIMALS (4 main families):

  • 1. Epithelial — Squamous / Cuboidal / Columnar / Ciliated / Glandular
  • 2. Connective — Areolar / Adipose / Bone / Cartilage / Blood / Ligaments / Tendons
  • 3. Muscular — Striated / Smooth / Cardiac
  • 4. Nervous — Neurons

If you remember this map, you've mastered Ch 3! 🎯

🎓 Last Reminder for Chapter 3

Plants grow from meristems (tip = length, side = thickness).

Plants stand using collenchyma (flex) and sclerenchyma (strong).

Plants transport via xylem (water up) and phloem (food both ways).

Animals have 4 tissue families: Epithelial covers, Connective links, Muscular moves, Nervous signals.

Practice 5 diagrams (parenchyma, xylem, phloem, neuron, muscles) and you're exam-ready! 💪

🌿 → 🦴 → 🧠 — every organism, from grass to giraffe, is built on these tissue principles!

⚠️ Common Confusions to Avoid

  • 🚫 Xylem ≠ Phloem (xylem = water, phloem = food)
  • 🚫 Tendon ≠ Ligament (tendon = muscle to bone, ligament = bone to bone)
  • 🚫 Cartilage ≠ Bone (cartilage = flexible, bone = hard)
  • 🚫 Striated ≠ Cardiac (cardiac is also striated but branched and involuntary)
  • 🚫 Cell ≠ Tissue (cell = single unit, tissue = group of cells)

🎉
Bravo! Chapter 3 complete.
🧬 Next stop: Motion!
🚀

🏃 Chapter 4: Describing Motion Around Us

Textbook: Exploration (New NCERT 2024-25) | 24 Pages | Topics: Distance vs Displacement, Speed vs Velocity, Acceleration, Position-Time Graphs, Velocity-Time Graphs, Equations of Motion, Uniform Circular Motion
How do we describe and measure the motion of objects around us?

4.1 Motion in a Straight Line

What is Motion?

An object is in motion if its position changes with time with respect to a reference point (origin).

An object is at rest if its position does NOT change with time.

Important: Motion is relative! A passenger in a moving bus is at rest relative to other passengers, but in motion relative to people on the road.

4.1.1 Distance vs Displacement

FeatureDistanceDisplacement
DefinitionTotal path length coveredShortest distance from start to end + direction
DirectionNO (scalar quantity)YES (vector quantity)
Can be zero?Only if object does not moveYes! (if object returns to start)
Can be negative?NeverYes (if moves in negative direction)
ComparisonDistance >= |Displacement| always|Displacement| <= Distance
SI Unitmetre (m)metre (m)

When are they equal? Only when object moves in a straight line WITHOUT changing direction!

4.1.2 Speed vs Velocity

FeatureSpeedVelocity
DefinitionDistance / TimeDisplacement / Time
Formulaspeed = distance / timevelocity = displacement / time
DirectionNO (scalar)YES (vector)
Can be zero?Only if stationaryYes! (swimmer returns to start)
SI Unitm/s (or m s-1)m/s (or m s-1)

Uniform vs Non-uniform Motion

  • Uniform motion: Object covers equal distances in equal time intervals. Speed is constant. Example: car on cruise control on highway.
  • Non-uniform motion: Object covers unequal distances in equal time intervals. Speed changes. Example: car in city traffic.

4.1.3 Acceleration

Acceleration = Rate of change of velocity = How quickly velocity changes

a = (v - u) / t

where: u = initial velocity, v = final velocity, t = time taken

  • SI Unit: m/s2 (or m s-2)
  • Positive acceleration: velocity increasing (speeding up in forward direction)
  • Negative acceleration (retardation/deceleration): velocity decreasing (slowing down)
  • Zero acceleration: velocity is constant (uniform motion)

4.2 Graphical Representation of Motion

4.2.1 Position-Time (x-t) Graphs

Reading a Position-Time Graph:

  • Slope of line = Velocity (steeper line = faster object)
  • Horizontal line = object at rest
  • Straight sloped line = uniform velocity
  • Curved line = acceleration (velocity changing)

4.2.2 Velocity-Time (v-t) Graphs

Reading a Velocity-Time Graph:

  • Slope of line = Acceleration
  • Area under graph = Displacement (very important!)
  • Horizontal line = zero acceleration (uniform motion)
  • Line going up = positive acceleration (speeding up)
  • Line going down = negative acceleration (slowing down)

4.3 Equations of Motion (for constant acceleration)

These three equations relate five quantities: u (initial velocity), v (final velocity), a (acceleration), t (time), s (displacement)

How to Choose Which Equation to Use:

Unknown (not given)Use Equation
s (displacement)v = u + at (1st equation)
v (final velocity)s = ut + (1/2)at2 (2nd equation)
t (time)v2 = u2 + 2as (3rd equation)

Special Cases to Remember:

  • Starting from rest: u = 0, so equations become: v = at, s = (1/2)at2, v2 = 2as
  • Coming to rest (braking): v = 0, so: u = at (time to stop), s = ut - (1/2)at2, u2 = 2as (braking distance)
  • Free fall: a = g = 9.8 m/s2 (downward), u = 0 if dropped

4.3.1 Solved Examples

Example 1: A car starts from rest and accelerates uniformly at 2 m/s2 for 10 s. Find (a) final velocity, (b) distance covered.

Given: u = 0 (starts from rest), a = 2 m/s2, t = 10 s

(a) v = u + at = 0 + 2 x 10 = 20 m/s

(b) s = ut + (1/2)at2 = 0 + (1/2)(2)(10)2 = 100 m

Example 2: A train moving at 72 km/h applies brakes and stops in 50 m. Find deceleration.

Given: u = 72 km/h = 20 m/s, v = 0 (stops), s = 50 m

Find: a = ? (t not given, so use 3rd equation)

v2 = u2 + 2as

0 = (20)2 + 2(a)(50)

0 = 400 + 100a

a = -400/100 = -4 m/s2 (negative = deceleration)

Example 3: A stone is dropped from a height of 45 m. How long does it take to reach the ground? (g = 10 m/s2)

Given: u = 0 (dropped), s = 45 m, a = g = 10 m/s2

s = ut + (1/2)gt2

45 = 0 + (1/2)(10)t2

45 = 5t2

t2 = 9, so t = 3 seconds

4.4 Uniform Circular Motion

Why is Circular Motion Accelerated?

Even though the speed is constant, the direction keeps changing at every point. Since velocity includes direction, and direction changes, velocity is changing. A change in velocity means acceleration exists!

This acceleration is always directed towards the centre of the circle and is called centripetal acceleration.

Formula for speed in circular motion:

v = 2 x pi x r / T

where r = radius of circular path, T = time period (time for one complete revolution)

Important Unit Conversions:

  • km/h to m/s: Multiply by 5/18 (or divide by 3.6)
  • m/s to km/h: Multiply by 18/5 (or multiply by 3.6)
  • Example: 72 km/h = 72 x (5/18) = 20 m/s
  • Example: 10 m/s = 10 x (18/5) = 36 km/h

Important Questions and Answers

Q1: An object travels 16 m in 4 s and then another 16 m in 2 s. What is the average speed?

Ans: Total distance = 16 + 16 = 32 m; Total time = 4 + 2 = 6 s
Average speed = 32/6 = 5.33 m/s

Q2: A car moving at 36 km/h is brought to rest in 10 s by applying brakes. Find (a) acceleration, (b) distance covered before stopping.

Ans: u = 36 km/h = 10 m/s, v = 0, t = 10 s

(a) a = (v-u)/t = (0-10)/10 = -1 m/s2

(b) s = ut + (1/2)at2 = 10(10) + (1/2)(-1)(100) = 100 - 50 = 50 m

Q3: Can an object have zero displacement but non-zero distance? Give example.

Ans: Yes! When an object returns to its starting point. Example: A runner completes one full lap of a 400 m track. Distance = 400 m, but Displacement = 0 (back at start).

Q4: Distinguish between speed and velocity.

SpeedVelocity
Rate of change of distanceRate of change of displacement
Scalar (no direction)Vector (has direction)
Always positiveCan be positive, negative, or zero
Average speed = total distance / total timeAverage velocity = displacement / time

Q5: A cyclist goes around a circular track of radius 35 m in 22 s. Find the speed.

Ans: Circumference = 2 x pi x r = 2 x (22/7) x 35 = 220 m
Speed = distance / time = 220 / 22 = 10 m/s

Q6: What does the area under a velocity-time graph represent?

Ans: The area under a velocity-time graph gives the displacement of the object in the given time interval. For example, if velocity = 20 m/s for 5 seconds (rectangle), area = 20 x 5 = 100 m displacement.

🏃 Section A: Understanding Motion

Look around you — almost EVERYTHING is moving! Cars zip on roads, birds fly, the Sun crosses the sky, your heart beats, electrons spin in atoms. Motion is everywhere.

Definition: An object is said to be in MOTION when its POSITION changes with TIME with respect to a fixed observer (called the REFERENCE POINT).

📝 The Reference Point Matters!

When you sit in a moving bus:

  • To a person standing at the bus stop — YOU are MOVING.
  • To another passenger sitting next to you — YOU are at REST.

So motion is RELATIVE — it depends on the observer!

📍 Section B: Types of Motion

📝 Mixed Motion

🌍 The Earth: rotates on its axis (1 day) AND revolves around the Sun (365 days). The Sun itself orbits the centre of the Milky Way! Nothing is truly "still" in the universe.

📏 Section C: Distance vs Displacement

These two words sound similar but mean very different things in physics!

Feature Distance Displacement
DefinitionTotal length of path coveredShortest line from start to end
TypeSCALAR (only magnitude)VECTOR (magnitude + direction)
ValueAlways positiveCan be +, − or zero
Unitmetre (m), kmmetre (m), km
If you return to startDistance > 0Displacement = 0!

📝 Example 1: Athlete on a Track

An athlete runs around a circular 400 m track and finishes back at the starting line.

Distance covered: 400 m

Displacement: 0 m (start = end)

This shows the BIG difference: distance is what you travel, displacement is your net change in position.

📝 Example 2: Walking Home

You walk 4 km east, then 3 km north.

Distance: 4 + 3 = 7 km

Displacement: √(4² + 3²) = √25 = 5 km (in north-east direction)

⚡ Section D: Speed and Velocity

SPEED

Definition: Distance covered per unit time.

$$\text{Speed} = \dfrac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Time}}$$

Unit: metre per second (m/s) or km/h

Type: SCALAR (no direction)

VELOCITY

Definition: Displacement covered per unit time.

$$\text{Velocity} = \dfrac{\text{Displacement}}{\text{Time}}$$

Unit: metre per second (m/s)

Type: VECTOR (has direction)

Speed Velocity
Uses DISTANCEUses DISPLACEMENT
ScalarVector
"60 km/h""60 km/h north"
Always positiveCan be negative

Conversion: km/h to m/s

$$1 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{1000 \text{ m}}{3600 \text{ s}} = \dfrac{5}{18} \text{ m/s}$$

Shortcut: To convert km/h to m/s, MULTIPLY by 5/18.

To convert m/s to km/h, MULTIPLY by 18/5.

📝 Example 3: Speed Conversion

Convert 72 km/h to m/s.

72 × 5/18 = 20 m/s ✓

Convert 25 m/s to km/h.

25 × 18/5 = 90 km/h ✓

Average Speed

If the speed isn't constant, we use AVERAGE speed:

$$\text{Average Speed} = \dfrac{\text{Total Distance}}{\text{Total Time}}$$

📝 Example 4: Average Speed

A car travels 60 km in the first 2 hours, then 90 km in the next 3 hours. Find average speed.

Total distance = 60 + 90 = 150 km

Total time = 2 + 3 = 5 hours

Average speed = 150/5 = 30 km/h

📈 Section E: Acceleration — Rate of Change of Velocity

When velocity changes (speed up, slow down, or change direction), the object is accelerating.

$$\text{Acceleration} (a) = \dfrac{\text{Change in velocity}}{\text{Time}} = \dfrac{v - u}{t}$$

where u = initial velocity, v = final velocity, t = time

Unit: m/s² (metre per second squared)

Type: VECTOR

Type Meaning Example
Positive (a > 0)Speed increasingCar speeding up on highway
Negative (a < 0)Speed decreasing (retardation/deceleration)Car braking
Zero (a = 0)Constant velocityCruise control on freeway
UniformEqual change in equal timesFree-falling object (g = 9.8 m/s²)
Non-uniformUnequal change in equal timesDriving in city traffic

📝 Example 5: Calculating Acceleration

A car starts from rest (u = 0) and reaches 20 m/s in 5 seconds. Find acceleration.

a = (v − u)/t = (20 − 0)/5 = 4 m/s²

📝 Example 6: Retardation

A bike moving at 30 m/s brakes and stops in 6 seconds. Find acceleration.

a = (0 − 30)/6 = −5 m/s² (negative means retardation)

📐 Section F: The 3 Equations of Motion (for uniform acceleration)

For motion with constant acceleration, three equations relate u, v, a, t and s:

1. $v = u + at$

2. $s = ut + \dfrac{1}{2}at^2$

3. $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$

Where:

  • u = initial velocity (m/s)
  • v = final velocity (m/s)
  • a = acceleration (m/s²)
  • t = time (s)
  • s = displacement (m)

💡 How to choose which equation?

  • If you DON'T have s → use equation 1
  • If you DON'T have v → use equation 2
  • If you DON'T have t → use equation 3

📝 Example 7: Using Eq. 1

A scooter starts at 5 m/s and accelerates at 2 m/s² for 6 s. Find final velocity.

v = u + at = 5 + 2 × 6 = 17 m/s

📝 Example 8: Using Eq. 2

A train starts from rest and accelerates at 1 m/s² for 30 s. Find distance covered.

s = ut + ½at² = 0 × 30 + ½ × 1 × 30² = 0 + ½ × 900 = 450 m

📝 Example 9: Using Eq. 3

A car moving at 10 m/s accelerates uniformly to 30 m/s over 100 m. Find acceleration.

v² = u² + 2as → 30² = 10² + 2 × a × 100

900 = 100 + 200a → 200a = 800 → a = 4 m/s²

📊 Section G: Motion Graphs

Graphs make motion easy to visualise!

1. Distance–Time Graph

2. Velocity–Time Graph

💡 Graph Tricks

  • Distance–time SLOPE = speed
  • Velocity–time SLOPE = acceleration
  • Velocity–time AREA under graph = distance covered
  • Steeper slope = greater rate of change

🌀 Section H: Uniform Circular Motion

When an object moves in a CIRCLE at CONSTANT SPEED, it is in uniform circular motion.

Speed in circular motion:

$$v = \dfrac{2\pi r}{T}$$

where r = radius and T = time for one full circle (period).

📝 Daily Life Examples

  • 🌎 Moon revolving around Earth
  • 🪐 Planets orbiting Sun (almost circular)
  • 🎡 Giant wheel (Ferris wheel)
  • 💿 Disc spinning on a turntable
  • 🚗 Car going around a roundabout at steady speed

🪂 Section I: Free Fall and Gravity

When an object falls under gravity ONLY (no air resistance), it's called FREE FALL.

On Earth, free-fall acceleration is:

g = 9.8 m/s² (approximately 10 m/s²)

(directed DOWNWARDS, towards Earth's centre)

For free fall (initial velocity = 0):

  • After 1 sec → speed = 9.8 m/s
  • After 2 sec → speed = 19.6 m/s
  • After 3 sec → speed = 29.4 m/s
  • ... and so on (constant acceleration!)

📝 Famous experiment (Galileo)

Galileo proved that ALL objects, regardless of mass, fall at the SAME rate in vacuum. Apollo 15 astronaut David Scott showed this on the MOON by dropping a hammer and a feather together — they hit the ground at the same time! 🌑

📚 Section J: Practice Problems

Q1. What is motion? Give two examples.

▶ Answer

Motion is change of position with time relative to a reference point. Examples: a car driving on a road, a bird flying.

Q2. Differentiate between distance and displacement with example.

▶ Answer

Distance is total path length (scalar). Displacement is shortest line from start to end (vector). Example: walking 4 km east then 3 km north — distance = 7 km, displacement = 5 km in NE direction.

Q3. Convert 90 km/h to m/s.

▶ Answer

90 × 5/18 = 25 m/s

Q4. A bike accelerates from 5 m/s to 15 m/s in 4 seconds. Find acceleration.

▶ Answer

a = (v − u)/t = (15 − 5)/4 = 2.5 m/s²

Q5. A train starts from rest and accelerates uniformly at 0.5 m/s² for 60 seconds. Find: (a) final velocity (b) distance covered.

▶ Answer

(a) v = u + at = 0 + 0.5 × 60 = 30 m/s

(b) s = ut + ½at² = 0 + ½ × 0.5 × 60² = 900 m

Q6. A ball is dropped from a 80 m tall building. How long does it take to hit the ground? (Take g = 10 m/s²)

▶ Answer

s = ut + ½gt² → 80 = 0 + ½ × 10 × t² → t² = 16 → t = 4 seconds

Q7. A car moves 80 km north in 1 hour, then 60 km south in 1 hour. Find average speed AND velocity.

▶ Answer

Total distance = 80 + 60 = 140 km, Time = 2 hr → Avg speed = 70 km/h.

Displacement = 80 − 60 = 20 km north → Avg velocity = 20/2 = 10 km/h north

Q8. Why is acceleration considered a vector quantity?

▶ Answer

Because acceleration has BOTH magnitude AND direction. Speeding up = positive direction; slowing down = opposite direction (retardation).

Q9. A car at 20 m/s applies brakes and stops in 8 s. Find (a) retardation, (b) distance covered while stopping.

▶ Answer

(a) a = (0 − 20)/8 = −2.5 m/s²

(b) v² = u² + 2as → 0 = 20² + 2(−2.5)s → s = 400/5 = 80 m

Q10. Explain the difference between uniform and non-uniform motion.

▶ Answer

Uniform: equal distances in equal intervals of time (constant speed). Non-uniform: unequal distances in equal intervals (changing speed).

Q11. What does the slope of a velocity–time graph represent? What does the area under it represent?

▶ Answer

Slope = acceleration. Area under graph = distance/displacement covered.

Q12. An athlete runs around a circular track of radius 35 m. After completing one full round in 40 s, find his average speed and average velocity.

▶ Answer

Distance = 2πr = 2 × 22/7 × 35 = 220 m

Avg speed = 220/40 = 5.5 m/s

Displacement = 0 (start = end), so avg velocity = 0 m/s

📋 Motion Cheat Sheet

  • Motion = position change with time (relative to reference)
  • Types: Translatory, Rotational, Oscillatory
  • Distance = scalar (path length); Displacement = vector (straight line)
  • Speed = distance/time (scalar); Velocity = displacement/time (vector)
  • Conversion: km/h × 5/18 = m/s; m/s × 18/5 = km/h
  • Acceleration = (v − u)/t (m/s²) — can be + or − or 0
  • Equations: v = u + at, s = ut + ½at², v² = u² + 2as
  • Free fall: g = 9.8 m/s² (≈10) downwards
  • Circular motion: speed constant but direction changes (so accelerating)
  • v–t graph slope = acceleration; area under = distance

🌟 Did You Know?

  • ⚡ Light travels at 3 × 10⁸ m/s — fastest speed in universe!
  • 🌍 Earth spins at about 1670 km/h at the equator (we don't feel it because everything around us moves too).
  • 🌌 Earth orbits Sun at ~107,000 km/h.
  • 🐌 The slowest land animal is the garden snail (~0.05 km/h).
  • 🚀 ISRO's Mars Orbiter travelled at over 22,000 km/h escaping Earth's gravity.
  • 🐆 The cheetah is the fastest land animal (~110 km/h sprint).
  • 🌊 Tsunami waves can travel as fast as a jet plane (~800 km/h) in the deep ocean!

💡 Final Recap Mantra

📍 Motion = changing position over time

📏 Distance (path) vs Displacement (straight line)

⚡ Speed (scalar) vs Velocity (vector)

📈 Acceleration = (v − u)/t

📐 3 equations: v = u + at • s = ut + ½at² • v² = u² + 2as

🪂 Free fall: g = 9.8 m/s²

Master these and ace Chapter 4! 🎯

🎉 Chapter 4 Complete! You can now describe motion using physics language.
Next: Mixtures and how to separate them → Chapter 5 🧪

🎯 Section K: Scalars vs Vectors — Master Concept

In physics, every measurable quantity is either a scalar or a vector:

Quantity Scalar / Vector Why
DistanceScalarOnly magnitude
DisplacementVectorMagnitude + direction
SpeedScalarOnly magnitude
VelocityVectorMagnitude + direction
MassScalarJust an amount
WeightVectorAlways downward (gravity)
TimeScalarAlways positive forward
AccelerationVectorHas direction
ForceVectorDirection matters (push/pull)
EnergyScalarJust an amount
TemperatureScalarJust an amount
MomentumVectorMass × velocity (direction)

💡 Quick test

Ask: "Does this measurement need a direction to make sense?" If YES → vector. If NO → scalar.

📊 Section L: Reading Motion Graphs in Detail

From a Distance–Time Graph, How to Find Speed

Slope = rise/run = (change in distance)/(change in time) = speed

Example: Graph rises 50 m over 10 s → slope = 50/10 = 5 m/s

From a Velocity–Time Graph, How to Find Acceleration AND Distance

Slope = (change in velocity)/(change in time) = acceleration

Area under graph = distance covered

Example: A car accelerates from 0 to 20 m/s over 10 s.

  • Slope = 20/10 = 2 m/s² (acceleration)
  • Area = ½ × base × height = ½ × 10 × 20 = 100 m (distance)

🎢 Section M: Real-Life Motion Stories

1. The 100 m Sprint

Usain Bolt's world record: 100 m in 9.58 seconds.

Average speed = 100/9.58 ≈ 10.44 m/s ≈ 37.6 km/h!

His PEAK speed was about 12.42 m/s ≈ 44.7 km/h!

That's faster than most cars in city traffic!

2. Cricket Ball

A fast bowler delivers at 150 km/h = 150 × 5/18 ≈ 41.7 m/s.

The batsman has just 0.45 seconds to react! (22 m pitch / 41.7 m/s ≈ 0.53 s).

This shows how fast professional cricket happens.

3. The Aeroplane

A commercial jet takes off at about 280 km/h. Cruise speed ~900 km/h.

From Delhi to Mumbai (1,400 km), flight time ≈ 1.5 hours.

Average speed = 1400/1.5 ≈ 933 km/h — fits!

4. The Pendulum

A pendulum's period (T) depends on its length (L) and gravity (g):

$$T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{g}}$$

For L = 1 m on Earth, T ≈ 2 seconds.

That's why grandfather clocks use ~1 m pendulums!

🧪 Section N: Activities You Can Try

Activity 1: Measure your walking speed

  1. Mark a 50 m straight line in a park.
  2. Walk at normal pace from one end to the other while timing yourself.
  3. Calculate speed = 50 / time (in m/s).
  4. Convert to km/h by multiplying by 18/5.

Typical walking speed: 1.4 m/s ≈ 5 km/h.

Activity 2: Free Fall Reaction Time

  1. Ask a friend to hold a ruler vertically with 0 at the bottom.
  2. Position your fingers near 0 without touching.
  3. Friend drops the ruler without warning; catch as fast as possible.
  4. Note the distance d where you caught it.
  5. Time = √(2d/g). For d = 0.2 m, t = √(0.4/10) = 0.2 s.

This measures your reaction time. Average human: 0.2 to 0.25 s.

Activity 3: Roll a ball down a slope

Watch how the speed increases (uniform acceleration). Mark its position every second — you'll see the gaps growing larger! That's positive acceleration in action.

📐 Section O: More Numerical Problems

Q13. A car travels first 30 km at 60 km/h and next 30 km at 40 km/h. Find average speed.

▶ Answer

Time 1 = 30/60 = 0.5 h. Time 2 = 30/40 = 0.75 h. Total time = 1.25 h.

Total dist = 60 km. Avg speed = 60/1.25 = 48 km/h

(Not 50! Average of two speeds with different times is weighted.)

Q14. An object thrown up reaches a max height of 45 m. Find initial velocity. (g = 10 m/s²)

▶ Answer

At max height, v = 0. Using v² = u² − 2gs: 0 = u² − 2(10)(45) → u² = 900 → u = 30 m/s

Q15. A car covers 36 km/h. What distance does it cover in 5 minutes?

▶ Answer

36 km/h = 10 m/s. 5 min = 300 s. Distance = 10 × 300 = 3000 m = 3 km

Q16. A body at rest accelerates uniformly. In 5 s it covers 25 m. Find acceleration.

▶ Answer

s = ut + ½at² → 25 = 0 + ½ × a × 25 → a = 50/25 = 2 m/s²

Q17. A bus decelerates from 25 m/s to 5 m/s over 10 s. Find retardation.

▶ Answer

a = (5 − 25)/10 = −2 m/s², i.e., retardation of 2 m/s².

Q18. The Moon orbits Earth in 27.3 days, radius ~384,000 km. Find orbital speed.

▶ Answer

Circumference = 2π × 384,000 ≈ 2,413,000 km

Time = 27.3 × 24 = 655.2 h

Speed ≈ 2,413,000/655.2 ≈ 3683 km/h ≈ 1023 m/s

🎓 Glossary — Motion Terms

  • Motion: Change in position with time, relative to a reference point.
  • Rest: No change in position over time.
  • Reference point: A fixed point from which motion is measured.
  • Distance: Total path length (scalar).
  • Displacement: Shortest path between start and end (vector).
  • Scalar: Only magnitude (distance, speed, mass, time).
  • Vector: Magnitude + direction (displacement, velocity, force).
  • Speed: Distance per unit time.
  • Velocity: Displacement per unit time.
  • Uniform motion: Equal distances in equal times.
  • Non-uniform motion: Unequal distances in equal times.
  • Acceleration: Rate of change of velocity.
  • Retardation: Negative acceleration (slowing down).
  • Free fall: Motion under gravity alone.
  • g: Acceleration due to gravity (9.8 m/s² on Earth).
  • Period (T): Time for one complete oscillation or revolution.
  • Frequency (f): Number of cycles per second; f = 1/T.
  • Uniform circular motion: Constant speed in a circular path.

💡 Exam-Ready Tips for Ch 4

  1. 📝 Always WRITE the formula first, then substitute values.
  2. 🔢 KEEP UNITS consistent — convert km/h to m/s before applying equations.
  3. 📐 DRAW the situation if possible (especially for direction problems).
  4. 🎯 In multi-step problems, find unknowns one at a time.
  5. 📊 Practice reading graphs — many objective questions come from graphs!
  6. 🔁 Remember: full circle → distance > 0 but displacement = 0.

🌍 Section P: Famous Scientists in Motion Studies

Scientist Year Contribution
Aristotle~350 BCEFirst wrote about motion (some ideas wrong!)
Galileo Galilei1600sStudied falling bodies; showed all fall at same rate in vacuum
Isaac Newton16873 Laws of Motion; foundation of mechanics
Albert Einstein1905Special Relativity — motion at near-light speed
Stephen Hawking1970s+Motion of black holes; cosmology

🚦 Section Q: Safety Through Motion Understanding

1. Stopping Distance

When a car brakes, it doesn't stop instantly. Total stopping distance = Thinking distance + Braking distance.

  • At 60 km/h dry road: stop in ~36 m
  • At 100 km/h dry road: stop in ~96 m
  • Double the speed → 4× the braking distance! (because v² in eq. 3)

2. Why Speed Limits Exist

Higher speed = longer reaction distance + much longer braking distance + more crash energy. Speed limits SAVE lives!

3. Seat Belts

In a sudden stop (deceleration), your body still moves forward due to inertia. Seat belts spread the deceleration force across body — prevent fatal injury.

4. Airbags

Inflate in 0.04 seconds during a crash. They INCREASE stopping time → reduce deceleration → reduce force on you!

🏃 Section R: Mind-Blowing Motion Facts

  • 🌌 The entire Universe is expanding! Galaxies are moving away from each other.
  • ⚡ Electrons in atoms orbit at about 2,200 km/s.
  • 🚀 ISS (International Space Station) orbits at 27,600 km/h — one orbit every 90 minutes.
  • ☀️ The Sun moves through the galaxy at about 828,000 km/h.
  • 🐢 A continental plate moves about 2–10 cm per YEAR — slowest "motion" you'd encounter!
  • 💡 Sound travels at 340 m/s in air; almost 1500 m/s in water; 5000 m/s in steel.
  • 🎯 Hummingbird wings beat 80 times per second!

⚠️ Common Pitfalls

  • 🚫 Don't forget to CONVERT units before using formulas.
  • 🚫 Don't confuse speed (scalar) and velocity (vector).
  • 🚫 Acceleration can be negative — DOESN'T mean "slow"; means direction is opposite to velocity.
  • 🚫 In a v–t graph, area under = distance — even if velocity changes!
  • 🚫 For uniform circular motion, speed is constant BUT velocity is NOT (direction keeps changing) — so there IS acceleration.

🎯 Chapter 4 nailed! Motion equations, graphs, free fall, circular motion — all in your toolkit.
Onward to Chapter 5: Mixtures and Separation 🧪

🧮 Section S: More Practice Numericals

Q19. A stone is thrown vertically up with velocity 20 m/s. Find: (a) max height (b) time to reach max height. (g = 10 m/s²)

▶ Answer

(a) v² = u² − 2gs → 0 = 400 − 20s → s = 20 m

(b) v = u − gt → 0 = 20 − 10t → t = 2 s

Q20. A motorcycle accelerates from 36 km/h to 72 km/h in 5 s. Find acceleration in m/s².

▶ Answer

36 km/h = 10 m/s; 72 km/h = 20 m/s

a = (20 − 10)/5 = 2 m/s²

Q21. A car at 20 m/s decelerates at 4 m/s². How long until it stops?

▶ Answer

v = u + at → 0 = 20 + (−4)t → t = 5 s

Q22. An object thrown up returns to the ground in 6 s. Find max height. (g = 10)

▶ Answer

Time UP = 3 s. Using v = u − gt → 0 = u − 10×3 → u = 30 m/s

Max height: s = ut − ½gt² = 30×3 − ½×10×9 = 90 − 45 = 45 m

Q23. What is the difference between average velocity and instantaneous velocity?

▶ Answer

Average velocity = total displacement/total time (over a period). Instantaneous velocity = velocity at one specific instant (what your speedometer shows).

Q24. If a body is moving with non-uniform velocity, what does its velocity–time graph look like?

▶ Answer

It is a CURVED line (not straight). The slope (acceleration) changes over time.

🎯 Quick Visual Recap

Distance vs Displacement: Path length vs straight-line.

Speed vs Velocity: Scalar vs vector (direction matters).

Acceleration: How fast velocity changes.

3 Equations of Motion (for constant a):

  • v = u + at (use when no displacement)
  • s = ut + ½at² (use when no final velocity)
  • v² = u² + 2as (use when no time)

Free fall: a = g = 9.8 m/s² downward.

Circular motion: direction changes → acceleration exists even at constant speed.

Graphs: Slope of v–t = acceleration; area under v–t = distance.

📐 Section T: Important Formulas Sheet

Quantity Formula Unit
Speeddistance / timem/s
Velocitydisplacement / timem/s
Average speedtotal distance / total timem/s
Acceleration(v − u) / tm/s²
Eq 1v = u + at—
Eq 2s = ut + ½at²—
Eq 3v² = u² + 2as—
Circular speedv = 2πr / Tm/s
Free fall (drop)v = gt; h = ½gt²—
km/h → m/s× 5/18—
m/s → km/h× 18/5—

🌟 Section U: Motion in Sports — Real Examples

Sport Speed Type of motion
100m sprint~10 m/sTranslatory + accelerated
Fast bowling (cricket)~42 m/sTranslatory
Tennis serve~70 m/sTranslatory
Golf ball~85 m/sProjectile + rotational
Football kick~35 m/sProjectile
Discus throw~25 m/sRotational + projectile
Pole vault~10 m/sRotational about pole

🏃 "Motion is the universe's most fundamental fact — even atoms are never truly still."
End of Chapter 4. Onwards to mixtures! 🧪

🎓 Section V: Important Sign Conventions

When working with motion, choose a direction as POSITIVE. Anything in opposite direction is NEGATIVE.

  • For horizontal motion: usually take RIGHT or FORWARD as positive.
  • For vertical motion: usually take UP as positive (so g = −9.8 m/s²).
  • If an object is thrown UP, its u is positive, but acceleration (g) is negative.
  • If an object is dropped, u = 0 and g is positive (since downward = positive in this case).

Consistent signs save you from wrong answers!

🚀 Section W: Mind-Stretcher Question

Two trains approach each other. Train A at 60 km/h east, Train B at 40 km/h west. They are 200 km apart. After how long do they meet?

▶ Solution

Relative speed (approaching) = 60 + 40 = 100 km/h.

Time = distance / relative speed = 200 / 100 = 2 hours.

Both trains will collide head-on after 2 hours! 💥

🔁 Section X: Quick Multiple Choice Review

  1. Which is a vector quantity?
    (a) Distance (b) Speed (c) Displacement ✓ (d) Time
  2. SI unit of acceleration is:
    (a) m/s (b) m/s² ✓ (c) km/h (d) m²/s
  3. If displacement is zero, distance must be:
    (a) Zero (b) Equal to velocity (c) ≥ 0 ✓ (d) Negative
  4. Free fall has acceleration of:
    (a) Zero (b) 9.8 m/s² ✓ (c) Variable (d) Negative
  5. In uniform circular motion:
    (a) Speed changes (b) Direction changes ✓ (c) Both change (d) Nothing changes

🌟 Final Wisdom for Motion

Motion isn't just a physics topic — it underlies everything in the universe. From the orbit of planets to the dance of electrons, from a child's swing to a rocket's launch, the same simple equations describe them all.

When you understand motion, you understand the language nature speaks. 🌌

🏃 → 🚗 → ✈️ → 🚀 → 🌌 — from walking to galaxies, motion is everywhere!

🎉
Bravo! Chapter 4 complete.
🚀 Next stop: Mixtures and Their Separation!
🧪

✨ Motion = position changing over time. ✨
Practise, observe, and the laws of motion will become second nature!

🏁 Chapter 4 complete!

🧪 Chapter 5: Exploring Mixtures and Their Separation

Textbook: Exploration (New NCERT 2024-25) | 22 Pages | Topics: Types of mixtures, Solutions (solute, solvent, concentration), Separation techniques (evaporation, distillation, chromatography, separating funnel, sublimation, centrifugation), Colloids, Suspensions, Tyndall Effect

5.1 How Can We Classify Mixtures?

FeatureHomogeneous (Solution)HeterogeneousColloid
CompositionUniform throughoutNon-uniformAppears uniform, but not at molecular level
Particle size< 1 nm (ions/molecules)> 1000 nm (visible)1 - 1000 nm
VisibilityParticles NOT visibleParticles clearly visibleNot visible (even under microscope)
Tyndall effectNo (light passes through)No (blocks light)YES! (scatters light)
Settle on standingNeverYes (particles sink)No (stable)
FiltrationCannot separateCan separateCannot separate by filter paper
ExamplesSalt water, sugar solution, vinegar, alloysMuddy water, sand+water, oil+waterMilk, fog, smoke, butter, blood

Tyndall Effect

When a beam of light passes through a colloid, the light gets scattered by the tiny particles, making the beam visible. This is called the Tyndall Effect.

  • Examples: Sunlight through a dense forest canopy, headlights in fog, beam of light in a dusty room
  • Does NOT happen in true solutions (particles too small to scatter light)
  • Named after physicist John Tyndall

5.2 Solutions in Detail

Solution = Solute + Solvent

  • Solute: The substance that dissolves (smaller quantity). Example: salt, sugar
  • Solvent: The substance in which solute dissolves (larger quantity). Example: water
  • Water is called the Universal Solvent (dissolves more substances than any other liquid)

Types of Solutions:

SoluteSolventExample
SolidLiquidSalt in water, sugar in water
LiquidLiquidVinegar (acetic acid in water), alcohol in water
GasLiquidSoda water (CO2 in water), oxygen in water
SolidSolidAlloys (brass = Cu+Zn, steel = Fe+C)
GasGasAir (O2+N2+CO2 etc.)

Concentration of a Solution

Tells us how much solute is dissolved in how much solvent.

Concentration (mass by mass %) = (Mass of solute / Mass of solution) x 100

Mass of solution = Mass of solute + Mass of solvent

Saturated vs Unsaturated:

TypeDefinitionCan dissolve more?
UnsaturatedLess solute than maximum possibleYES
SaturatedMaximum solute dissolved at that temperatureNO (at same temp)
SupersaturatedMore solute than normally possible (unstable)NO - crystals form

Effect of temperature on solubility:

  • For most solids: Solubility INCREASES with temperature (hot water dissolves more sugar)
  • For gases: Solubility DECREASES with temperature (warm soda goes flat faster)

5.3 Separation of Homogeneous Mixtures

Distillation - How it Works:

  1. Heat the mixture in a distillation flask
  2. Lower boiling point liquid evaporates FIRST
  3. Vapours pass through a condenser (cooled by cold water)
  4. Vapours condense back to liquid and collect in a separate container
  5. Higher boiling point liquid stays behind in the flask

Example: Acetone (BP 56 C) + Water (BP 100 C) - acetone distills first because it boils at lower temperature.

Paper Chromatography - How it Works:

Based on the principle that different substances have different solubilities in the same solvent. More soluble components travel farther with the solvent.

  1. Put a spot of the mixture on chromatography paper
  2. Dip the paper in solvent (water)
  3. Solvent rises up by capillary action
  4. Different components travel different distances
  5. Result: separated coloured bands!

Applications: Separating dyes, detecting drugs in urine, separating pigments from plants, forensic science (ink analysis)

5.4 Separation of Heterogeneous Mixtures

TechniquePrincipleUsed ForExample
Separating FunnelImmiscible liquids form layers (density difference)Two liquids that don't mixOil and water, petrol and water
SublimationSome solids directly turn to vapour on heatingSublimable + non-sublimable solidCamphor + salt, naphthalene + sand
CentrifugationSpinning at high speed separates by densityFine suspended particlesCream from milk, blood components
FiltrationFilter paper allows liquid, blocks solidInsoluble solid + liquidSand from water, tea leaves
DecantationPour off clear liquid after settlingHeavy particles that settleMud from water
Magnetic separationMagnet attracts magnetic materialMagnetic + non-magneticIron filings from sand/sulfur

Separating Funnel - How it Works:

  • Pour mixture of two immiscible liquids (e.g., oil + water) into separating funnel
  • Let it stand - liquids separate into layers (denser liquid at bottom)
  • Open the stopcock - drain bottom layer (water) into a container
  • Close stopcock when only top layer (oil) remains
  • Real-world use: Separating petroleum and water at oil wells

Centrifugation - How it Works:

  • Mixture placed in test tubes in a centrifuge machine
  • Spins at very high speed (thousands of RPM)
  • Heavier particles get pushed to the bottom (sediment)
  • Lighter particles stay on top (supernatant)
  • Applications: Separating cream from milk, blood testing (RBCs settle, plasma on top), washing machines (spin dry)

5.5 How to Choose the Right Technique?

Important Questions and Answers

Q1: What is Tyndall effect? Which type of mixture shows it?

Ans: Tyndall effect is the scattering of light by particles in a mixture when a beam of light passes through it. It is shown by colloids (like milk, fog, smoke) because their particles (1-1000 nm) are large enough to scatter light. True solutions do not show this effect.

Q2: Differentiate between a solution, colloid, and suspension.

PropertySolutionColloidSuspension
Particle size< 1 nm1-1000 nm> 1000 nm
Homogeneous?YesAppears soNo
Tyndall effectNoYesNo
Settles?NoNoYes
Filterable?NoNoYes

Q3: How would you separate a mixture of salt, sand, and iron filings?

Ans: Step-by-step:

  1. Magnetic separation: Use a magnet to remove iron filings (attracted to magnet)
  2. Dissolve: Add water - salt dissolves, sand does not
  3. Filtration: Filter to separate sand (residue) from salt solution (filtrate)
  4. Evaporation: Heat the salt solution to evaporate water and get salt crystals

Q4: Why is crystallisation better than evaporation for obtaining pure solid?

Ans: In evaporation, rapid heating may decompose the solute (e.g., sugar chars). Also, impurities remain mixed. In crystallisation, slow cooling allows pure, well-formed crystals to separate while impurities remain dissolved. This gives a purer product with proper crystal structure.

Q5: How does centrifugation help in blood testing?

Ans: Blood is placed in a centrifuge and spun at high speed. The heavier RBCs settle at the bottom, the lighter plasma rises to the top, and WBCs/platelets form a thin middle layer. This helps doctors examine each component separately to detect diseases like malaria (checking RBCs) or anaemia (counting cells).

🧪 Section A: Matter Classification — Pure or Mixed?

Everything around you is matter. But matter can be of two main types:

Feature Pure Substance Mixture
CompositionFixedVariable
ParticlesOne kindMultiple kinds
Melting/boiling pointSharp/definiteRange
SeparationOnly by chemical meansPhysical methods work
ExamplesWater, salt, iron, oxygenAir, soil, juice, milk

🌍 Section B: Types of Mixtures

1. Homogeneous Mixtures (Uniform Throughout)

Look the same throughout. You can't see individual components even with a magnifier.

Also called: SOLUTIONS.

Examples:

  • 🥤 Sugar dissolved in water
  • 🧂 Salt water
  • 🌬️ Air (mixture of nitrogen, oxygen, etc.)
  • 🪙 Brass (copper + zinc)
  • 💎 Stainless steel (iron + chromium + nickel)

2. Heterogeneous Mixtures (Non-uniform)

Components can be SEEN separately or distinguished.

Examples:

  • 🥗 Salad (vegetables)
  • 🏝️ Sand and water
  • 🥛 Milk (looks uniform but is actually a colloid — particles dispersed in water)
  • 🌫️ Smoke + air
  • 🪨 Granite rock

💧 Section C: Solutions — Deep Dive

A SOLUTION is a homogeneous mixture of two or more substances.

Components

  • Solute — the substance dissolved (usually smaller amount). Example: Sugar in lemonade.
  • Solvent — the substance that dissolves the solute (usually larger amount). Example: Water in lemonade.

SOLUTION = SOLUTE + SOLVENT

Common Types of Solutions

Type Solute Solvent Example
Solid in liquidSaltWaterSalt water
Liquid in liquidVinegarWaterVinegar solution
Gas in liquidCO₂WaterSoda water
Solid in solidZincCopperBrass alloy
Gas in gasOxygenNitrogenAir

Properties of True Solutions

  • Particle size: less than 1 nm (very tiny)
  • Transparent — light passes through cleanly
  • Particles can't be seen even under microscope
  • Doesn't scatter light (no Tyndall effect)
  • Particles can't be separated by filtration
  • Stable — doesn't settle on standing

Saturated vs Unsaturated

  • Unsaturated: more solute can still dissolve at that temperature.
  • Saturated: no more solute can dissolve; any extra sinks at the bottom.
  • Supersaturated: MORE than saturated (formed by hot water — when it cools, crystals form). Used to make ROCK CANDY!

Concentration of a Solution

Concentration = how much solute is dissolved in a given amount of solvent/solution.

Formula:

$$\text{Mass \%} = \dfrac{\text{Mass of solute}}{\text{Mass of solution}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Volume \%} = \dfrac{\text{Volume of solute}}{\text{Volume of solution}} \times 100$$

📝 Example: Concentration

Dissolve 25 g sugar in 100 g water. Find mass percentage.

Mass of solution = 25 + 100 = 125 g

Mass % = (25/125) × 100 = 20%

🌫️ Section D: Suspensions and Colloids

1. SUSPENSION (large particles, settle down)

A heterogeneous mixture where SOLID particles are scattered in a LIQUID but DON'T dissolve.

Examples:

  • 🪨 Sand in water
  • 🟫 Mud water
  • 🥣 Chalk in water
  • 💊 Some medicines ("shake well before use")

Properties:

  • Particle size: more than 1000 nm (visible to eye)
  • Opaque — light is blocked
  • Particles SETTLE down on standing
  • Can be SEPARATED by filtration
  • UNSTABLE

2. COLLOID (middle-sized particles, don't settle)

A heterogeneous mixture where particles are MEDIUM-sized — too small to see with naked eye but too big to dissolve like a true solution.

Examples:

  • 🥛 Milk
  • 🌫️ Fog, smoke
  • 🧴 Cream
  • 🍮 Jelly
  • 🥚 Egg white
  • 🩸 Blood (yes, blood is a colloid!)

Properties:

  • Particle size: 1 to 1000 nm
  • Appears uniform (like solutions) but is heterogeneous
  • SCATTERS light (Tyndall effect — you can see the beam!)
  • Particles DON'T settle (stable)
  • CAN'T be filtered by ordinary filter paper

The Tyndall Effect

When light passes through a colloid, the particles are big enough to SCATTER the light. You can SEE the beam!

Daily-life examples:

  • ☀️ Sunlight passing through trees in a forest — visible rays!
  • 🔦 Torch beam in a misty room
  • 🚗 Headlights in fog
  • 🎬 Movie projector beam in dust

True solutions DON'T show Tyndall effect — light passes straight through.

Types of Colloids

Type Dispersed Phase Dispersing Medium Example
FoamGasLiquidWhipped cream, soap foam
AerosolLiquidGasFog, cloud, mist
SmokeSolidGasSmoke from a fire
EmulsionLiquidLiquidMilk, mayonnaise
SolSolidLiquidPaint, ink
GelLiquidSolidJelly, butter, hair gel
Solid solSolidSolidColoured glass, gemstones

📊 Section E: Comparison — Solution vs Suspension vs Colloid

Property True Solution Colloid Suspension
TypeHomogeneousHeterogeneous*Heterogeneous
Particle size< 1 nm1 – 1000 nm> 1000 nm
Visible particles?NoOnly by ultra-microscopeYes — eye
Settles?NoNoYes
Filterable?NoOnly specialYes
Tyndall effect?NoYESSometimes
Stable?YesYesNo
ExampleSalt waterMilkSand water

🔬 Section F: Separation Techniques — How to Pull Mixtures Apart

Since mixtures can be physically separated, here are the main techniques used:

1. Handpicking

Used when: components have different sizes/colours/shapes.

Example: Separating stones from rice, picking out raisins from cereal.

2. Threshing

Used when: separating grains from stalks of harvested crops.

How: Beat the stalks to make grains fall out.

Today: Done by thresher machines.

3. Winnowing

Used when: separating LIGHT particles (chaff) from HEAVIER ones (grain).

How: Drop mixture from height in air — wind blows lighter chaff away.

Example: Farmers separate wheat grain from husk.

4. Sieving

Used when: separating particles of DIFFERENT SIZES (one passes through holes, other doesn't).

Examples: Sieving flour from bran; separating sand from gravel.

5. Sedimentation and Decantation

Sedimentation: Heavier particles settle at the bottom of a liquid (like sand in muddy water).

Decantation: Carefully pour off the top liquid, leaving the sediment behind.

Example: Settling muddy water before drinking; getting clear water from rice water.

6. Loading

Use: Speeds up sedimentation. Add ALUM (phitkari) to muddy water — particles clump together and settle faster.

Why important: Used to purify drinking water in villages.

7. Filtration

Use: Separate INSOLUBLE solid from liquid.

How: Pour mixture through filter paper in a funnel. Solid stays as RESIDUE; clear liquid passes through as FILTRATE.

Daily life: Making tea (tea leaves stay; tea is filtrate), coffee filters, vacuum cleaners.

8. Evaporation

Use: Get DISSOLVED SOLID from a solution.

How: Heat the solution; water evaporates, solid remains.

Famous example: Sea salt is made by evaporating sea water — India is among top salt producers (Gujarat, TN).

9. Distillation

Use: Separate two LIQUIDS with DIFFERENT boiling points OR get pure liquid from a solution.

How: Heat the mixture; vapours of lower-bp liquid rise; pass through a CONDENSER; cool back to liquid; collect.

Examples: Purifying drinking water; making distilled water; separating alcohol from water.

Fractional Distillation: For liquids with similar boiling points (e.g., petroleum refining → petrol, diesel, kerosene).

10. Chromatography

Use: Separate DIFFERENT COLOURS or COMPONENTS from a mixture using paper.

How: Put a dot of ink on chromatography paper; dip end in water — water rises and carries different pigments to different heights.

Examples:

  • Separating colours in black ink (it's actually many colours!)
  • Forensic science — analysing crime scene chemicals
  • Pharmacy — identifying drug components

11. Magnetic Separation

Use: Separate IRON (or other magnetic materials) from a mixture.

How: Use a strong magnet to attract iron particles.

Examples: Iron filings from sand; scrap iron from garbage in junkyards.

12. Centrifugation

Use: Separate suspended particles from a liquid by spinning fast.

How: Mixture is rotated rapidly; heavier particles move outward and settle.

Examples:

  • Separating cream from milk
  • Washing machine "spin dry"
  • Blood test — separating plasma from blood cells

13. Separating Funnel

Use: Separate two IMMISCIBLE liquids (don't mix), like oil and water.

How: Pour into pear-shaped funnel; let layers settle; open stopcock — heavier liquid (water) flows out first; close to keep oil.

Examples: Petrol from water; oil from water.

14. Crystallisation

Use: Get PURE SOLID from a solution by forming crystals.

How: Make a hot saturated solution; let it cool SLOWLY — pure crystals form; impurities stay in liquid.

Examples: Making sugar crystals, alum, copper sulphate crystals.

Better than evaporation because crystallisation gives PURER substance.

🌊 Section G: How Drinking Water is Purified

Real-world application of several separation techniques together:

📚 Section H: Practice Questions

Q1. Differentiate between pure substances and mixtures.

▶ Answer

Pure substances have ONE kind of particle, fixed composition, sharp m.p./b.p. Mixtures have many kinds, variable composition, range of m.p./b.p. Examples: pure — gold, water; mixtures — air, milk.

Q2. What is the Tyndall effect? Give two examples.

▶ Answer

Tyndall effect is the SCATTERING OF LIGHT by colloidal particles, making the light beam visible. Examples: sunlight through trees in fog; torch beam in mist.

Q3. How would you separate (a) iron filings from sand, (b) salt from water, (c) oil from water?

▶ Answer

(a) Magnetic separation (use a magnet). (b) Evaporation (boil off water). (c) Separating funnel (oil floats; drain water from below).

Q4. Calculate concentration of a solution made by dissolving 40 g salt in 160 g water.

▶ Answer

Mass of solution = 40 + 160 = 200 g

Mass % = (40/200) × 100 = 20%

Q5. Why is milk classified as a colloid and not a true solution?

▶ Answer

Milk has fat globules dispersed in water of size 1–1000 nm — too small to settle but too large to dissolve. It shows Tyndall effect and is opaque, both properties of colloids.

Q6. What is the difference between solute and solvent? Give example.

▶ Answer

Solute is the dissolved substance (smaller amount); solvent does the dissolving (larger amount). Example: in salt water, salt = solute, water = solvent.

Q7. Explain how distillation works.

▶ Answer

Distillation separates liquids based on boiling points. Heat the mixture — lower-bp liquid vaporises first; vapour goes through a condenser and cools back to pure liquid which is collected. Used for distilled water and purifying liquids.

Q8. Why is centrifugation used to separate cream from milk?

▶ Answer

Cream particles are too small to settle naturally. Fast spinning generates high force that pushes heavier liquid outward and lighter cream towards the centre, separating them.

Q9. What is a saturated solution?

▶ Answer

A solution in which NO MORE solute can dissolve at that temperature. Adding more solute will only collect at the bottom.

Q10. What is fractional distillation? Where is it used?

▶ Answer

It separates liquids with close boiling points using a fractionating column. Used in petroleum refining to get petrol, diesel, kerosene, etc.

Q11. Why is crystallisation considered better than evaporation?

▶ Answer

Because in crystallisation only the pure substance forms crystals — impurities stay in the solution. Evaporation may leave behind impurities too.

Q12. Name the technique used to separate dyes in black ink.

▶ Answer

Chromatography (paper chromatography). Different dyes rise to different heights on the paper.

📋 Mixtures Cheat Sheet

  • Matter = Pure (elements/compounds) OR Mixtures
  • Mixtures = Homogeneous (solutions) OR Heterogeneous
  • True solution: < 1 nm; no Tyndall; transparent
  • Colloid: 1–1000 nm; Tyndall YES; stable
  • Suspension: > 1000 nm; settles; filterable
  • Concentration (mass %) = (solute/solution) × 100
  • Separation techniques:
    • Handpicking, threshing, winnowing, sieving (solid-solid by size)
    • Sedimentation, decantation, filtration (solid from liquid)
    • Evaporation, distillation, crystallisation (solid dissolved in liquid)
    • Separating funnel (immiscible liquids)
    • Chromatography (colour separation)
    • Centrifugation (suspension separation)
    • Magnetic separation (magnetic from non-magnetic)

🌟 Did You Know?

  • 🌊 OCEAN water has about 3.5% salt — but distilling 1 litre gives only ~35 g salt.
  • 💎 Diamonds and graphite are BOTH PURE carbon — but with different structures (allotropes).
  • 🧂 The largest natural salt mine in the world is in Pakistan (Khewra) — operating since 1200s!
  • 🛢️ Petroleum at refineries is fractionally distilled into 8+ products!
  • 🌫️ Cloud is a colloid — tiny water droplets in air.
  • 🩸 Blood plasma is 92% water + dissolved proteins + ions.
  • ☕ Chai is a mixture of tea solution + milk colloid + sugar solution — all in one cup!

💡 Final Recap Mantra

Pure vs Mixed; True Solution vs Colloid vs Suspension

Separation: Handpicking → Filtration → Distillation → Centrifuge → Chromatography

Tyndall = light beam visible (only in colloids)

Master these and Chapter 5 is conquered! 🎯

🎉 Chapter 5 Complete! You know how to identify and separate any mixture.
Next: Forces and how they shape motion → Chapter 6 💪

🔬 Section I: Solubility in Detail

Solubility = maximum amount of solute that can dissolve in 100 g of solvent at a given temperature.

Factors Affecting Solubility

1. Nature of solute & solvent: "Like dissolves like."

  • Polar solutes (salt, sugar) dissolve in polar solvents (water).
  • Non-polar solutes (oil, wax) dissolve in non-polar solvents (kerosene).
  • That's why oil doesn't mix with water but mixes with petrol!

2. Temperature:

  • For most SOLIDS — solubility INCREASES with temperature (hot water dissolves more sugar).
  • For GASES — solubility DECREASES with temperature (warm cola loses fizz fast).

3. Pressure:

  • Affects gases mainly. Higher pressure → more gas dissolved (that's why soda is bottled under pressure).
  • When you open a soda bottle, pressure drops → gas escapes → bubbles!

4. Stirring/agitation:

  • Doesn't increase MAX solubility, but speeds up the dissolving process.
  • That's why we stir sugar in tea!

Solubility Curve Example

Substance Solubility at 20°C (g/100g water) Solubility at 80°C
Common salt (NaCl)36 g38 g (barely changes)
Sugar (sucrose)203 g362 g (a LOT more)
Copper sulphate32 g55 g
Potassium nitrate31 g170 g (huge increase)

🧪 Section J: Activities You Can Do at Home

Activity 1: Make a Rainbow with Chromatography

You need: Coffee filter paper or paper towel, water-soluble markers (black/brown work best), a glass of water.

Steps:

  1. Cut a strip of filter paper.
  2. Draw a thick dot about 2 cm from one end with marker.
  3. Dip the bottom in water (don't let the dot touch water).
  4. Watch — water climbs up; the ink spreads into MULTIPLE colours!

What you learn: Black ink is actually made of many dyes mixed together.

Activity 2: Grow Sugar Crystals

You need: Sugar, water, string, pencil, jar.

Steps:

  1. Boil 1 cup water. Add 3 cups sugar slowly until no more dissolves (supersaturated).
  2. Cool slightly; pour into a jar.
  3. Tie a string around a pencil; dip string into solution.
  4. Wait 3–7 days → sugar crystals form on the string!

What you learn: Crystallisation — pure crystals grow as solution cools.

Activity 3: Separating a Mixture

You need: Salt, sand, iron filings, water, magnet, filter paper, funnel, pan.

Mixture: Salt + sand + iron filings together.

Steps to separate:

  1. Use a MAGNET to pull out iron filings.
  2. Add WATER — salt dissolves; sand doesn't.
  3. FILTER — sand stays on paper; salt water passes through.
  4. EVAPORATE the salt water — pure salt remains.

What you learn: Multiple techniques combined can separate complex mixtures.

Activity 4: Tyndall Effect Indoors

You need: A glass of water, milk, torch, dark room.

Steps:

  1. Shine the torch through a glass of plain water — you can barely see the beam.
  2. Add 2 drops of milk and stir — now the torch beam is clearly VISIBLE through the glass!

What you learn: The milk created a colloid. Particles scatter light = Tyndall effect.

🏭 Section K: Industrial Applications

1. Petroleum Refining (Fractional Distillation)

Crude oil is heated in a tall fractionating column. Different fractions condense at different heights:

Fraction Boiling Range Use
Petroleum gas (LPG)Below 40°CCooking
Petrol40–200°CCars, bikes
Kerosene150–250°CStoves, jet fuel
Diesel250–350°CTrucks, buses
Lubricating oil300–370°CGreasing machines
Bitumen> 370°CRoad tar

2. Air Separation

Liquefying air and fractional distillation gives:

  • Nitrogen (b.p. –196°C) → fertilisers, food packaging
  • Oxygen (b.p. –183°C) → hospitals, rocket fuel
  • Argon (b.p. –186°C) → light bulbs

3. Sugar Industry

Sugar from sugarcane uses several steps:

  1. Extraction — crush cane to get juice (mixture).
  2. Filtration — remove fibres.
  3. Evaporation — concentrate the juice.
  4. Crystallisation — pure sugar crystals form.
  5. Centrifugation — separate crystals from molasses.

4. Pharmaceutical Industry

Distillation purifies drugs. Centrifugation separates blood cells for tests. Chromatography identifies what's in medicine and tests purity.

🔬 Section L: Some Tricky Concepts

Alloys — A Special Solid Solution

Alloys are solid solutions of metals (or metals + non-metals). They have better properties than pure metals.

Alloy Composition Use
BrassCu + ZnLocks, utensils, instruments
BronzeCu + SnStatues, medals, bells
SteelFe + CConstruction, vehicles
Stainless SteelFe + Cr + Ni + CUtensils, surgical tools
SolderPb + SnElectronics, joining wires
DuraluminAl + Cu + MgAircraft bodies
22 Carat GoldAu + Cu/AgJewellery (stronger than pure gold)

Why Pure Gold isn't used for Jewellery?

Pure gold (24 carat) is too SOFT to make rings or chains — they would bend easily. Mixing with copper or silver makes it stronger. That's why 22 carat (91.6% gold) is used.

Why Air is Considered Homogeneous

Air looks uniform — you cannot see the different gases (78% N₂, 21% O₂, 1% Ar, etc.). They are mixed at the molecular level. So air = HOMOGENEOUS mixture.

But smoke + air = HETEROGENEOUS (you can see the smoke particles).

🎯 Section M: Higher-Order Thinking Questions

Q13. Why does cold water hold MORE oxygen than warm water? How does this affect fish?

▶ Answer

Gases are MORE soluble at LOWER temperatures. So cold rivers have more dissolved O₂. In summer, warmer water holds less O₂ — that's why fish suffocate in heat waves or warm polluted rivers.

Q14. If you mix sand, salt, water, and iron nails — describe the full separation procedure.

▶ Answer

1) Use MAGNET to remove iron nails. 2) FILTER mixture — sand stays on paper, salt water passes through. 3) EVAPORATE filtrate to get salt. Total: 3 techniques!

Q15. Tea is made by boiling water + tea leaves + sugar + milk. Why is the tea you drink a mixture of three things at once: solution + colloid + suspension (when residue settles)?

▶ Answer

Sugar in water = SOLUTION (dissolves completely). Milk fat in water = COLLOID. Tea leaves at the bottom of teapot = SUSPENSION (they settle). One cup = all three types of mixtures!

Q16. Why must milk be PASTEURISED but not DISTILLED?

▶ Answer

Pasteurisation heats milk to kill harmful bacteria but keeps nutrients intact. Distilling would separate milk into water and solid residues — destroying it as a food.

Q17. 50 g of common salt is dissolved in 200 g water. Calculate the mass percentage of the solution.

▶ Answer

Mass of solution = 50 + 200 = 250 g.

Mass % = (50/250) × 100 = 20%

Q18. Identify whether each is a solution, suspension or colloid: (a) Soap water, (b) Sand in oil, (c) Salt in vinegar, (d) Blood, (e) Hair gel.

▶ Answer

(a) Soap water — colloid. (b) Sand in oil — suspension. (c) Salt in vinegar — solution. (d) Blood — colloid. (e) Hair gel — colloid (gel type).

Q19. Why does the colour change when you add sugar to tea — but the solubility of salt in water remains nearly the same regardless of temperature?

▶ Answer

Sugar molecules interact strongly with water — solubility shoots up with heat. Salt (NaCl) ionises completely; the ion-water bonds don't get much stronger with heat, so solubility barely changes.

Q20. Why is the term "ALLOY" technically a solid solution? Give an example.

▶ Answer

Alloys are homogeneous mixtures of metals (or metal + non-metal). Atoms of one metal are uniformly mixed in another. Example: Brass = uniform mixture of copper and zinc atoms.

📖 Glossary of Key Terms

Pure Substance
Made of one kind of particle (element or compound).
Mixture
Two or more substances physically combined; can be separated by physical means.
Homogeneous
Uniform composition throughout (solution).
Heterogeneous
Non-uniform composition (different parts visible).
Solution
Homogeneous mixture of solute + solvent.
Solute
Substance dissolved (usually smaller in quantity).
Solvent
Substance that dissolves the solute (usually larger).
Solubility
Max amount of solute that dissolves in 100 g solvent at given temperature.
Saturated solution
Cannot dissolve any more solute at that temperature.
Unsaturated
Can still dissolve more solute.
Supersaturated
Holds MORE than max — unstable; excess crystallises out.
Suspension
Heterogeneous; particles large; settle on standing.
Colloid
Heterogeneous; medium-sized particles; don't settle; show Tyndall effect.
Tyndall Effect
Scattering of light by colloid particles, making the beam visible.
Filtrate
The liquid that passes THROUGH a filter.
Residue
The solid LEFT ON the filter paper.
Decantation
Pouring off the upper liquid after sedimentation.
Distillate
The condensed liquid collected after distillation.
Centrifuge
Machine that spins fast to separate particles.
Alloy
Solid solution of two or more metals.

🌐 Section N: Real-World Case Studies

Case Study 1: How Sea Salt is Made in India

India is the third-largest salt producer in the world! Gujarat alone makes about 70% of India's salt.

The process:

  1. Sea water (~3.5% salt) is pumped into shallow ponds called "salt pans."
  2. Sun and wind EVAPORATE the water over several weeks.
  3. What remains? A thick brine, then beautiful WHITE SALT crystals.
  4. Workers shovel the salt into mounds, wash, dry, and pack it.

This is large-scale EVAPORATION on a coastal industrial scale.

Case Study 2: Cleaning Up Oil Spills

When oil tankers leak, a thick layer of oil floats on sea water. This kills marine life.

Separation techniques used:

  • Booms contain the spill (like floating walls).
  • Skimmers use the IMMISCIBILITY of oil and water — they scoop up the top layer (like a giant separating funnel).
  • Absorbents like straw or special foams soak up oil.
  • Bioremediation — bacteria eat the oil!

Case Study 3: Dialysis — Saving Lives with Separation

When kidneys fail, blood contains too much urea and waste. DIALYSIS is a separation technique that cleans blood.

How: Blood flows through tubes made of a special membrane that lets small waste molecules pass through into a cleaning fluid — but keeps blood cells back. Like a high-tech filter!

This is similar to FILTRATION + DIFFUSION on a microscopic scale.

Case Study 4: Why Don't Sand Particles in Water Pass Through Filter Paper?

Filter paper has tiny pores — about 11 micrometres wide. Sand grains are MUCH larger (often 50–500 micrometres). So sand can't fit through and stays as RESIDUE.

But salt molecules are TINY (less than 1 nm) — they pass right through with water!

🎓 Section O: Quick Concept Review

Concept Key Idea Remember by...
Pure substanceOne particle, fixed composition"Water is always H₂O"
SolutionSolute + solvent, uniform"Sugar in tea — invisible"
ColloidMedium particles, shows Tyndall"Milk shines in torch beam"
SuspensionBig particles, settle down"Mud in water — settles"
FiltrationSolid from liquid"Tea strainer"
EvaporationGet dissolved solid back"Sea salt"
DistillationPure liquid by boiling+cooling"Distilled water"
ChromatographySeparate colours"Rainbow on filter paper"
CentrifugeSpin to separate"Washing machine dry cycle"
MagnetPull iron out"Iron filings from sand"
Separating funnelImmiscible liquids"Oil and water"
CrystallisationPure crystals from solution"Sugar crystals on string"

Memory Trick — "Cool Solutions Settle Carefully"

Colloids — Tyndall

Solutions — Homogeneous

Suspensions — Settle down

Crystals — Pure solid

❓ Section P: More MCQs (Self Test)

MCQ1: Which of these is a true solution?

(a) Milk   (b) Salt water   (c) Smoke   (d) Sand in water

Answer

(b) Salt water

MCQ2: Tyndall effect is shown by:

(a) Salt solution   (b) Sugar solution   (c) Milk   (d) Water

Answer

(c) Milk (colloid)

MCQ3: Filtration is used to separate:

(a) Two liquids   (b) Insoluble solid from liquid   (c) Two gases   (d) Salt from water

Answer

(b) Insoluble solid from liquid

MCQ4: Petroleum is refined by:

(a) Filtration   (b) Chromatography   (c) Fractional distillation   (d) Centrifugation

Answer

(c) Fractional distillation

MCQ5: 22-carat gold contains roughly:

(a) 50% gold   (b) 75% gold   (c) 91.6% gold   (d) 100% gold

Answer

(c) 91.6% gold (the rest is copper or silver)

MCQ6: Cream is separated from milk by:

(a) Filtration   (b) Centrifugation   (c) Decantation   (d) Crystallisation

Answer

(b) Centrifugation

MCQ7: Which is an element?

(a) Water   (b) Salt   (c) Iron   (d) Sugar

Answer

(c) Iron (Fe is a single element)

MCQ8: Which technique separates two miscible liquids?

(a) Filtration   (b) Distillation   (c) Sieving   (d) Decantation

Answer

(b) Distillation

MCQ9: The Tyndall effect is observed in:

(a) Air   (b) Salt water   (c) Coloured water   (d) Milk in water

Answer

(d) Milk in water — also in sunbeams in a dusty room

MCQ10: Which is heterogeneous?

(a) Vinegar   (b) Brass   (c) Sugar solution   (d) Sand and salt

Answer

(d) Sand and salt — visibly different particles

🌟 Champion of Chapter 5! 🌟

You now understand mixtures, solutions, colloids, suspensions, and 14+ separation techniques!

Practise drawing the classification chart from memory — it ties everything together.

🔁 Section Q: Comparison Charts Recap

Element vs Compound vs Mixture

Feature Element Compound Mixture
Made ofOne type of atomTwo or more atoms chemicallyTwo or more substances mixed
CompositionPure, fixedPure, fixed ratioVariable
Separable?No (smallest chemical unit)By chemical meansBy physical means
ExampleO, H, Fe, AuH₂O, CO₂, NaClAir, milk, soil

Mass Percentage Practice

Quick formula: Mass % = (mass of solute / mass of solution) × 100

  1. 10 g salt in 90 g water → Mass % = 10/100 × 100 = 10%
  2. 5 g sugar in 95 g water → 5%
  3. 20 g sugar in 100 g water → 20/120 × 100 = 16.67%
  4. 15 g NaCl in 85 g water → 15%
  5. 40 g sugar in 60 g water → 40%

Did You Notice?

  • 🥤 Soda fizz disappears in warm cola → gases are LESS soluble in warmth.
  • 🌬️ A car windshield fogs up in winter — water droplets in air = aerosol (colloid).
  • 🧂 Salt feels cool to touch even on hot days — it has high specific heat.
  • 🥛 Skim milk = less fat colloid; cream = more fat colloid (centrifuge separates them).
  • 🌫️ Mist over a river at dawn = water droplets in air = aerosol colloid.

🏁 Chapter 5 Mixtures fully covered with theory, examples, activities, MCQs and applications!

📝 Section R: Extra Numerical Problems

NP1. A solution contains 8 g of sugar in 32 g of water. Calculate (a) mass percentage of solute, (b) mass percentage of solvent.

▶ Answer

Mass of solution = 8 + 32 = 40 g

(a) Mass % of solute = (8/40)×100 = 20%

(b) Mass % of solvent = (32/40)×100 = 80%

NP2. 12 g of common salt is dissolved in 88 g of water. Calculate its mass percentage.

▶ Answer

Mass of solution = 12 + 88 = 100 g

Mass % = (12/100)×100 = 12%

NP3. The mass of a saturated KNO₃ solution at 50°C is 200 g. If the solubility of KNO₃ at 50°C is 80 g/100g water, find the mass of KNO₃ in the solution.

▶ Answer

Saturated solution → 80 g solute in 100 g water → 180 g solution.

So for 200 g solution: solute = (80/180)×200 = 88.89 g

NP4. Find the volume % of 25 mL of ethanol dissolved in water to make 200 mL of solution.

▶ Answer

Volume % = (25/200)×100 = 12.5%

NP5. Calculate the mass of salt and water required to prepare 250 g of 15% solution.

▶ Answer

Mass of solute = 15% of 250 = 37.5 g of salt

Mass of water = 250 − 37.5 = 212.5 g

🧠 Final Tip — How to Tackle Mixture Questions

  1. Identify if it's a SOLUTION, COLLOID, or SUSPENSION (use Tyndall + settling tests).
  2. For separation, ask: what STATES are involved? (solid+liquid, liquid+liquid, etc.)
  3. Then pick the right technique from the table.
  4. For numerical: use mass-% = (solute/solution) × 100. Always check that mass of solution = solute + solvent.

🌿 Section S: Mixtures You Use Every Day

Item Type What's in it
Tap waterSolutionWater + minerals + chlorine
Soft drinkSolution + colloidWater + sugar + CO₂ + colour
ToothpasteColloid (gel)Water + abrasives + flavour
AirSolutionN₂ + O₂ + Ar + CO₂
SoilHeterogeneousSand + clay + water + air + organic
ConcreteHeterogeneousCement + sand + gravel + water
HoneySolutionSugars + water + traces

⭐ Insight: Almost every food and material you encounter is a mixture!

📌 30-Second Summary

  • Matter = Pure substances OR Mixtures
  • Pure = Elements (Fe, O₂) + Compounds (H₂O, NaCl)
  • Mixtures = Homogeneous (solutions) + Heterogeneous (sand+water)
  • Particle size order: solutions < colloids < suspensions
  • Tyndall effect = light scatters in colloids only
  • 14 separation techniques learned — match each to its problem!
  • Mass % = (solute / solution) × 100
  • Alloys = solid solutions of metals

🎓 Mastered Mixtures! On to Chapter 6 — Forces and Laws of Motion 💪

🏆 Chapter 5 — All sections covered! Take a break and review before Chapter 6.

— End of Chapter 5 —

Next: Forces and Laws of Motion

📚

⬇ Continue learning ⬇

💪 Chapter 6: How Forces Affect Motion

Textbook: Exploration (New NCERT 2024-25) | 22 Pages | Topics: Force concepts, Balanced & Unbalanced forces, Friction, Newton's Three Laws of Motion, Momentum, Inertia, Action-Reaction pairs

6.1 The Concept of Force

Force = A push or pull acting on an object. It can:

  • Make a stationary object move
  • Change the speed of a moving object (speed up or slow down)
  • Change the direction of motion
  • Change the shape/size of an object

SI Unit: Newton (N) | Force has both magnitude and direction (it is a vector)

Balanced vs Unbalanced Forces

Balanced ForcesUnbalanced Forces
Equal in magnitude, opposite in directionUnequal forces (net force is not zero)
Net force = 0Net force is not equal to 0
Object stays at rest OR keeps moving at same speedObject accelerates (speeds up, slows down, or changes direction)
Example: Book on table (weight balanced by table's force)Example: Pushing a box and it moves

Key Point: Only unbalanced forces cause a change in the state of motion!

6.3 Force of Friction

Friction = Force that opposes relative motion between two surfaces in contact.

  • Acts in the opposite direction to applied force or motion
  • Depends on: Nature of surfaces (rough = more friction) and weight of object
  • Static friction: Prevents a stationary object from moving (stronger)
  • Kinetic friction: Opposes motion of a moving object (weaker than static)

Why objects stop: Without continuous force, friction gradually brings moving objects to rest.

6.4 Newton's First Law of Motion (Law of Inertia)

Understanding Inertia:

  • Inertia = Natural tendency of an object to resist change in its state of motion
  • More mass = More inertia (a truck has more inertia than a bicycle)
  • Inertia of rest: Dust flies off carpet when beaten, passengers pushed back when bus starts
  • Inertia of motion: Passengers lurch forward when bus brakes, athlete cannot stop instantly

6.5 Newton's Second Law of Motion (F = ma)

Momentum (p = mv)

Momentum = mass x velocity | SI unit: kg m/s

Newton's 2nd Law in terms of momentum: F = change in momentum / time = (mv - mu) / t

  • A heavy truck at low speed can have same momentum as a light car at high speed
  • Momentum has direction (same as velocity)
  • If no external force acts, momentum is conserved (stays constant)

Solved Example:

A car of mass 1000 kg accelerates from 0 to 20 m/s in 10 s. Find the force.

F = ma = m(v-u)/t = 1000 x (20-0)/10 = 1000 x 2 = 2000 N

6.6 Newton's Third Law of Motion (Action-Reaction)

Key Points about 3rd Law:

  • Action and reaction are equal in magnitude but opposite in direction
  • They act on DIFFERENT bodies (not on the same body!)
  • They occur simultaneously (at the same time)
  • They do NOT cancel each other (because they act on different objects)

Example: When a bullet (0.1 kg) is fired from a gun (5 kg) with force 500 N:

  • Bullet acceleration = F/m = 500/0.1 = 5000 m/s2 (forward)
  • Gun recoil acceleration = F/m = 500/5 = 100 m/s2 (backward) - gun kicks back but much less because it is heavier!

Summary: Newton's Three Laws

LawStatementKey ConceptFormulaExample
1st LawObject stays at rest/uniform motion unless net force actsInertiaIf F=0, then a=0Passengers lurch in braking bus
2nd LawNet force = mass x accelerationF = maF = ma = dp/dtHeavier ball needs more force to throw far
3rd LawEvery action has equal and opposite reactionAction-Reaction pairsF(AB) = -F(BA)Rocket propulsion, walking, swimming

Important Questions and Answers

Q1: Why do passengers fall forward when a bus suddenly stops?

Ans: When the bus stops suddenly, the lower body (in contact with bus) stops with the bus. But the upper body continues to move forward due to inertia of motion (Newton's 1st Law). This is why passengers fall forward.

Q2: A force of 5 N gives a mass m1 an acceleration of 10 m/s2, and a mass m2 an acceleration of 20 m/s2. What acceleration would it give if both masses are tied together?

Ans: m1 = F/a = 5/10 = 0.5 kg; m2 = F/a = 5/20 = 0.25 kg
Combined mass = 0.5 + 0.25 = 0.75 kg
Acceleration = F/m = 5/0.75 = 6.67 m/s2

Q3: Explain why a cricketer draws his hands back while catching a ball.

Ans: By drawing hands back, the cricketer increases the time over which the ball decelerates. From F = (mv-mu)/t, if t increases, the force F decreases. This reduces the impact force on hands and prevents injury. (Same reason: airbags in cars increase collision time)

Q4: If action and reaction are equal and opposite, why don't they cancel?

Ans: Action and reaction act on DIFFERENT bodies. They would cancel only if they acted on the same body. For example, when you push a wall: your force acts on the wall, the wall's reaction acts on you. These are on different objects, so they don't cancel.

Q5: A 1000 kg car is moving at 72 km/h. Brakes apply a force of 5000 N. How far does it travel before stopping?

Ans: u = 72 km/h = 20 m/s, v = 0, F = -5000 N, m = 1000 kg
a = F/m = -5000/1000 = -5 m/s2
Using v2 = u2 + 2as: 0 = 400 + 2(-5)s
s = 400/10 = 40 m

💪 Section A: What is a Force?

A FORCE is a PUSH or a PULL on an object.

Forces can:

  • 🚀 Make a stationary object MOVE
  • 🛑 STOP a moving object
  • ⚡ Change the SPEED of motion
  • 🔄 Change the DIRECTION of motion
  • 🍌 Change the SHAPE or SIZE of an object

Unit of Force: Newton (N) — named after Sir Isaac Newton

SI base form: 1 N = 1 kg·m/s² (the force that accelerates a 1 kg mass at 1 m/s²)

Force is a VECTOR quantity: it has both magnitude AND direction.

📋 Section B: Types of Forces

1. CONTACT Forces (need touch)

  • Muscular force — your hand lifting a bag, ox pulling a cart.
  • Friction — when surfaces rub against each other (opposes motion).
  • Normal force — surface pushing up on you (the table holds your book).
  • Tension — pulling force in a rope or string.
  • Spring force — a stretched spring pulling back.
  • Applied force — direct push or pull by a person.

2. NON-CONTACT Forces (act from distance)

  • Gravity — Earth pulls everything down (also called gravitational force).
  • Magnetic force — magnets attract iron, attract/repel other magnets.
  • Electrostatic force — charged objects attract or repel.
  • Nuclear force — holds atoms together (you'll learn more later).

⚖️ Section C: Balanced and Unbalanced Forces

Balanced Forces

When forces acting on an object are EQUAL in magnitude but OPPOSITE in direction.

Result: Net force = 0 → object stays at rest OR moves with same velocity.

Example: Tug-of-war when both teams pull equally hard — rope doesn't move!

Unbalanced Forces

When forces are NOT equal — there's a net force.

Result: Object's motion CHANGES (speeds up, slows down, or changes direction).

Example: Pushing a stationary cart — your push is unbalanced; cart moves.

🌌 Section D: Newton's First Law of Motion (Law of Inertia)

Statement:

"An object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion with the same speed and in the same direction, unless acted upon by an unbalanced external force."

INERTIA — Resistance to Change in Motion

Inertia is the tendency of an object to RESIST any change in its state of motion.

Mass = measure of inertia. More mass = more inertia = harder to push or stop.

Three Types of Inertia:

  1. Inertia of rest: Object at rest tends to stay at rest.
  2. Inertia of motion: Moving object tends to keep moving.
  3. Inertia of direction: Moving object tends to keep moving in the same direction.

Real-Life Examples of Inertia

  • 🚌 Bus stops suddenly — you fall FORWARD (your body wants to keep moving — inertia of motion).
  • 🚌 Bus starts suddenly — you jerk BACKWARD (your body wants to stay at rest — inertia of rest).
  • 🪣 Shake a wet umbrella — water drops fly off (water has inertia of rest while umbrella moves).
  • 🪙 Coin on card on glass — flick the card; coin falls INTO the glass (inertia of rest).
  • 🥕 Hit a carpet — dust flies out (carpet moves, dust has inertia of rest).
  • 🛞 Wheel spinning — keeps spinning until friction stops it (inertia of motion).
  • 🚗 Sharp turn in car — passengers thrown to the side (inertia of direction).
  • 🏃 Athlete runs before long jump — to use inertia of motion to jump farther.

⚡ Section E: Newton's Second Law of Motion

Statement:

"The rate of change of momentum of an object is directly proportional to the applied unbalanced force in the direction of the force."

The Famous Formula: F = ma

$$F = m \times a$$

where:

  • F = force in Newtons (N)
  • m = mass in kilograms (kg)
  • a = acceleration in m/s²

Rearranged: $a = F/m$     or     $m = F/a$

Momentum — A Key Concept

Momentum (p) = mass × velocity

$$p = mv$$

Unit: kg·m/s

Momentum is a VECTOR (has direction).

A heavy or fast object has MORE momentum and is harder to stop!

Examples:

  • 🚛 A truck moving at 10 m/s has WAY more momentum than a cycle at 10 m/s.
  • 🏏 A cricket ball at 150 km/h has more momentum than the same ball at 50 km/h.

Why F = Rate of Change of Momentum

Force = (change in momentum) ÷ (time taken)

$$F = \dfrac{\Delta p}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{m(v - u)}{t} = m \times \dfrac{(v-u)}{t} = m \times a$$

So F = ma! 🎯

Numerical Examples

Example 1: A force of 20 N acts on a 4 kg object. Find acceleration.

a = F/m = 20/4 = 5 m/s²

Example 2: A car of mass 800 kg accelerates from rest to 20 m/s in 10 seconds. Find force.

a = (v−u)/t = (20−0)/10 = 2 m/s²

F = ma = 800 × 2 = 1600 N

Example 3: A football of mass 0.5 kg is kicked with a force of 25 N. Find its acceleration.

a = F/m = 25/0.5 = 50 m/s²

Example 4: A 1500 kg car moving at 25 m/s comes to rest in 5 seconds. Find the retarding force.

a = (0−25)/5 = −5 m/s²

F = ma = 1500 × (−5) = −7500 N (negative = opposes motion)

🔄 Section F: Newton's Third Law of Motion

Statement:

"To every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction."

These ACTION-REACTION forces ALWAYS act on TWO DIFFERENT objects.

Real-Life Examples

  • 🚀 Rocket launch: hot gases pushed downward; rocket pushed upward!
  • 🏊 Swimming: hands push water back; water pushes you forward.
  • 🚶 Walking: your foot pushes ground back; ground pushes you forward.
  • 🔫 Recoil of a gun: bullet shoots forward; gun jerks backward.
  • 🎈 Inflated balloon released: air pushed out; balloon zooms in opposite direction.
  • 🚣 Rowing a boat: oars push water back; boat moves forward.
