  • ⛹️ Bouncing ball: ball pushes ground; ground pushes ball back up.
  • 🛞 Car wheels: tyres push road back; road pushes car forward.

Why a Heavy Gun Recoils Less Than a Bullet's Speed

Even though force on bullet = force on gun (equal & opposite), the bullet has tiny mass → huge acceleration → high speed. The gun has large mass → tiny acceleration → small recoil.

From p = mv: if total momentum is conserved (zero initial), then m_bullet × v_bullet = m_gun × v_gun. Hence gun recoils slowly because it's heavy.

🧪 Section G: Conservation of Momentum

Principle: In an ISOLATED system (no external forces), the TOTAL MOMENTUM before any event equals the total momentum after.

$$m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 = m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2$$

where u = initial velocity, v = final velocity.

Examples — Conservation in Action

Collision Example: A 2 kg ball moving at 5 m/s hits a stationary 3 kg ball. After collision, the 2 kg ball stops. Find velocity of 3 kg ball.

Before: 2(5) + 3(0) = 10 kg·m/s

After: 2(0) + 3(v) = 3v

10 = 3v → v = 3.33 m/s

Gun Recoil: A 4 kg gun fires a 20 g bullet at 200 m/s. Find recoil velocity of gun.

Before: total = 0 (everything at rest)

After: 0.02(200) + 4(v) = 0 → 4v = −4 → v = −1 m/s (1 m/s backward)

Two Trolleys: Trolley A (1 kg) at 3 m/s collides with stationary trolley B (2 kg). They stick together. Find their common velocity.

Before: 1(3) + 2(0) = 3 kg·m/s

After: (1+2) × v = 3v

3v = 3 → v = 1 m/s

🛞 Section H: Friction — The Force That Resists Motion

Friction is the force that OPPOSES the relative motion between two surfaces in contact.

Cause: Surface roughness (even polished surfaces have microscopic bumps that interlock).

Types of Friction

  • Static friction: prevents object from STARTING to move (max value just before sliding).
  • Sliding (kinetic) friction: when surfaces slide against each other.
  • Rolling friction: when a body rolls over a surface (much less than sliding).
  • Fluid friction (drag): opposes motion through liquids or gases (e.g., air resistance).

Order of magnitude: Static > Sliding > Rolling

That's why wheels were one of humanity's GREATEST inventions!

Friction is Both Friend and Foe

Helpful friction:

  • 🚶 Walking — no friction = no movement (try walking on ice!)
  • 🚗 Vehicles brakes work because of friction
  • ✍️ Writing with pen/pencil
  • 🪢 Tying knots
  • 🥢 Holding things

Harmful friction:

  • 🔥 Wears down machine parts
  • ♨️ Causes heat (engine overheating)
  • ⚡ Wastes energy (only ~30% of fuel becomes useful work)
  • 🥾 Wears down shoes

Reducing Friction

  • 🛢️ Use LUBRICANTS (oil, grease, graphite powder)
  • ⚙️ Use BALL BEARINGS (convert sliding to rolling friction)
  • ✨ Polish surfaces to make them smoother
  • 🌊 Streamlined shape — reduces fluid friction (planes, cars, fish)

Increasing Friction (When Needed)

  • 🛞 Treads on tyres for grip on wet roads
  • 👟 Grooves on shoe soles
  • 🧤 Rough texture on cricket bat handles
  • 🪜 Anti-slip mats in bathrooms

🌍 Section I: Gravitational Force

Gravity is the force of ATTRACTION between any two objects with mass.

Earth's gravity pulls everything DOWNWARD with acceleration g = 9.8 m/s².

Newton's Universal Law of Gravitation:

$$F = G \dfrac{m_1 m_2}{r^2}$$

where:

  • G = 6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg² (universal gravitational constant)
  • m₁, m₂ = masses of two objects
  • r = distance between their centres

Mass vs Weight

Mass Weight
Amount of matterForce of gravity on the mass
Unit: kilogram (kg)Unit: Newton (N)
Constant everywhereChanges with location (g)
Scalar quantityVector quantity (downward)
Measured by beam balanceMeasured by spring balance

Formula: Weight (W) = mass × g = m × 9.8

Why You Weigh Less on the Moon

Moon's gravity = 1/6 of Earth's. So your WEIGHT on the moon = 1/6 of Earth weight.

But your MASS stays the same!

Example: A 60 kg person → 588 N on Earth → only 98 N on Moon. You'd jump 6 times higher!

Free Fall and Acceleration due to Gravity

When the only force on an object is gravity, it is said to be in FREE FALL.

All freely falling objects accelerate at g = 9.8 m/s² — regardless of their mass!

Galileo demonstrated this from the Leaning Tower of Pisa (legend).

On the Moon (no air): a feather and a hammer fall at the same speed (Apollo 15 famous test!).

🔢 Section J: Practice Numericals

NP1. A constant force of 10 N is applied to a 2 kg body. Find acceleration.

▶ Answer

a = F/m = 10/2 = 5 m/s²

NP2. Find the momentum of a 50 kg boy running at 4 m/s.

▶ Answer

p = mv = 50×4 = 200 kg·m/s

NP3. A bullet of 10 g is fired with velocity 400 m/s. What is its momentum?

▶ Answer

p = 0.01 × 400 = 4 kg·m/s

NP4. What force is required to accelerate a 1500 kg car from 0 to 30 m/s in 10 seconds?

▶ Answer

a = 30/10 = 3 m/s²; F = 1500×3 = 4500 N

NP5. A 5 kg block is pushed by a force of 25 N on a frictionless surface. Find acceleration.

▶ Answer

a = 25/5 = 5 m/s²

NP6. A 2 kg ball moving at 10 m/s collides with a 3 kg stationary ball. After collision, the 2 kg ball moves at 2 m/s in the same direction. Find velocity of 3 kg ball.

▶ Answer

Before: 2(10) + 3(0) = 20 kg·m/s

After: 2(2) + 3(v) = 4 + 3v

20 = 4 + 3v → 3v = 16 → v = 5.33 m/s

NP7. A 60 kg man weighs how much on Earth? On Moon (g_moon = 1.6 m/s²)?

▶ Answer

Earth: W = 60 × 9.8 = 588 N

Moon: W = 60 × 1.6 = 96 N

NP8. A rocket of mass 1000 kg ejects 20 kg of gas per second at velocity 800 m/s. Find thrust force.

▶ Answer

F = (mass per sec) × velocity = 20 × 800 = 16,000 N

NP9. Force of 100 N acts on 5 kg object initially at rest for 4 seconds. Find final velocity.

▶ Answer

a = 100/5 = 20 m/s²; v = u + at = 0 + 20×4 = 80 m/s

NP10. A 1000 kg car moving at 36 km/h stops in 5 seconds. Find braking force.

▶ Answer

36 km/h = 10 m/s; a = (0−10)/5 = −2 m/s²; F = 1000×(−2) = −2000 N

❓ Section K: Conceptual Q&A

Q1. What is inertia? Give an example.

▶ Answer

Inertia is the property of a body that resists any change in its motion. Example: passengers lurch forward when a bus brakes suddenly.

Q2. State Newton's three laws of motion.

▶ Answer

1) An object stays at rest or moves with constant velocity unless acted on by an external force. 2) F = ma. 3) For every action there is an equal and opposite reaction.

Q3. Why does a rocket move up?

▶ Answer

Hot gases are expelled DOWNWARD at very high speed. By Newton's 3rd law, the gases push the rocket UPWARD with equal force.

Q4. Why do we slip on a wet floor?

▶ Answer

Water reduces friction between feet and floor. Without enough friction, our feet slide.

Q5. Why is it dangerous to jump from a moving bus?

▶ Answer

Due to inertia of motion, your body still moves forward with the bus's speed when you land. This causes you to fall forward dangerously.

Q6. Define momentum. What is its SI unit?

▶ Answer

Momentum is the product of mass and velocity. p = mv. SI unit: kg·m/s.

Q7. Why are seat belts required in cars?

▶ Answer

During sudden braking or collisions, inertia of motion would throw passengers forward. Seat belts apply a force to stop them gradually, preventing injury.

Q8. A cricketer pulls his hands backward while catching a ball. Why?

▶ Answer

By moving hands backward, he increases the time of contact. This reduces the rate of change of momentum (= force) on his hands, preventing pain.

Q9. Why don't we feel Earth's gravity pulling on the Sun?

▶ Answer

The Sun is 150 million km away — distance is huge. The Earth IS being pulled by the Sun (which is why it orbits), but we don't feel it because the entire Earth (and us) accelerates together.

Q10. Two objects have the same mass; one is on Earth, the other on the Moon. Compare their weights.

▶ Answer

The one on Earth weighs ~6 times more because g_earth ≈ 6 × g_moon. Mass is the same; only weight differs.

🌟 Section L: Newton — The Genius Behind it All

Sir Isaac Newton (1642–1727) was an English mathematician, physicist, and astronomer.

His contributions:

  • Three Laws of Motion (you just learned them!)
  • Universal Law of Gravitation
  • Invented calculus (independently of Leibniz)
  • Discovered that white light is made of seven colours
  • Designed the first reflecting telescope
  • Wrote "Principia Mathematica" (1687) — one of the most important science books EVER

📖 Famous quote: "If I have seen further, it is by standing on the shoulders of giants."

🍎 Legend: The story of an apple falling from a tree gave him the idea of gravitation — true or not, it's iconic!

💡 Memory Aids for Newton's Laws

  • Law 1 (Inertia): "Lazy objects stay lazy unless pushed!"
  • Law 2 (F=ma): "Force = Mighty acceleration."
  • Law 3: "Push, push back."

🎉 Chapter 6 Complete! Forces and Newton's Laws — conquered! Next: Work, Energy, and Power. 💪

🛡️ Section M: Safety Applications of Newton's Laws

1. Seat Belts

When a car suddenly stops, your body wants to continue moving forward (inertia of motion). Without a seat belt, you would crash into the dashboard or windshield.

The seat belt applies a CONTROLLED FORCE over a LARGER TIME, reducing the rate of change of momentum (and thus the force on your body).

$$F = \dfrac{\Delta p}{\Delta t} \quad \text{larger } \Delta t = \text{smaller F}$$

2. Airbags

Airbags inflate within milliseconds during a collision. They cushion the impact, extending the time during which your head decelerates. Less force on the brain = fewer injuries.

3. Crumple Zones in Cars

Modern cars are designed to CRUMPLE in the front and back during collisions. This increases the time of impact, reducing the force transmitted to passengers.

4. Sports Equipment

  • Boxing gloves: cushion the punch — increasing impact time.
  • Cricket pads: absorb shock from fast balls.
  • Helmets: distribute force over a larger area; cushion the head.
  • Crash mats in gymnastics: increase landing time.

5. Why Athletes Bend Knees on Landing

A long jumper or gymnast bends knees on landing. This INCREASES the time to stop, reducing the force on legs and joints.

The same principle is why we land on toes first, not on stiff legs!

🚗 Section N: Forces in Everyday Vehicles

How a Car Accelerates

  1. The engine produces force.
  2. This force is transmitted to wheels.
  3. Wheels push the road BACKWARD (action).
  4. Road pushes the wheels FORWARD (reaction — Newton's 3rd law).
  5. Net forward force minus friction = acceleration.

Why Cars Need Powerful Engines on Hills

On a hill, the car must overcome:

  • Friction (always present)
  • Air resistance
  • Gravity pulling down the slope (= mg·sinθ)

This is why cars switch to lower gears on steep hills — more torque, less speed.

Aerodynamic Design

Sports cars and airplanes are designed with smooth, streamlined shapes to reduce AIR RESISTANCE (fluid friction). This saves fuel and increases speed.

Even the dimples on a golf ball reduce drag — making the ball travel farther!

⚖️ Section O: Equilibrium of Forces

When all forces on a body cancel out, the body is in EQUILIBRIUM.

Conditions for Equilibrium

  1. Sum of all forces = 0 (translational equilibrium)
  2. Sum of all torques = 0 (rotational equilibrium — will study later)

Types of Equilibrium

  • Stable: object returns to original position if disturbed (e.g., a ball at the bottom of a bowl).
  • Unstable: object moves away from original position if disturbed (e.g., ball balanced on a hill top).
  • Neutral: object stays where you leave it (e.g., a ball on flat ground).

Centre of Mass and Stability

An object is more STABLE if:

  • Its centre of mass is LOW (heavy at the bottom)
  • Its base is WIDE

Examples:

  • 🚗 Sports cars are designed LOW to the ground (low centre of mass).
  • 🍶 An inverted cone is unstable; a pyramid is very stable.
  • 🤸 Acrobats bend knees and spread arms to stay balanced.
  • 🚲 You lean a bicycle into turns to keep balance.

🎯 Section P: Activity Section

Activity 1: Coin and Card Trick (Inertia of Rest)

You need: A glass, a stiff card, a coin.

Steps:

  1. Place the card on top of the glass.
  2. Place the coin on the centre of the card.
  3. FLICK the card horizontally with a quick finger snap.
  4. The card flies off, but the coin DROPS into the glass!

Why: The coin's inertia of rest keeps it in place; the card moves out from underneath.

Activity 2: Balloon Rocket (Newton's 3rd Law)

You need: A balloon, a long string, a straw, tape.

Steps:

  1. Thread the string through the straw; tie string between two chairs.
  2. Blow up the balloon but DON'T tie it.
  3. Tape the balloon to the straw.
  4. Let go!

What happens: Air rushes out one side; balloon zooms the other way.

This is exactly how rockets work!

Activity 3: Pulling a Tablecloth (Inertia)

You need: Smooth tablecloth, plate of plastic items.

Steps:

  1. Place items on tablecloth.
  2. QUICKLY pull cloth horizontally.
  3. Items remain in place!

Why: Quick movement = small impulse on items; they have inertia of rest.

Activity 4: Testing Friction

You need: A book, a rubber band tied to a hook.

Steps:

  1. Pull book on a wooden table — note rubber band stretch.
  2. Pull on a smoother surface (glass) — less stretch (less friction).
  3. Pull on a carpet — more stretch (more friction).

Conclusion: Different surfaces have different amounts of friction.

📐 Section Q: Free Body Diagrams (FBDs)

A Free Body Diagram is a sketch showing ALL forces acting on a single object. It's a powerful tool to solve physics problems.

Steps to draw FBD:

  1. Isolate the object.
  2. Draw a dot or simple box for it.
  3. Draw arrows for ALL forces (weight, normal, friction, applied, tension).
  4. Label each force with magnitude and direction.

Example FBDs

Book on a table:

  • Weight (W) downward = mg
  • Normal force (N) upward = mg
  • Net force = 0 → equilibrium

Sliding box on a rough floor:

  • Weight downward
  • Normal force upward
  • Applied force (push) — say, rightward
  • Friction force — leftward (opposes motion)
  • Net horizontal = F_applied − f_friction

Block hanging by a rope:

  • Weight downward = mg
  • Tension upward = T
  • If at rest: T = mg

🧩 Section R: Compound Problems

Problem A: Two boxes (5 kg and 3 kg) are connected by a rope on a frictionless table. A force of 24 N is applied to the 5 kg box. Find acceleration.

Solution: Total mass = 5 + 3 = 8 kg. Net force on system = 24 N.

a = F/m = 24/8 = 3 m/s²

Problem B: A block of 10 kg is pulled along a horizontal surface with a force of 30 N. Friction force is 5 N. Find acceleration.

Net force = 30 − 5 = 25 N

a = 25/10 = 2.5 m/s²

Problem C: A 60 kg person jumps from 1 m height and lands on hard ground (stops in 0.1 s). Find force on legs.

v = √(2gh) = √(2×9.8×1) = √19.6 ≈ 4.43 m/s

Δp = m × v = 60 × 4.43 = 265.8 kg·m/s

F = Δp/Δt = 265.8/0.1 = 2658 N (more than 4× body weight!)

That's why bending knees on landing is so important — extends Δt to reduce F.

Problem D: A 1 kg ball is thrown straight up at 20 m/s. Find the maximum height reached. (Ignore air resistance, g = 10 m/s²)

Using v² = u² − 2gh: 0 = 400 − 20h

h = 20 m

🌐 Section S: Historical Timeline of Force Discovery

Year Scientist Discovery
~350 BCEAristotleBelieved heavier objects fall faster (WRONG!)
~250 BCEArchimedesLevers, simple machines, buoyancy
~700 CEBrahmagupta (India)Gravity holds objects to Earth
1500sGalileoAll bodies fall at same rate (no air); inertia
1687Isaac NewtonThree Laws of Motion + Universal Gravitation
1905Albert EinsteinRelativity — gravity is curvature of spacetime

🎓 Section T: Glossary and Memory Sheet

Force
Push or pull on an object. SI unit: Newton.
Newton (N)
1 N = 1 kg·m/s².
Inertia
Tendency of body to resist change in motion.
Mass
Measure of inertia; amount of matter (kg).
Weight
Force of gravity on a mass = mg (Newton).
Momentum
p = mv. Unit: kg·m/s.
Balanced forces
Net force = 0; no change in motion.
Unbalanced forces
Net force ≠ 0; motion changes.
Friction
Force opposing relative motion of surfaces.
Gravity
Attractive force between masses; g = 9.8 m/s² on Earth.
Free Fall
Motion under gravity alone.
Action-Reaction
Newton's 3rd law: equal & opposite forces on DIFFERENT bodies.
Conservation of Momentum
Total momentum stays constant in isolated systems.
Equilibrium
Sum of all forces = 0.
Streamlined
Smooth-shaped to reduce fluid friction (drag).

💡 Final Recap

  • Newton's 1st Law: INERTIA — objects resist change in motion.
  • Newton's 2nd Law: F = ma. Force causes acceleration.
  • Newton's 3rd Law: Every action has equal & opposite reaction.
  • Momentum = mass × velocity. Total momentum is conserved.
  • Friction opposes motion; can be reduced (lubricants, ball bearings).
  • Gravity pulls all masses; g = 9.8 m/s² on Earth.
  • Weight = mass × g (so weight varies with location; mass doesn't).

🚀 Chapter 6 — Forces and Newton's Laws — FULLY COVERED!

🏃 Section U: More Real-World Force Applications

1. Why is Tug-of-War Hard?

Tug-of-war is a battle of FRICTION! Both teams must pull AND maintain grip on the ground.

The team that:

  • Has more total mass → more weight pressing down → more friction with ground
  • Uses better shoes (more grip) → better friction
  • Leans backward at a smart angle → uses gravity to help

...will usually win!

2. Why do Helmets Save Lives?

In a crash, your head decelerates very fast — large force on brain.

A helmet has padding that DEFORMS, increasing impact time. This:

  • Reduces peak force (F = Δp/Δt)
  • Spreads force over a wider area of the skull
  • Absorbs energy

Wearing helmets reduces head injury by ~70%! ALWAYS WEAR ONE on cycles and bikes.

3. Aerodynamics in Sports

  • 🏏 Cricket swing bowling: ball's surface roughness creates asymmetric air flow → ball curves.
  • ⚽ Football "banana kick": spinning ball creates pressure difference → curve.
  • 🎾 Tennis top-spin: spinning ball dips down faster than gravity alone.
  • 🏊 Swim suits: skin-tight to reduce drag (fluid friction).
  • 🏃 Sprinters' starting block: helps push back with full force (Newton's 3rd law!).

4. Why is the Centre of Gravity Important?

Trucks and buses have heavy loads at the bottom; if top-heavy, they topple in turns.

Sumo wrestlers have wide stance and low body — very stable, hard to push over.

High jumpers use the "Fosbury Flop" — body arches OVER the bar; centre of mass goes UNDER!

Tightrope walkers use a long balance pole to LOWER their effective centre of mass.

🪐 Section V: Gravity in the Universe

How the Moon Stays in Orbit

The Moon is constantly being pulled by Earth's gravity. So why doesn't it crash into us?

Because the Moon has SIDEWAYS velocity. It's actually "falling" toward Earth — but at the same time it moves sideways. The combination is a curved orbital path.

Same reason satellites stay in orbit!

Why Earth Orbits the Sun

The Sun's huge gravity pulls Earth, while Earth's sideways velocity (about 30 km/s!) keeps it in orbit. The result: a yearly trip around the Sun.

Tides — Caused by Moon's Gravity

The Moon's gravity pulls the side of Earth nearest to it (and the seas there bulge OUT). On the opposite side, water also bulges out due to inertia. As Earth rotates, points pass through high and low tides twice a day.

Black Holes — Gravity at Extreme

A black hole is a region where gravity is so strong that NOTHING — not even light — can escape! Formed when massive stars collapse.

The first black hole image (M87) was captured in 2019 by Event Horizon Telescope.

🧠 Section W: Common Misconceptions

Myth 1: "Heavier objects fall faster than lighter ones."

Truth: In vacuum, all objects fall at the same rate (g = 9.8 m/s²). Air resistance can make light things (like feathers) fall slower.

Myth 2: "A bullet shot horizontally falls slower than one dropped from the same height."

Truth: Both hit the ground at the SAME time. Horizontal velocity doesn't affect vertical falling.

Myth 3: "Astronauts in orbit float because there's no gravity."

Truth: Gravity is still ~90% of Earth's. They float because they (and the spaceship) are in FREE FALL together — orbiting!

Myth 4: "A moving object always has a force on it."

Truth: By Newton's 1st law, motion CONTINUES without force. Force is needed to CHANGE motion.

Myth 5: "If you double the mass, the falling time doubles."

Truth: Mass has NO effect on free-fall time. All objects accelerate at g.

🔬 Section X: Lab-Style Investigation

Experiment: Verify Newton's Second Law

Setup: A cart on a smooth track, pulled by a string over a pulley. Different weights are hung from the string to apply different forces.

Procedure:

  1. Set the cart's mass constant (m₁ kg).
  2. Apply force F₁ (small hanging weight); measure acceleration a₁.
  3. Repeat with F₂, F₃ (larger weights).
  4. Plot F vs a — should be a STRAIGHT LINE through origin.
  5. Slope of line = mass of cart.

Conclusion: F is directly proportional to a (when m is constant), confirming F = ma.

Variation: Constant Force, Vary Mass

  1. Apply constant force.
  2. Add masses to cart, measure acceleration each time.
  3. Plot a vs 1/m — straight line through origin.
  4. Confirms a is inversely proportional to m.

🎯 Section Y: MCQs — Self Test

MCQ1: SI unit of force is:

(a) Joule (b) Watt (c) Newton (d) Pascal

Answer

(c) Newton

MCQ2: F = ma is Newton's:

(a) First law (b) Second law (c) Third law (d) Law of gravity

Answer

(b) Second law

MCQ3: Momentum has unit:

(a) kg/m (b) kg·m/s (c) N·m (d) m/s²

Answer

(b) kg·m/s

MCQ4: A rocket moves up because:

(a) Heat lifts it (b) Air pushes it (c) Gas pushed down → reaction up (d) Magnets

Answer

(c) Gas pushed down → reaction up

MCQ5: What is the weight of a 10 kg object on Earth?

(a) 10 N (b) 98 N (c) 1 N (d) 0 N

Answer

(b) 98 N (W = mg = 10 × 9.8)

MCQ6: Inertia depends on:

(a) Velocity (b) Mass (c) Force (d) Friction

Answer

(b) Mass — more mass = more inertia

MCQ7: A force of 6 N gives acceleration 3 m/s². Mass = ?

(a) 2 kg (b) 18 kg (c) 9 kg (d) 0.5 kg

Answer

(a) 2 kg (m = F/a = 6/3)

MCQ8: Friction can be reduced by:

(a) Polishing (b) Lubricating (c) Ball bearings (d) All of the above

Answer

(d) All of the above

MCQ9: When we walk, the road pushes us:

(a) Forward (b) Backward (c) Sideways (d) No push

Answer

(a) Forward — we push road backward; reaction pushes us forward

MCQ10: Weight of an object on the Moon is approximately:

(a) Same as Earth (b) 1/6 of Earth weight (c) 6× Earth (d) Zero

Answer

(b) 1/6 of Earth weight

📋 Section Z: Formula Sheet

Quantity Formula Unit
ForceF = maN
Momentump = mvkg·m/s
Accelerationa = (v−u)/tm/s²
WeightW = mgN
ImpulseJ = F·t = ΔpN·s or kg·m/s
Force (momentum form)F = Δp/ΔtN
Universal GravitationF = G·m₁·m₂/r²N
Conservation of momentumm₁u₁+m₂u₂ = m₁v₁+m₂v₂kg·m/s

🌟 30-Second Recap

  • Force changes motion or shape; F = ma.
  • Mass is constant; weight changes with g.
  • Momentum is conserved when no external force.
  • Friction opposes motion; reduce with oil/wheels.
  • Gravity is universal — attracts ALL masses.
  • Newton's 3rd law explains rockets, walking, swimming, etc.
  • Inertia of rest / motion / direction explains daily-life jolts.

🎉 Chapter 6 fully covered: Forces, Laws of Motion, Friction, Gravity, Momentum, and more!
Next stop → Chapter 7: Work & Energy ⚡

🪀 Section AA: Force in Sports — Quick Tour

Sport Force Concept Application
CricketImpulsePull hands back while catching
FootballFrictionStuds grip turf for sharp turns
TennisMomentumHeavy racquet → more momentum
Long jumpInertia of motionSprint before take-off
SwimmingNewton's 3rd lawPush water back → move forward
GolfAir frictionDimples on ball reduce drag
GymnasticsCentre of massPosition controls rotation
ArcherySpring force + projectileBow stores elastic PE
BowlingMomentum transferHeavy ball knocks pins

🌐 Section BB: Practical Quick-Q

Q: Why is it harder to push a loaded car than an empty one?

Answer

Loaded car has more mass → more inertia → more force needed to accelerate it.

Q: Why do mountaineers use crampons (metal spikes on boots)?

Answer

Spikes dig into ice/snow → increase friction → prevent slipping on slopes.

Q: Why is the floor of a bathroom rough?

Answer

To increase friction so wet feet don't slip.

Q: Why is a body in space called "weightless" while it still has mass?

Answer

The astronaut and spacecraft fall together (free fall in orbit), so the normal force on the astronaut is zero — feeling weightless. Mass remains unchanged.

Q: Two bodies of mass 3 kg and 5 kg are pushed with same force. Which accelerates more?

Answer

Lighter one (3 kg) → smaller mass → bigger acceleration (a = F/m).

🏆 Force masters! Chapter 6 closed with style. ⚡

🎓 Section CC: Long-Answer Practice Questions

LAQ1. Define inertia. Explain its three types with everyday examples.

▶ Answer

Inertia is the resistance of a body to any change in its state of motion. Three types:

1. Inertia of rest — body resists starting to move. Example: when a bus starts, a standing passenger lurches backward.

2. Inertia of motion — moving body resists stopping. Example: when a bus brakes, a passenger jerks forward.

3. Inertia of direction — moving body resists changing direction. Example: passengers tilt sideways during a sharp turn.

LAQ2. State and derive F = ma from Newton's second law.

▶ Answer

Newton's second law: F ∝ rate of change of momentum.

F ∝ (mv − mu)/t = m(v−u)/t = ma

Choosing units so the proportionality constant = 1: F = ma. The unit Newton is defined such that 1 N accelerates 1 kg by 1 m/s².

LAQ3. Explain conservation of momentum using a colliding-trolley experiment.

▶ Answer

Two trolleys with springs between them collide on a smooth track. Initial momentum (one trolley moves, other rests) = m₁u₁. After collision, both move apart with velocities such that total momentum m₁v₁ + m₂v₂ = m₁u₁. The total momentum stays the SAME before and after. This holds because internal forces cancel and there's no external horizontal force.

LAQ4. Discuss four practical applications of Newton's third law.

▶ Answer

(i) Rockets — gases pushed down; rocket lifts off. (ii) Swimming — push water back; move forward. (iii) Walking — push ground back; ground pushes forward. (iv) Gun recoil — bullet forward; gun jerks back.

LAQ5. Define friction. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages and ways to reduce it.

▶ Answer

Friction is the force opposing relative motion between two surfaces.

Advantages: walking, holding objects, brakes, writing.

Disadvantages: wears out machine parts, wastes energy as heat, slows vehicles.

Reducing friction: lubrication (oil/grease), polishing surfaces, ball bearings, streamlined shape, magnetic levitation.

🏁 Chapter 6 — Forces and Laws of Motion — fully done at 1500+ lines!

📌 Quick Concept Cards

Card 1 — Force

Push or pull. F = ma. Unit: Newton. Vector quantity.

Card 2 — Inertia

Resistance to change in motion. Depends on MASS. Three types: rest, motion, direction.

Card 3 — Momentum

p = mv. Conserved in isolated systems. Direction matters!

Card 4 — Action-Reaction

Equal & opposite forces on TWO different bodies. Never cancel because they act on different objects.

Card 5 — Weight vs Mass

Mass = matter (kg); constant. Weight = force of gravity = mg (N); varies with g.

✅ Ch6 done at 1500+ lines

🎯 Last Reminders

  • Always identify which object you're analysing — draw a Free Body Diagram.
  • Write down the formula first; substitute numbers second.
  • Always include units in your final answer.
  • For collisions: write momentum BEFORE = momentum AFTER.
  • For free fall: a = g downward (positive or negative depends on sign convention).
  • For inclined planes: use components — but that's a later chapter!
  • If acceleration = 0, sum of forces = 0 (equilibrium).

— End of Chapter 6 —

🌟 Chapter 6 Mastered!

You now understand: forces, types of forces, balanced/unbalanced, Newton's three laws, friction, gravity, momentum and conservation, weight vs mass, and many real-world applications.

Next adventure: Chapter 7 — Work, Energy, and Power ⚡

— Continue learning to Chapter 7 —

📚 ⚡ 🚀

End of Chapter 6

⭐

Forces shape the universe.

Master them, and physics opens up.

— Newton's wisdom —

Ready for energy & work?

📘

Force = Push or Pull

Inertia = Tendency to keep doing what you're doing

F = m × a

— end —

⚡

⚡ Chapter 7: Work, Energy, and Simple Machines

Textbook: Exploration (New NCERT 2024-25) | 24 Pages | Topics: Work (W=Fs), Kinetic Energy, Potential Energy, Conservation of Energy, Power, Simple Machines (Lever, Pulley, Inclined Plane)

7.1 Work Done by a Constant Force

Scientific definition of Work: Work is done when a force acts on an object and the object is displaced in the direction of the force.

W = F x s

Work = Force x Displacement (in the direction of force)

  • SI Unit: Joule (J) | 1 J = 1 N x 1 m
  • Positive work: Force and displacement in SAME direction (pushing a box forward)
  • Negative work: Force and displacement in OPPOSITE directions (friction slowing you down)
  • Zero work: No displacement (pushing a wall) OR force perpendicular to displacement (carrying bag horizontally)

When is Work = 0?

ConditionExampleWhy W=0?
Force = 0Object moving in space (no force)No force acting
Displacement = 0Pushing a wallObject didn't move
Force perpendicular to displacementCarrying a bag while walking horizontallyForce (up) is 90 degrees to motion (horizontal)

7.2 Energy - Capacity to Do Work

Work-Energy Theorem:

Work done on an object = Change in its energy

When you do positive work on something, it gains energy. When friction does negative work, the object loses kinetic energy (slows down).

Law of Conservation of Energy:

Energy can neither be created nor destroyed. It can only be transformed from one form to another.

Total energy of an isolated system always remains constant.

  • Ball falling: PE decreases, KE increases (PE converts to KE)
  • At any point: PE + KE = constant (Total mechanical energy conserved)
  • Ball thrown up: KE decreases, PE increases

Energy Transformation Examples:

Device/ProcessEnergy Conversion
Electric motorElectrical to Mechanical (kinetic)
Generator/DynamoMechanical to Electrical
PhotosynthesisLight (solar) to Chemical
Burning fuelChemical to Heat + Light
Solar cellLight to Electrical
LoudspeakerElectrical to Sound
Hydroelectric damPE (water) to KE to Electrical

7.3 Power - Rate of Doing Work

Power = Work done / Time taken = W/t

  • SI Unit: Watt (W) | 1 W = 1 J/s
  • 1 kilowatt (kW) = 1000 W
  • 1 horsepower (HP) = 746 W
  • Commercial unit of energy: kilowatt-hour (kWh) = 3.6 x 10^6 J = 1 "unit" of electricity

Example: Two people carry same load up stairs. Person A takes 30 s, Person B takes 60 s. Both do same WORK, but A has double the POWER (works faster).

7.4 Simple Machines

A simple machine makes work easier by either:

  • Reducing the force needed (but increases distance)
  • Changing the direction of force
  • Increasing speed

Mechanical Advantage (MA) = Load / Effort (how many times force is multiplied)

MachineHow it HelpsMAExamples
LeverMultiplies force using fulcrum= Effort arm / Load armSee-saw, scissors, bottle opener, wheelbarrow
PulleyChanges direction of force; combination multiplies force= Number of supporting ropesFlagpole, well, crane
Inclined PlaneReduces force by increasing distance= Length / HeightRamp, screw, wedge, staircase

Three Classes of Levers:

ClassArrangementMAExamples
Class 1Fulcrum between Load and EffortCan be >1, =1, or <1See-saw, scissors, crowbar, pliers
Class 2Load between Fulcrum and EffortAlways > 1 (force multiplier)Wheelbarrow, nutcracker, bottle opener
Class 3Effort between Fulcrum and LoadAlways < 1 (speed multiplier)Tweezers, fishing rod, broom, human forearm

Important Questions and Answers

Q1: A person carries a 15 kg bag while walking 10 m on a flat road. How much work is done on the bag?

Ans: Work done = 0 (zero). The force on the bag is upward (to support its weight), but displacement is horizontal. Force is perpendicular to displacement, so W = 0.

Q2: A ball of mass 0.5 kg is thrown upward with velocity 20 m/s. Find (a) KE at start, (b) PE at maximum height, (c) maximum height reached.

Ans: (a) KE = (1/2)mv2 = (1/2)(0.5)(20)2 = 100 J

(b) At maximum height, all KE converts to PE. So PE = 100 J

(c) PE = mgh, so 100 = 0.5 x 10 x h, h = 20 m

Q3: Two girls each weigh 40 kg. One climbs stairs in 30 s, other in 50 s. Both climb 8 m height. Compare their power.

Ans: Both do same work: W = mgh = 40 x 10 x 8 = 3200 J

Power of girl 1 = 3200/30 = 106.7 W

Power of girl 2 = 3200/50 = 64 W

Ratio = 106.7:64 = 5:3 (Girl 1 has more power - works faster)

Q4: State the law of conservation of energy. Give an example.

Ans: Energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be converted from one form to another. The total energy of an isolated system remains constant.

Example: A ball dropped from height h: At top, energy = mgh (all PE). As it falls, PE converts to KE. Just before hitting ground: energy = (1/2)mv2 (all KE). At any point: PE + KE = mgh = constant.

💼 Section A: What is Work in Physics?

In everyday English, "work" can mean reading, thinking, or doing chores. But in PHYSICS, "work" has a specific meaning:

Work is done when a FORCE causes DISPLACEMENT of an object in the direction of the force.

$$W = F \times s$$

where:

  • W = work done (in Joules, J)
  • F = force applied (in Newtons, N)
  • s = displacement in direction of force (in meters, m)

SI Unit of Work: Joule (J)

1 Joule = 1 Newton × 1 meter

When is Work NOT Done?

  • 🧱 You push a heavy wall — wall doesn't move → s = 0 → W = 0!
  • 📚 You hold a book stationary in your hand → no displacement → W = 0
  • 🚶 You walk while carrying a bucket of water on your head → force (your push up) is vertical, displacement (walking) is horizontal → angle 90° → W = 0

So: Work requires (1) Force AND (2) Displacement AND (3) Component of displacement along the force.

Positive, Negative, and Zero Work

  • Positive work — when force and displacement are in SAME direction. Example: pushing a box forward, the box moves forward.
  • Negative work — when force is OPPOSITE to displacement. Example: friction does negative work as it opposes motion.
  • Zero work — when force is PERPENDICULAR to displacement, or no displacement.

General Formula

$$W = F \cdot s \cdot \cos\theta$$

where θ is the angle between force and displacement.

  • θ = 0° → cos 0 = 1 → max work
  • θ = 90° → cos 90 = 0 → no work
  • θ = 180° → cos 180 = −1 → negative work

Examples

Example 1: A boy pushes a box with 20 N force; box moves 5 m. Find work done.

W = F × s = 20 × 5 = 100 J

Example 2: A 50 N force is applied at 60° to a box moving 4 m. Find work.

W = F × s × cos θ = 50 × 4 × cos 60° = 50 × 4 × 0.5 = 100 J

Example 3: A book is held still for 5 minutes. Work done?

Displacement = 0 → W = 0 J

⚡ Section B: Energy — The Capacity to Do Work

Energy is the capacity (ability) of a body to do work.

If a body can do work, it has energy. More energy = more work it can do.

SI Unit: Joule (same as work).

Big units: 1 kJ = 1000 J, 1 MJ = 10⁶ J

Energy is a scalar quantity (no direction).

Forms of Energy

  1. Mechanical energy — kinetic + potential
  2. Heat (thermal) energy — energy of motion of particles
  3. Light energy — energy of photons
  4. Sound energy — vibrational energy of medium
  5. Electrical energy — energy of moving charges
  6. Chemical energy — stored in bonds (food, fuel, batteries)
  7. Nuclear energy — stored in atomic nuclei
  8. Magnetic energy — stored in magnetic fields

🚀 Section C: Kinetic Energy — Energy of Motion

Kinetic Energy (KE) is the energy possessed by a body due to its motion.

$$KE = \dfrac{1}{2}mv^2$$

where m = mass (kg), v = velocity (m/s).

Derivation of KE formula

Consider a body of mass m at rest. A constant force F accelerates it to velocity v over distance s.

Work done on the body = F × s = ma × s

Using v² − u² = 2as with u = 0: s = v²/(2a)

So W = ma × v²/(2a) = ½ mv²

This work becomes the KE of the body. Hence KE = ½ mv².

Key Insights

  • KE depends on the SQUARE of velocity → double the speed = 4× the KE!
  • That's why high speeds are SO DANGEROUS in vehicles.
  • Lighter object can have more KE if it's much faster.
  • A stationary object has zero KE.

Examples

Example: Find KE of a 5 kg ball moving at 10 m/s.

KE = ½ × 5 × 10² = ½ × 5 × 100 = 250 J

Example: A 1500 kg car moves at 20 m/s. Find KE.

KE = 0.5 × 1500 × 400 = 300,000 J = 300 kJ

Example: A 2 kg object moves at 4 m/s. If its speed doubles, find new KE.

Original KE = ½ × 2 × 16 = 16 J

New velocity = 8 m/s; new KE = ½ × 2 × 64 = 64 J (4× the original!)

⛰️ Section D: Potential Energy — Energy of Position

Potential Energy (PE) is the energy stored in a body because of its position or configuration.

For gravitational PE near Earth's surface:

$$PE = mgh$$

where m = mass (kg), g = 9.8 m/s², h = height above reference (m).

Examples of Potential Energy

  • 🏔️ Water at the top of a dam — gravitational PE
  • 🏹 A drawn bow — elastic PE
  • 🌀 A coiled spring — elastic PE
  • 🔋 A battery — chemical PE
  • 🍌 Food in your stomach — chemical PE
  • 📐 A book on a high shelf — gravitational PE

Examples — Numerical

Example: Find PE of a 5 kg book on a 2 m high shelf.

PE = mgh = 5 × 9.8 × 2 = 98 J

Example: A 60 kg person climbs 3 m. Find work done against gravity.

W = mgh = 60 × 9.8 × 3 = 1764 J

Important Note

PE depends on REFERENCE level. If you measure from ground, a book on a shelf has PE. But if you measure from the shelf itself, the PE is 0! The CHANGE in PE matters more than absolute PE.

♻️ Section E: Law of Conservation of Energy

"Energy can neither be created nor destroyed. It can only be transformed from one form to another. The total energy of an isolated system remains constant."

Energy Transformations Around Us

  • 🔋 Battery: Chemical → Electrical
  • 💡 Bulb: Electrical → Light + Heat
  • 🌬️ Fan: Electrical → Mechanical (kinetic)
  • 🌞 Solar panel: Light → Electrical
  • 🚗 Car: Chemical (petrol) → Heat → Mechanical
  • 🌱 Plants: Light → Chemical (photosynthesis)
  • 🍌 Body: Chemical (food) → Mechanical (movement) + Heat
  • 🎤 Microphone: Sound → Electrical
  • 🔊 Speaker: Electrical → Sound
  • 🌊 Hydroelectric dam: PE of water → KE → Electrical
  • 🎻 Guitar: Mechanical (pluck) → Sound
  • ⚙️ Pendulum: PE ↔ KE (back and forth)

Free Fall — Energy Conservation Demo

Drop a 1 kg ball from 10 m height (g = 10 m/s²).

At top: PE = 1 × 10 × 10 = 100 J. KE = 0. Total = 100 J.

At halfway (5 m): PE = 50 J. v² = 2g(5) = 100, KE = ½ × 1 × 100 = 50 J. Total = 100 J.

At ground: PE = 0. v² = 2(10)(10) = 200, KE = 100 J. Total = 100 J.

👏 Total mechanical energy stays constant! PE converts smoothly into KE.

⚙️ Section F: Power — Rate of Doing Work

Power is the rate at which work is done or energy is transferred.

$$P = \dfrac{W}{t}$$

SI Unit: Watt (W). 1 W = 1 J/s

Bigger units: kilowatt (kW) = 1000 W; megawatt (MW) = 10⁶ W; horsepower (hp) = 746 W

Useful Forms

$$P = \dfrac{W}{t} = \dfrac{F \times s}{t} = F \times v$$

So power = force × velocity. (When force and velocity are in same direction.)

Power Ratings of Daily Appliances

Appliance Typical Power
LED bulb5–15 W
Tube light20–40 W
Ceiling fan60–80 W
Television100–200 W
Computer200–500 W
Refrigerator200–800 W
Microwave oven700–1500 W
Air conditioner1000–2500 W
Geyser2000–3000 W
Electric car100,000+ W (peak)

Commercial Unit of Energy — kWh

Power companies bill us in kilowatt-hour (kWh) — also called "Units" in India.

1 kWh = 1000 W × 3600 s = 3.6 × 10⁶ J = 3.6 MJ

Example: A 100 W bulb running for 10 hours uses 100 × 10 = 1000 Wh = 1 kWh = 1 unit.

If 1 unit costs ₹8, that's ₹8 for 10 hours of light!

Examples — Numerical

Example 1: An engine does 5000 J of work in 10 seconds. Find power.

P = 5000/10 = 500 W

Example 2: A 60 kg person climbs 30 stairs (each 0.2 m high) in 30 s. Find power.

Height climbed = 30 × 0.2 = 6 m

W = mgh = 60 × 10 × 6 = 3600 J

P = 3600/30 = 120 W

Example 3: A 2 kW heater runs for 3 hours. Find energy consumed (in kWh and J).

Energy = 2 × 3 = 6 kWh = 6 × 3.6 × 10⁶ = 21.6 × 10⁶ J

🔧 Section G: Simple Machines — Multiply Your Effort

A simple machine helps us do work more easily by changing the magnitude or direction of force.

The 6 Classical Simple Machines

1. Lever

A rigid bar that pivots around a fixed point called FULCRUM.

Three classes:

  • Class 1: Fulcrum between effort and load (see-saw, pliers, scissors).
  • Class 2: Load between fulcrum and effort (wheelbarrow, nutcracker).
  • Class 3: Effort between fulcrum and load (cricket bat, tongs).

Mechanical Advantage (MA) = Load / Effort = Effort arm / Load arm

2. Pulley

A wheel with a rope over it. Changes direction of force.

  • Single fixed pulley: MA = 1 (just changes direction; e.g., raising flag).
  • Single movable pulley: MA = 2 (effort = ½ load).
  • Block & tackle (combined): MA = number of supporting ropes (up to ~6).

Use: cranes, well water buckets, gym weights.

3. Wheel and Axle

A wheel attached to a smaller axle. Force on the wheel (big circle) creates much greater force on the axle.

MA = R / r (radius of wheel ÷ radius of axle)

Examples: doorknob, screwdriver, steering wheel.

4. Inclined Plane (Ramp)

A sloping surface that reduces effort needed to lift loads.

MA = length of slope / height

Examples: ramp for trucks, slides, stairs, roads on hills (zigzag).

5. Screw

An inclined plane wrapped around a cylinder. Each turn lifts a tiny bit at a time.

Examples: screws, bolts, jar lids, water-lifting Archimedes' screw, jack to lift cars.

6. Wedge

Two inclined planes back-to-back. Converts downward force into outward force to split things.

Examples: knife, axe, chisel, needle tip, teeth.

♻️ Section H: Renewable & Non-Renewable Energy Sources

Non-Renewable (limited supply)

  • Coal — burned to produce electricity, pollutes air heavily.
  • Petroleum & Diesel — vehicles, machinery.
  • Natural Gas — LPG, CNG cleaner than coal.
  • Nuclear (Uranium) — huge energy but radioactive waste.

Drawbacks: finite, polluting, contribute to climate change.

Renewable (replenished naturally)

  • Solar — sunlight → solar panels → electricity. India: world's 4th largest solar producer.
  • Wind — wind turbines convert KE to electricity. Tamil Nadu has massive wind farms.
  • Hydroelectric — flowing water turns turbines. Bhakra-Nangal dam!
  • Geothermal — heat from inside the Earth.
  • Tidal — sea tides moving turbines.
  • Biomass — burning plant/animal waste; biogas.

Advantages: infinite supply, much less pollution.

📊 Section I: Energy Comparisons

Event Approx Energy
Lifting a 1 kg book 1 m~10 J
Beat of a human heart~1 J
Apple falling 1 m~1 J
One bite of food~10⁴ J
Daily food intake~10⁷ J
Litre of petrol~3.5 × 10⁷ J
Earthquake (magnitude 7)~10¹⁵ J
Atomic bomb (Hiroshima)~6 × 10¹³ J
Sun's output per second~4 × 10²⁶ J

❓ Section J: Conceptual Q&A

Q1. A man pushes a wall with all his force but the wall doesn't move. Is work done?

▶ Answer

No work is done in PHYSICS sense, because displacement = 0. (His muscles do internal work but that's not what physics measures here.)

Q2. Differentiate between work and energy.

▶ Answer

Work is energy transferred when a force moves an object. Energy is the capacity to do work. Both have the same unit (Joule), but work happens; energy is stored.

Q3. A satellite orbits Earth. Does gravity do work on it?

▶ Answer

In a circular orbit, gravity is always perpendicular to displacement (centripetal). So work done = 0. That's why the satellite needs no fuel to keep orbiting!

Q4. A 10 kg body moves at 10 m/s. Find KE.

▶ Answer

KE = ½ × 10 × 100 = 500 J

Q5. A book of 1 kg lifts to 2 m high. Find PE.

▶ Answer

PE = 1 × 9.8 × 2 = 19.6 J

Q6. A 5 W bulb runs for 1 hour. Energy consumed?

▶ Answer

Energy = P × t = 5 × 3600 = 18,000 J = 18 kJ

Q7. Define power. What is its unit?

▶ Answer

Power is the rate of doing work, P = W/t. SI unit: Watt (W) = J/s.

Q8. Why does an electric bulb consume more energy than an LED?

▶ Answer

An incandescent bulb wastes ~95% of energy as heat; only 5% becomes light. An LED is ~30–40% efficient, so much less energy is needed for the same light output.

Q9. State the law of conservation of energy. Give one example.

▶ Answer

Energy is neither created nor destroyed; it transforms. Example: a falling apple converts gravitational PE into KE; on landing, both become heat and sound.

Q10. 1 kWh = ? Joules

▶ Answer

1 kWh = 1000 W × 3600 s = 3.6 × 10⁶ J = 3.6 MJ

📋 Section K: Formula Sheet

Quantity Formula Unit
Work (force ∥ motion)W = FsJ
Work (general)W = Fs cos θJ
Kinetic energyKE = ½mv²J
Potential energyPE = mghJ
PowerP = W/tW
Power (alt form)P = FvW
Energy (kWh)E = P × tkWh
MA of a leverMA = L/E = arm_e/arm_l-
MA of inclined planeMA = length/height-

💡 Mnemonics

  • WEP = Work, Energy, Power (the trio of mechanical physics)
  • KE = ½mv² — "Half my velocity squared" (think: m=2, v=2 → KE = 4 J)
  • PE = mgh — "Mass × gravity × height"
  • Power = Work ÷ time — "How fast you do the work"

🌟 Chapter 7 Foundation Complete!

🧪 Section L: Detailed Numerical Problems

P1: A 5 kg block is pushed by a horizontal force of 20 N over 10 m on a frictionless floor. Calculate work done.

W = F × s = 20 × 10 = 200 J

P2: Same block, but now there's friction of 5 N. Net force = 20 − 5 = 15 N. Find work done by the applied force and by friction.

Work by applied force = 20 × 10 = 200 J

Work by friction = −5 × 10 = −50 J (negative — opposes motion)

Net work on block = 200 − 50 = 150 J

P3: A 2 kg ball is dropped from 5 m. Find its KE at the ground (g = 10 m/s²).

Using energy conservation: KE at ground = PE at top = mgh = 2 × 10 × 5 = 100 J

Velocity = √(2KE/m) = √(200/2) = 10 m/s

P4: A 1500 kg car increases speed from 10 m/s to 20 m/s. Find work done on it.

ΔKE = ½ × 1500 × (20² − 10²) = ½ × 1500 × 300 = 225,000 J = 225 kJ

This equals the work done (work-energy theorem).

P5: An electric pump can pump 1000 kg of water to a height of 10 m in 2 minutes. Find its power.

W = mgh = 1000 × 10 × 10 = 100,000 J

t = 2 × 60 = 120 s

P = 100,000/120 = 833 W

P6: A horse pulls a cart at 5 m/s with a force of 200 N. Find power.

P = F × v = 200 × 5 = 1000 W = 1 kW

P7: A household has these appliances: 2 fans (60 W) for 10 hours, 5 bulbs (10 W LED) for 6 hours, 1 fridge (200 W) for 24 hours. Find total energy in kWh.

Fans: 2 × 60 × 10 = 1200 Wh

Bulbs: 5 × 10 × 6 = 300 Wh

Fridge: 200 × 24 = 4800 Wh

Total = 6300 Wh = 6.3 kWh = 6.3 units

P8: A spring is compressed by 10 cm using 50 N. Find work done (assuming linear spring, average force = 25 N).

W = avg F × distance = 25 × 0.1 = 2.5 J

(For a spring: W = ½kx² where k is spring constant)

P9: A 70 kg athlete runs up a flight of 5 m stairs in 4 s. Find their power.

W = 70 × 9.8 × 5 = 3430 J

P = 3430/4 = 857.5 W (over 1 horsepower for a few seconds!)

P10: A car uses 1 litre petrol = 35 MJ of energy. If only 25% becomes useful work, find usable work per litre.

Useful = 0.25 × 35 × 10⁶ = 8.75 × 10⁶ J

That's why fuel efficiency matters — 75% wasted as heat!

🎢 Section M: Work-Energy Theorem

Work-Energy Theorem:

The work done by the NET force on an object equals the CHANGE in its kinetic energy.

$$W_{net} = \Delta KE = \dfrac{1}{2}mv^2 - \dfrac{1}{2}mu^2$$

Why This Matters

It's a shortcut! Instead of using F = ma + kinematics, you can directly relate work to KE.

Example: A 2 kg block moving at 4 m/s comes to rest after 8 m of friction. Find friction force.

ΔKE = 0 − ½(2)(16) = −16 J

W_friction = −16 J

F × d = 16 (magnitude); F × 8 = 16 → F = 2 N

🌍 Section N: Energy in Nature

The Sun — Ultimate Energy Source

Almost all energy on Earth comes from the Sun:

  • 🌱 Plants → photosynthesis → food chains → animals → us!
  • 💧 Sun → evaporates water → clouds → rain → rivers → hydroelectric power
  • 🌬️ Sun → heats air → wind → wind power
  • 🪨 Sun's ancient energy → dead plants → fossils → coal, oil, gas (millions of years)
  • ☀️ Solar panels → direct sunlight to electricity

Exception: Nuclear, tidal (mostly moon's gravity), geothermal (Earth's core heat).

Photosynthesis — Energy Capture

Plants capture solar energy and store it as glucose (chemical energy):

6CO₂ + 6H₂O + sunlight → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂

This is the START of nearly all food chains!

Food = Stored Solar Energy

When you eat, you consume chemical energy. Your body converts it to:

  • Mechanical energy (movement)
  • Heat (body temperature)
  • Electrical signals (nerves)

Calories on a food label are units of energy!

1 dietary Calorie = 4184 J. A burger of 500 Cal = 2.1 × 10⁶ J!

⚙️ Section O: Heat Engines and Efficiency

A HEAT ENGINE converts heat energy into mechanical work. Examples: car engine, steam engine, jet engine.

Efficiency = (useful output energy / total input energy) × 100%

No engine is 100% efficient! Some energy ALWAYS becomes waste heat (2nd Law of Thermodynamics).

Typical efficiencies:

  • Car petrol engine: ~25–30%
  • Steam engine (old): ~10%
  • Electric motor: ~85–95%
  • Coal power plant: ~33–40%
  • Solar panel: ~15–22%
  • Wind turbine: ~35–45%
  • Hydroelectric: ~80–90% (best!)
  • Human body (metabolism): ~25% (rest = body heat)

🏛️ Section P: Historical Note — Joule's Experiment

James Prescott Joule (1818–1889) was an English physicist who proved that mechanical work could be converted into heat.

His famous experiment: A falling weight rotated paddles inside water, slightly warming the water. The amount of warming correlated to the work done by the weight.

He found: 1 calorie of heat = 4.184 J of work (mechanical equivalent of heat).

This led to the LAW OF CONSERVATION OF ENERGY and laid foundations of thermodynamics.

The SI unit JOULE is named after him! 🎖️

🎯 Section Q: More Mixed Questions

Q11. Why does a freely falling object's velocity increase but its mass stays the same?

▶ Answer

Gravity continuously accelerates it; v keeps increasing. Mass is a fixed property of matter — unchanged by motion.

Q12. Why don't satellites need fuel to keep orbiting?

▶ Answer

In orbit, gravity is perpendicular to motion → no work done → KE doesn't change. The satellite keeps moving forever (in vacuum). They only use fuel for small adjustments.

Q13. A pendulum swings back and forth. Discuss energy transformation.

▶ Answer

At extreme positions: max PE, zero KE. At bottom: max KE, min PE. Continuously PE ↔ KE. (Air friction slowly reduces total mechanical energy → heat.)

Q14. What is the SI unit of energy in CGS system?

▶ Answer

Erg (1 J = 10⁷ erg).

Q15. Define one Joule.

▶ Answer

One Joule = work done when 1 N force moves an object by 1 m in the direction of the force.

Q16. Why do we get tired holding a heavy object even if no work is done?

▶ Answer

Your muscles continuously contract and relax microscopically to hold the weight. This biological work uses energy (chemical → heat). Physics says no displacement = no work, but biology disagrees!

Q17. What is the difference between work and power?

▶ Answer

Work = total energy transferred. Power = rate at which work is done. Two engines can do the same work, but the faster one is more powerful.

Q18. A 1 kW heater operates for 30 minutes. Find energy used in J and kWh.

▶ Answer

kWh: 1 × 0.5 = 0.5 kWh

J: 0.5 × 3.6 × 10⁶ = 1.8 × 10⁶ J

Q19. A 60 W bulb runs all day. Energy used?

▶ Answer

60 × 24 = 1440 Wh = 1.44 kWh = about ₹11.50 at ₹8/unit.

Q20. What happens to the KE when velocity is tripled?

▶ Answer

KE = ½mv². If v becomes 3v, KE becomes 9× (since v²).

🔋 Section R: Energy Saving Tips

  • 💡 Replace bulbs with LEDs (saves ~80% energy).
  • 🌬️ Turn off fans/ACs in empty rooms.
  • 🧊 Set AC to 24°C+ (each degree lower = 6% more energy).
  • 🚿 Take shorter showers (saves geyser energy).
  • 🔌 Unplug chargers when not in use.
  • 🌞 Use natural light during the day.
  • 🚲 Walk or cycle for short distances.
  • 🌡️ Use star-rated appliances (5-star most efficient).
  • ♻️ Compost food waste — reduces methane.
  • 🌱 Plant trees — they absorb CO₂!

Small habits → BIG impact on energy bills AND the planet 🌍

💡 Quick Recap

  • Work = Force × displacement (in direction of force)
  • Energy = capacity to do work; many forms
  • KE = ½mv² (depends on v²)
  • PE = mgh (depends on height)
  • Conservation: total energy never lost — just transformed
  • Power = rate of doing work = W/t = Fv
  • 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ (your electricity bill unit)
  • Simple machines amplify force but never give "free" energy

🎉 Chapter 7 — Work, Energy, Power — fully covered! Next: Atom 🧪

🔍 Section S: Deep Dive into Each Simple Machine

The Lever — Three Classes Compared

Class 1 Lever:

  • Fulcrum is BETWEEN load and effort.
  • Direction of effort is OPPOSITE to load movement.
  • Examples: see-saw, scissors, pliers, crowbar, beam balance.
  • MA can be >1, =1, or <1.

Class 2 Lever:

  • Load is BETWEEN fulcrum and effort.
  • Effort moves SAME direction as load.
  • Examples: wheelbarrow, nutcracker, bottle opener, door (handle = effort, hinge = fulcrum, weight at edge = load).
  • MA always >1 (always multiplies effort).

Class 3 Lever:

  • Effort is BETWEEN fulcrum and load.
  • Examples: cricket bat, fishing rod, tongs, sweeping broom, your forearm!
  • MA always <1 (effort > load) — BUT gives more speed and range of motion.

Forearm as a Class 3 Lever

Your elbow = fulcrum. Bicep muscle (close to elbow) = effort. Object in hand = load.

Even though you use MORE force than the object's weight, you get FAST hand motion. This is why we can throw fast!

Many animal limbs use class 3 levers for SPEED rather than force.

Pulleys in Detail

1. Single Fixed Pulley (pulley fixed; rope moves):

  • MA = 1 (no force advantage)
  • Just changes direction (pull DOWN to lift UP)
  • Easier psychologically
  • Example: well bucket, flag pole

2. Single Movable Pulley (pulley moves with load):

  • MA = 2 (you pull with half the load's weight)
  • You must pull TWICE the distance though (energy conserved!)

3. Block and Tackle System:

  • Combines fixed + movable pulleys
  • MA = number of segments supporting the load
  • Used in cranes, sailboats, elevators

Inclined Plane — Saves Effort, Increases Distance

To lift a 100 kg box straight up by 1 m needs 1000 N (gravity) but only 1 m of effort = 1000 J.

Push it up a 5 m ramp to 1 m height: only 200 N effort but 5 m distance = 1000 J.

Energy is SAME! You just trade FORCE for DISTANCE.

That's why mountain roads ZIG-ZAG — longer but easier climb.

⚡ Section T: Compound Machines

Real machines often combine multiple simple machines.

Machine Simple Machines Used
BicycleWheel-axle, lever (pedals), gears
ScissorsTwo class 1 levers + two wedges (blades)
WheelbarrowLever + wheel-axle
Can openerLever + wheel-axle + wedge
Hand pumpLever + valves
CarWheel-axle, lever, screw, inclined plane, wedge

📐 Section U: Working with Graphs

Force vs Displacement Graph → Area = Work Done

If you plot Force on y-axis vs displacement on x-axis, the AREA under the curve = work done.

Constant force: rectangle area = F × s

Variable force: integrate (area under curve)

Spring: triangle area = ½ × max_F × max_x = ½ kx²

Energy-Position Graph for Falling Body

For a ball dropped from height H:

  • PE starts at mgH, decreases linearly to 0.
  • KE starts at 0, increases linearly to mgH.
  • Total mechanical energy = mgH = constant horizontal line.

🌟 Section V: Energy and Everyday Decisions

Why LED bulbs are best

Take a 60 W incandescent bulb vs 8 W LED giving same light:

  • 60 W bulb running 4 hours/day for 1 year = 60 × 4 × 365 = 87,600 Wh = 87.6 units
  • 8 W LED same usage = 8 × 4 × 365 = 11,680 Wh = 11.68 units
  • Savings: 76 units per year! At ₹8/unit = ₹608 saved per bulb per year!
  • For 10 bulbs in a home: ~₹6,000 saved per year!

EV vs Petrol Car Energy

Per km:

  • Petrol car: ~3 MJ/km from fuel (efficiency ~25%)
  • EV: ~0.6 MJ/km from electricity (efficiency ~85%)
  • EV is ~5× more efficient!
  • + Even when accounting for power plant losses, EV usually wins.

📋 Section W: Concept Cards & Memory Aids

Card 1 — Work

Force × displacement (in same direction). No motion = no work.

Card 2 — KE

½mv². Quadruples when speed doubles. Always positive.

Card 3 — PE

mgh. Depends on REFERENCE (where h = 0).

Card 4 — Conservation

Energy is transformed, not created or destroyed.

Card 5 — Power

How FAST work is done. P = W/t. SI unit: Watt.

Card 6 — kWh

Commercial energy unit. 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ. Used in bills.

🎓 Section X: Quick MCQs

1. Unit of work is:

(a) Watt (b) Joule (c) Newton (d) None

Answer

(b) Joule

2. KE depends on:

(a) Mass only (b) Speed only (c) Mass and v² (d) Height

Answer

(c) Mass and v²

3. If you double speed, KE becomes:

(a) Same (b) Doubled (c) 4× (d) 8×

Answer

(c) 4× (depends on v²)

4. PE formula:

(a) ½mv² (b) mgh (c) Fs (d) Both b & c

Answer

(b) mgh

5. Energy from food is:

(a) Mechanical (b) Chemical (c) Light (d) Sound

Answer

(b) Chemical

6. Power formula:

(a) W = Pt (b) P = W/t (c) P = Fv (d) Both b & c

Answer

(d) Both b & c

7. 1 horsepower equals approximately:

(a) 100 W (b) 746 W (c) 1000 W (d) 200 W

Answer

(b) 746 W

8. Renewable energy source:

(a) Coal (b) Wind (c) Petrol (d) Nuclear

Answer

(b) Wind

9. Mechanical advantage of single fixed pulley:

(a) 1 (b) 2 (c) 3 (d) 0

Answer

(a) 1 (only changes direction)

10. Work done by gravity on a satellite in circular orbit:

(a) Positive (b) Negative (c) Zero (d) Variable

Answer

(c) Zero (gravity perpendicular to motion)

🌟 Chapter 7 Complete! Onwards to atoms and structure of matter! 🧬

🌍 Section Y: Global Energy Crisis

Why we need to switch to renewable energy:

  • 🔥 Burning fossil fuels releases CO₂ → global warming
  • 🛢️ Oil reserves estimated to last ~50 years
  • ⛏️ Coal reserves ~150 years (but pollution from coal is severe)
  • 🌡️ Earth's temperature already 1.1°C above pre-industrial levels
  • 🌊 Sea levels rising due to melting ice caps
  • ❗ India aims for 500 GW renewable capacity by 2030

India's Renewable Energy Achievements:

  • 🏆 4th largest renewable energy capacity in the world
  • ☀️ World's largest solar park: Bhadla Solar Park (Rajasthan) — 2,245 MW!
  • 🌬️ Wind: Muppandal, Tamil Nadu — one of largest wind farms
  • 💧 Hydroelectric: Bhakra-Nangal dam — 1325 MW
  • 🌱 Biogas: rural India uses cow dung gas plants
  • 🔆 PM-KUSUM scheme: solar pumps for farmers

🔬 Section Z: Long Answer Questions

LAQ1. Define work. Derive an expression for work done when force is applied at an angle θ to displacement.

▶ Answer

Work is the product of force and displacement in the direction of force. If F is applied at angle θ, only the component F cos θ contributes to motion along displacement. Hence W = F·s·cos θ. When θ = 0, W = Fs (max). When θ = 90°, W = 0. When θ = 180°, W = −Fs (negative work).

LAQ2. Derive the formula for kinetic energy.

▶ Answer

A body of mass m at rest is acted on by force F, gaining velocity v over distance s. Work done = Fs. Using v² − u² = 2as with u = 0: s = v²/(2a). F = ma. So W = ma · v²/(2a) = ½ mv². This becomes the body's KE: KE = ½ mv².

LAQ3. State and explain the law of conservation of energy with example of free fall.

▶ Answer

Energy can't be created or destroyed, only transformed. Consider a body of mass m dropped from height H. At top: KE = 0, PE = mgH. As it falls, PE → KE. At any height h: PE = mgh, v² = 2g(H−h), so KE = ½ m·2g(H−h) = mg(H−h). Total = mgh + mg(H−h) = mgH = constant ✓

LAQ4. Differentiate among the three classes of levers with examples.

▶ Answer

Class 1: Fulcrum between effort and load. Examples: see-saw, scissors, pliers. MA can be anything.

Class 2: Load between fulcrum and effort. MA > 1. Examples: wheelbarrow, nutcracker.

Class 3: Effort between fulcrum and load. MA < 1 but gains speed/range. Examples: forearm, cricket bat, tongs.

LAQ5. Calculate the energy consumed by a household with 5 LED bulbs (10 W) on for 6 hours, 2 fans (75 W) on for 8 hours, 1 fridge (250 W) on for 24 hours, and 1 TV (150 W) on for 4 hours. Total cost at ₹8/kWh?

▶ Answer

Bulbs: 5 × 10 × 6 = 300 Wh

Fans: 2 × 75 × 8 = 1200 Wh

Fridge: 250 × 24 = 6000 Wh

TV: 150 × 4 = 600 Wh

Total = 8100 Wh = 8.1 kWh; Cost = 8.1 × 8 = ₹64.80

💡 Last Words on Chapter 7

Energy is everything. Every push, pull, throw, lift, light, sound, motion — it's all energy in different forms. Master this chapter and you'll see physics everywhere!

  • W = Fs (work)
  • KE = ½mv² (motion energy)
  • PE = mgh (height energy)
  • Conservation rules!
  • P = W/t (rate)

🏁 Chapter 7 mastered! Time for atoms ⚛️

🧠 Section AA: Common Mistakes & Misconceptions

Mistake 1: "If I push a wall and it doesn't move, work is being done because I'm tired."

Reality: Physics defines work mathematically — W = F × s. If s = 0, W = 0 regardless of fatigue.

Mistake 2: "Heavy objects have more energy than light ones."

Reality: Energy depends on BOTH mass AND velocity (KE) or height (PE). A bullet (low mass) at high speed has more KE than a brick at rest.

Mistake 3: "Pulleys give us free energy."

Reality: No machine creates energy! They trade force for distance — total work stays the same (or slightly less due to friction).

Mistake 4: "Power and energy are the same thing."

Reality: Energy is total work done. Power is how fast work is done. Same energy can be delivered slowly (low power) or quickly (high power).

Mistake 5: "Negative work means no work."

Reality: Negative work means force is OPPOSITE to displacement — energy is being REMOVED (e.g., friction removes KE).

📊 Section BB: Comparison Tables

Mechanical Energy Forms

Type Formula When?
Translational KE½mv²Object moving
Rotational KE½Iω²Object spinning
Gravitational PEmghObject at height
Elastic PE½kx²Stretched/compressed spring

Work, Energy, Power — Snapshot

Concept What it means SI Unit
WorkEnergy transferred via forceJoule (J)
EnergyCapacity to do workJoule (J)
PowerRate of doing workWatt (W)

🏆 Chapter 7 done! 1500+ lines of energy mastery!

🎓 Section CC: Exam-Ready Quick Notes

  1. Work: W = F · s · cos θ. Joule.
  2. Positive work: Force and displacement same direction (cos θ > 0).
  3. Negative work: Force opposes displacement (cos θ < 0).
  4. Zero work: F = 0, s = 0, or θ = 90°.
  5. Energy: Capacity to do work. Joule.
  6. KE: ½ m v². Always positive.
  7. PE: m g h. Reference-dependent.
  8. Mechanical energy: KE + PE.
  9. Conservation of energy: Total energy of isolated system is constant.
  10. Work-energy theorem: W_net = ΔKE.
  11. Power: P = W/t = F·v. Watt.
  12. Commercial unit: kWh. 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J.
  13. Renewable: sun, wind, water, biomass, geothermal, tidal.
  14. Non-renewable: coal, oil, gas, nuclear.

🎯 Final Pep Talk

You've mastered work, energy, and power — the engine of physics! These concepts power EVERYTHING from cars to your heartbeat. Keep practising those numericals — they'll appear in many exams!

🏆 Chapter 7 — Work, Energy, Power: COMPLETE! ⚡

🌟 Section DD: Real-World Applications Recap

  • 🚗 Car braking — KE converts to heat in brakes
  • 🌊 Dam — water's PE → turbine KE → electrical energy
  • 🌬️ Wind turbine — wind KE → mechanical → electrical
  • 🔋 Battery — chemical energy → electrical
  • 🥾 Walking — chemical (food) → mechanical → some heat
  • 📱 Phone charging — electrical → chemical (stored in battery)
  • 🎢 Roller coaster — PE ↔ KE (no engine needed after first hill!)
  • ⚡ Lightning — electrical PE → KE + heat + sound + light
  • 🌞 Solar cell — photon energy → electrical
  • 🎻 Guitar — mechanical (pluck) → sound (vibration)
  • 🚀 Rocket — chemical (fuel) → kinetic (motion) + heat
  • 🌱 Photosynthesis — light → chemical (glucose)

Every transformation obeys conservation of energy.

— Chapter 7 truly complete —

🌟 Energy Vocabulary Glossary

Calorie
Unit of heat; 1 cal = 4.184 J. Diet "Calorie" = 1000 calories = 1 kcal.
BTU
British Thermal Unit ≈ 1055 J. Used in heating/cooling.
Watt-hour
Energy unit; 1 Wh = 3600 J.
Joule
SI unit; work done by 1 N over 1 m.
Horsepower
Power unit; 1 hp ≈ 746 W. Used for engines.
Watt
SI unit of power = 1 J/s.
Erg
CGS unit of work; 1 J = 10⁷ ergs.

🌟 Mind Map (Text Form)

WORK
 └─ W = F · s · cos θ
 └─ Joule
 └─ Can be +, −, or 0
       │
       ▼
ENERGY (capacity to do work)
 ├─ Mechanical
 │   ├─ Kinetic (½mv²)
 │   └─ Potential (mgh, ½kx²)
 ├─ Thermal (heat)
 ├─ Light, Sound
 ├─ Chemical (food, fuel, battery)
 ├─ Electrical
 ├─ Nuclear
 └─ Magnetic
       │
       ▼
CONSERVATION
 └─ Total energy = constant
       │
       ▼
POWER (rate of energy)
 ├─ P = W/t = F · v
 └─ Watt, kW, hp
       │
       ▼
SOURCES
 ├─ Renewable: sun, wind, water, biomass
 └─ Non-renewable: coal, oil, gas, nuclear

⚡⚡⚡

Energy never dies — it just dances from one form to another.

— end of Chapter 7 —

Onward to Chapter 8 ⚛️

📘

⚛️

→

Chapter 8

⚛️

⚛ Chapter 8: Journey Inside the Atom

Textbook: Exploration (New NCERT 2024-25) | 22 Pages | Topics: Atomic models (Thomson, Rutherford, Bohr), Subatomic particles (electron, proton, neutron), Atomic number, Mass number, Electron configuration, Valency, Isotopes, Isobars

8.1-8.2 Atomic Models - Evolution of Ideas

Rutherford's Gold Foil Experiment - Key Observations:

ObservationConclusion
Most alpha particles passed straight throughMost of atom is empty space
Some were deflected at small anglesPositive charge exists somewhere inside
Very few (1 in 20,000) bounced backPositive charge concentrated in very small, dense nucleus

8.3 Subatomic Particles

ParticleSymbolChargeMassLocationDiscovered by
Electrone--1 (negative)1/1840 of proton (negligible)Orbiting nucleus in shellsJ.J. Thomson (1897)
Protonp++1 (positive)1 u (1 atomic mass unit)Inside nucleusGoldstein (1886), Rutherford
Neutronn00 (neutral)1 u (same as proton)Inside nucleusJames Chadwick (1932)

Key Points:

  • In a neutral atom: Number of protons = Number of electrons
  • Nucleus contains: protons + neutrons (almost all the mass!)
  • Atom is mostly empty space
  • Nucleus size is about 10-15 m; Atom size is about 10-10 m (100,000 times larger!)

8.5-8.6 Atomic Number and Mass Number

QuantitySymbolDefinitionDetermines
Atomic NumberZNumber of protons in nucleusIdentity of element (what element it is!)
Mass NumberAProtons + NeutronsMass of the atom
NeutronsnA - Z (mass number - atomic number)Stability of nucleus

A = Z + n (Mass number = Atomic number + Neutrons)

Example: Sodium (Na): Z=11, A=23, so neutrons = 23-11 = 12

8.7 Electron Configuration (Distribution in Shells)

Rules for filling electrons:

  1. Maximum electrons in shell n = 2n2
  2. Outermost shell can have maximum 8 electrons
  3. Fill inner shells first (K, then L, then M...)

Examples:

ElementZKLMNValence e-Valency
Hydrogen (H)11---11
Carbon (C)624--44
Oxygen (O)826--62
Sodium (Na)11281-11
Chlorine (Cl)17287-71
Calcium (Ca)20288222

8.8 Valency (Combining Capacity)

Valency = Number of electrons an atom needs to gain, lose, or share to complete its outermost shell (octet = 8).

Valence electronsValencyTendencyExamples
1, 2, 31, 2, 3Lose electrons (metals)Na(1), Mg(2), Al(3)
44Share electronsCarbon, Silicon
5, 6, 73, 2, 1Gain electrons (non-metals)N(3), O(2), Cl(1)
8 (or 2 for He)0Already stable (noble gases)Ne, Ar, Kr

Shortcut: If valence electrons are less than or equal to 4, valency = valence electrons. If more than 4, valency = 8 - valence electrons.

8.9 Isotopes and Isobars

FeatureIsotopesIsobars
DefinitionSame element, same Z, different ADifferent elements, different Z, same A
SameAtomic number (protons)Mass number
DifferentNumber of neutrons and mass numberAtomic number (protons) and chemical properties
Chemical propertiesSAME (same electrons)DIFFERENT (different elements)
ExampleC-12, C-13, C-14 (all Carbon)Ca-40 and Ar-40 (different elements, same mass)

Applications of Isotopes:

  • C-14: Carbon dating (age of fossils and archaeological samples)
  • Co-60: Treatment of cancer (radiation therapy)
  • I-131: Treatment of thyroid disorders
  • U-235: Nuclear fuel for power generation

Important Questions and Answers

Q1: Compare Thomson's, Rutherford's, and Bohr's models of atom.

FeatureThomsonRutherfordBohr
Positive chargeSpread throughoutConcentrated in nucleusIn nucleus
ElectronsEmbedded in + sphereOrbit nucleus randomlyFixed energy shells
LimitationDisproved by gold foil exptCan't explain stabilityWorks for H, not complex atoms

Q2: An element has atomic number 12 and mass number 24. Find protons, electrons, neutrons, and electronic configuration.

Ans: Protons = Z = 12 | Electrons = 12 (neutral atom) | Neutrons = A - Z = 24 - 12 = 12

Electronic configuration: K(2), L(8), M(2) = 2, 8, 2

Valence electrons = 2, so Valency = 2. This is Magnesium (Mg).

Q3: What are isotopes? Give two examples with applications.

Ans: Isotopes are atoms of the same element with same atomic number but different mass numbers (different number of neutrons).

  • Carbon-14: Used in radiocarbon dating to determine age of archaeological artifacts
  • Cobalt-60: Used in radiation therapy for cancer treatment

⚛️ Section A: What is an Atom?

An ATOM is the smallest unit of matter that retains the properties of an element. The word "atom" comes from the Greek "atomos" meaning "indivisible."

Modern definition: An atom is the basic building block of matter, made up of three smaller particles called SUBATOMIC PARTICLES — protons, neutrons, and electrons.

Atoms are incredibly small!

  • Diameter: about 0.1 nanometers (1 × 10⁻¹⁰ m)
  • 1 millimetre contains about 10 million atoms in a line!
  • A single drop of water contains about 1.67 × 10²¹ atoms

How Small is an Atom?

Imagine you blow up a marble until it's as big as the Earth. The atoms inside that marble would each be the size of a small cherry!

If an atom were the size of a football stadium, the nucleus would be the size of a pea at the centre! Most of the atom is empty space.

🏛️ Section B: History of Atomic Theory

1. Ancient Greek Idea (~400 BCE)

Democritus & Leucippus proposed that matter is made of tiny indivisible particles called "atomos." This was philosophical — not based on experiment!

2. John Dalton's Atomic Theory (1803)

English scientist John Dalton proposed the FIRST scientific atomic theory:

  • 1. All matter is made of tiny indivisible particles called ATOMS.
  • 2. Atoms of the same element are IDENTICAL in mass and properties.
  • 3. Atoms of different elements have different properties.
  • 4. Atoms combine in fixed whole-number ratios to form compounds.
  • 5. Chemical reactions involve rearrangement of atoms; atoms are not created or destroyed.

Limitations: Wrong on "indivisible" — atoms have parts! Also doesn't explain isotopes.

3. J.J. Thomson's "Plum Pudding" Model (1897)

J.J. Thomson discovered the ELECTRON using cathode ray experiments. He proposed:

  • Atom is a sphere of POSITIVE charge with NEGATIVE electrons embedded in it.
  • Like plums in a pudding (or seeds in a watermelon).
  • Total positive charge = total negative charge → atom is neutral.

Limitations: Couldn't explain Rutherford's experiment.

4. Rutherford's Nuclear Model (1911)

Ernest Rutherford fired alpha particles (positively charged) at a thin gold foil. Most passed through, but a FEW bounced back!

His conclusions:

  • Atom is MOSTLY EMPTY SPACE (most α particles went through).
  • There's a tiny, dense, positively charged NUCLEUS at the centre (rare collisions caused bouncing).
  • Electrons revolve around the nucleus.
  • Nucleus is about 10⁻¹⁵ m; atom is 10⁻¹⁰ m → atom is 100,000× the size of nucleus!

Quote from Rutherford: "It was as incredible as if you fired a 15-inch shell at a piece of tissue paper, and it came back and hit you!"

Limitations: Orbiting electrons should LOSE energy and crash into nucleus (according to classical physics). They don't! Rutherford couldn't explain this.

5. Bohr's Model (1913)

Niels Bohr improved Rutherford's model:

  • Electrons orbit the nucleus in fixed, ALLOWED orbits called SHELLS or ENERGY LEVELS.
  • These shells have fixed energies — electrons don't lose energy while in an orbit.
  • Electrons can JUMP between shells by absorbing/releasing specific amounts of energy.
  • Shells are labeled K, L, M, N, ... (or 1, 2, 3, 4, ...).
  • Each shell holds maximum 2n² electrons (where n = shell number).

Maximum electrons per shell:

  • K shell (n=1): 2 × 1² = 2 electrons
  • L shell (n=2): 2 × 2² = 8 electrons
  • M shell (n=3): 2 × 3² = 18 electrons
  • N shell (n=4): 2 × 4² = 32 electrons

6. Quantum Mechanical Model (1920s, ongoing)

Modern model based on quantum mechanics (Schrödinger, Heisenberg):

  • Electrons don't have fixed orbits — they exist in PROBABILITY CLOUDS called orbitals.
  • Orbitals have shapes: s (sphere), p (dumbbell), d (cloverleaf), f (complex).
  • You can't know an electron's position AND momentum exactly (Heisenberg's uncertainty principle).

This is the most accurate model we have today, but Bohr's model is great for understanding chemistry!

🔬 Section C: Subatomic Particles

Particle Symbol Charge Mass (kg) Mass (amu) Location
Protonp+11.673 × 10⁻²⁷1Nucleus
Neutronn01.675 × 10⁻²⁷1Nucleus
Electrone⁻−19.109 × 10⁻³¹1/1836Shells

Discoveries Timeline

  • Electron — discovered by J.J. Thomson in 1897 (cathode ray tube)
  • Proton — discovered by Ernest Rutherford in 1911 (gold foil experiment)
  • Neutron — discovered by James Chadwick in 1932 (later, because it's neutral, hard to detect!)

Mass Comparison

Proton and neutron have nearly the same mass (~ 1 amu).

Electron is about 1836 TIMES LIGHTER than a proton!

So the nucleus contains nearly ALL the mass of the atom (~99.97%).

The electrons hardly contribute to mass, but they determine chemistry!

🔢 Section D: Atomic Number and Mass Number

Atomic Number (Z) = Number of PROTONS in the nucleus.

It's like the atom's ID number. Z determines which element it is.

Example: Carbon Z = 6 (always 6 protons). Sodium Z = 11. Gold Z = 79.

Mass Number (A) = Number of PROTONS + Number of NEUTRONS.

A = Z + N (where N is the number of neutrons)

It tells you the TOTAL particles in the nucleus.

Standard Notation

An element X is written as: AZX

Examples:

  • 126C: Carbon with mass 12, 6 protons, 6 neutrons
  • 2311Na: Sodium with mass 23, 11 protons, 12 neutrons
  • 11H: Hydrogen with mass 1, 1 proton, 0 neutrons
  • 168O: Oxygen with mass 16, 8 protons, 8 neutrons
  • 19779Au: Gold with mass 197, 79 protons, 118 neutrons

Quick Calculations

Given Z and A, you can find everything:

  • Number of protons = Z
  • Number of neutrons = A − Z
  • Number of electrons (in neutral atom) = Z

Example: Calcium has Z = 20, A = 40. So:

  • Protons = 20
  • Neutrons = 40 − 20 = 20
  • Electrons = 20

⚡ Section E: Electronic Configuration

The arrangement of electrons in different shells around the nucleus is called ELECTRONIC CONFIGURATION.

Rules:

  1. Fill the INNER shells first (K, then L, then M, ...).
  2. Maximum electrons per shell: 2n² (K=2, L=8, M=18, N=32)
  3. BUT outermost shell can NEVER have more than 8 electrons.
  4. Penultimate (second-last) shell can never have more than 18.

Examples — First 20 Elements

Element Symbol Z K L M N
HydrogenH11—
HeliumHe22—
LithiumLi321—
CarbonC624—
NitrogenN725—
OxygenO826—
NeonNe1028—
SodiumNa11281—
MagnesiumMg12282—
AluminiumAl13283—
SiliconSi14284—
PhosphorusP15285—
SulfurS16286—
ChlorineCl17287—
ArgonAr18288—
PotassiumK192881
CalciumCa202882

Valence Electrons

Valence electrons = number of electrons in the OUTERMOST shell.

These are the most important! They decide how an atom reacts chemically.

Example:

  • Sodium (Na): K=2, L=8, M=1 → valence = 1
  • Chlorine (Cl): K=2, L=8, M=7 → valence = 7
  • Argon (Ar): K=2, L=8, M=8 → valence = 8 (FULL — very stable!)

Atoms react to GAIN or LOSE electrons to get a FULL outermost shell (octet).

🔁 Section F: Isotopes, Isobars, and Isotones

Isotopes — Same Z, Different A

Isotopes are atoms of the SAME element with the SAME number of protons but DIFFERENT number of neutrons.

Examples:

  • Hydrogen: 1H (protium), 2H (deuterium, 1n), 3H (tritium, 2n)
  • Carbon: 12C (normal), 13C, 14C (radioactive — used in carbon dating!)
  • Uranium: 235U (nuclear fuel), 238U (more common)
  • Chlorine: 35Cl (75%), 37Cl (25%)

Properties: Isotopes have SAME chemical properties (same number of electrons!) but DIFFERENT physical properties (mass, density).

Uses of Isotopes

  • 🪨 Carbon-14: dating ancient fossils (up to 50,000 years old)
  • 🩺 Iodine-131: treating thyroid cancer
  • 🧬 Cobalt-60: cancer radiotherapy
  • 💊 Phosphorus-32: study plant metabolism
  • ⚛️ Uranium-235: nuclear power plants, atomic bombs
  • 🚀 Plutonium-238: spacecraft power (radioactive decay heat)

Isobars — Different Z, Same A

Different elements with same MASS NUMBER but different atomic numbers.

Example: 4018Ar (argon) and 4020Ca (calcium) — both have mass 40.

They have completely different chemical properties.

Isotones — Same N, Different Z

Different elements with the SAME number of neutrons.

Example: 146C (6p + 8n) and 157N (7p + 8n) — both have 8 neutrons.

⚛️ Section G: Atomic Mass and Average Atomic Mass

Atomic mass unit (amu) is the unit used for atomic masses. 1 amu = 1/12 the mass of a 12C atom = 1.66 × 10⁻²⁴ g.

An element can have multiple isotopes. The reported atomic mass is the WEIGHTED AVERAGE of all naturally occurring isotopes.

Example: Chlorine

Cl exists as 75% 35Cl + 25% 37Cl.

Average = (0.75 × 35) + (0.25 × 37) = 26.25 + 9.25 = 35.5 amu

That's why Chlorine's atomic mass on the periodic table is 35.5 (not a whole number).

📋 Section H: Formula Sheet & Quick Notes

  • Z = number of protons = number of electrons (neutral atom)
  • A = Z + N (mass number = protons + neutrons)
  • N = A − Z (neutrons)
  • Max electrons in shell n = 2n²
  • Outermost shell max = 8 electrons
  • 1 amu = 1.66 × 10⁻²⁴ g
  • e⁻ mass ≈ 1/1836 of proton
  • Atom: ~10⁻¹⁰ m; nucleus: ~10⁻¹⁵ m

⚡ Section I: Ions — Charged Atoms

An IONS is an atom (or group of atoms) that has GAINED or LOST one or more electrons, giving it an electric charge.

If atom loses electron(s) → fewer e⁻ than p⁺ → POSITIVE ion (cation).

If atom gains electron(s) → more e⁻ than p⁺ → NEGATIVE ion (anion).

Cations (Positive Ions)

Metals tend to LOSE electrons → form cations.

  • Sodium: Na → Na⁺ + e⁻ (loses 1 electron)
  • Magnesium: Mg → Mg²⁺ + 2e⁻ (loses 2)
  • Aluminium: Al → Al³⁺ + 3e⁻ (loses 3)
  • Potassium: K → K⁺ + e⁻
  • Calcium: Ca → Ca²⁺ + 2e⁻

Anions (Negative Ions)

Non-metals tend to GAIN electrons → form anions.

  • Chlorine: Cl + e⁻ → Cl⁻ (gains 1)
  • Oxygen: O + 2e⁻ → O²⁻ (gains 2)
  • Nitrogen: N + 3e⁻ → N³⁻ (gains 3)
  • Fluorine: F + e⁻ → F⁻
  • Sulfur: S + 2e⁻ → S²⁻

Why Atoms Form Ions — The Octet Rule

Atoms are stable when they have 8 electrons in their outermost shell (called an OCTET).

The noble gases (He, Ne, Ar, Kr...) have a full octet — that's why they're "noble" (don't react).

Other atoms lose or gain electrons to ACHIEVE this stable arrangement.

Example: Na has 11 electrons (2,8,1). It loses 1 to become Na⁺ (2,8) — same as Neon!

Cl has 17 (2,8,7). It gains 1 to become Cl⁻ (2,8,8) — same as Argon!

🔗 Section J: Ionic Bonds — How Atoms Combine

When a metal LOSES electron(s) and a non-metal GAINS them, the two oppositely-charged ions attract each other — forming an IONIC BOND.

Classic example: Sodium Chloride (NaCl, table salt)

  1. Sodium (Na, 2,8,1) loses 1 e⁻ → Na⁺ (2,8)
  2. Chlorine (Cl, 2,8,7) gains that 1 e⁻ → Cl⁻ (2,8,8)
  3. Na⁺ and Cl⁻ attract each other → form NaCl

Same for other ionic compounds:

  • Magnesium oxide: Mg²⁺ + O²⁻ → MgO
  • Aluminium chloride: Al³⁺ + 3Cl⁻ → AlCl₃
  • Calcium fluoride: Ca²⁺ + 2F⁻ → CaF₂

🌍 Section K: Atoms of the Most Common Elements

Element Symbol Z A Where found
HydrogenH11Stars, water, organic matter
HeliumHe24Balloons, sun
CarbonC612Life (all organic)
NitrogenN714Air (78%), proteins
OxygenO816Air (21%), water
SodiumNa1123Salt, sea
AluminiumAl1327Earth's crust (3rd most)
SiliconSi1428Sand, glass, chips
IronFe2656Earth's core, blood (haemoglobin)
