CopperCu2964Wires, coins
SilverAg47108Jewellery, mirrors
GoldAu79197Jewellery, electronics
MercuryHg80200Old thermometers
UraniumU92238Nuclear fuel

🔬 Section L: Detailed Discovery Stories

Story 1: Discovery of the Electron (Thomson, 1897)

J.J. Thomson was studying CATHODE RAY TUBES — sealed glass tubes with gas at low pressure and electrodes at each end. When high voltage was applied, mysterious GREEN GLOWING RAYS appeared.

He noticed:

  • The rays travelled in straight lines.
  • They were deflected by electric and magnetic fields → so they were CHARGED.
  • They were deflected TOWARD the positive plate → so they were NEGATIVELY charged.
  • They were the SAME regardless of the gas in the tube → must be UNIVERSAL.

He concluded these were tiny negative particles inside every atom! He called them "corpuscles" — later renamed ELECTRONS.

He won the 1906 Nobel Prize in Physics.

Story 2: Discovery of the Nucleus (Rutherford, 1911)

Ernest Rutherford and his students (Geiger and Marsden) fired ALPHA PARTICLES (helium nuclei) at a very thin gold foil.

Expected (based on Thomson's model): All particles would pass through with slight deflection.

Observed:

  • Most particles passed straight through ✓
  • Some were deflected by SMALL angles
  • BUT a few (~1 in 8000) were deflected by LARGE angles or even BOUNCED BACK!

Rutherford was shocked. He concluded:

  • Atom is MOSTLY empty space (explains why most passed through)
  • There's a small DENSE POSITIVE NUCLEUS at the centre (explains rare bounces)
  • Electrons revolve in the empty space around it

Story 3: Discovery of the Neutron (Chadwick, 1932)

Why was the neutron discovered so much LATER?

Because it's neutral! It doesn't interact electrically, so it doesn't leave easy tracks in detectors.

James Chadwick bombarded beryllium with alpha particles. Mysterious neutral rays came out that could knock protons out of paraffin wax. Using conservation of momentum, he calculated the mass of the rays = about 1 amu (same as proton).

He named them NEUTRONS. Won 1935 Nobel Prize.

📐 Section M: Drawing Atom Diagrams

To draw an atom (Bohr model):

  1. Draw the nucleus as a small filled circle in the centre.
  2. Label it with number of protons (+) and neutrons (n).
  3. Draw concentric circles for each shell (K, L, M, ...).
  4. Place electrons on each shell (as dots).
  5. Follow shell capacity rules.

Example Atoms

❓ Section N: Practice Questions

Q1. What are the three subatomic particles?

▶ Answer

Proton (+1), Neutron (0), Electron (−1).

Q2. Who proposed the plum pudding model?

▶ Answer

J.J. Thomson (1897).

Q3. What is the difference between mass number and atomic number?

▶ Answer

Atomic number Z = number of protons. Mass number A = protons + neutrons. So Z identifies the element; A tells the total nuclear particles.

Q4. Write the electronic configuration of Oxygen (Z = 8) and Silicon (Z = 14).

▶ Answer

Oxygen: 2, 6

Silicon: 2, 8, 4

Q5. An atom has 12 protons, 12 neutrons. Find Z, A, and identify the element.

▶ Answer

Z = 12, A = 24. Element: Magnesium (Mg).

Q6. What is meant by valency? What is the valency of Na, O, Cl?

▶ Answer

Valency is the combining capacity of an atom = how many electrons it loses/gains to attain a full octet.

Na (2,8,1): valency 1 (loses 1 e⁻).

O (2,6): valency 2 (gains 2 e⁻).

Cl (2,8,7): valency 1 (gains 1 e⁻).

Q7. Define isotopes and give two examples.

▶ Answer

Isotopes are atoms of the same element with same Z but different A (different neutrons). Examples: 12C and 14C; 1H and 2H.

Q8. Why does an atom have no net charge?

▶ Answer

Because the number of protons (+) equals the number of electrons (−). Charges cancel out.

Q9. What did Rutherford conclude from the gold foil experiment?

▶ Answer

(i) Atom is mostly empty space. (ii) There's a dense, positively charged nucleus at the centre. (iii) Electrons revolve around it.

Q10. State the limitations of Rutherford's model.

▶ Answer

According to classical physics, an orbiting electron should continuously emit energy and spiral into the nucleus. This doesn't happen — atoms are stable. Rutherford's model couldn't explain why.

📋 Section O: Glossary

Atom
Smallest unit of matter that has properties of an element.
Nucleus
Tiny dense centre of the atom containing protons and neutrons.
Proton
Positively charged particle in nucleus. Mass ~1 amu.
Neutron
Neutral particle in nucleus. Mass ~1 amu.
Electron
Negatively charged particle outside nucleus. Mass ≈ 1/1836 amu.
Atomic Number (Z)
Number of protons.
Mass Number (A)
Total protons + neutrons.
Shell
Energy level where electrons reside; K, L, M, N...
Valence Electrons
Electrons in the outermost shell.
Octet Rule
Atoms tend to have 8 e⁻ in outermost shell for stability.
Ion
Charged atom (lost or gained electrons).
Cation
Positive ion (lost electrons).
Anion
Negative ion (gained electrons).
Isotope
Same Z, different A.
Isobar
Same A, different Z.
Isotone
Same N, different Z.

🌟 Chapter 8 Foundation Complete!

☢️ Section P: Radioactivity

Radioactivity is the spontaneous emission of radiation from unstable atomic nuclei.

Some atoms (especially heavy ones like Uranium) have unstable nuclei that break down, releasing energy and particles.

Discovered by Henri Becquerel (1896) — saw that uranium salts fogged photographic plates even in the dark.

Marie Curie and Pierre Curie then discovered Polonium and Radium — both naturally radioactive. Marie won TWO Nobel Prizes!

Three Types of Radiation

Type Symbol Composition Charge Penetration
Alphaα2p + 2n (He nucleus)+2Stopped by paper
BetaβHigh-speed electrons−1Stopped by aluminium
GammaγHigh-energy EM rays0Needs lead/concrete

Half-Life

Half-life is the time taken for HALF of a radioactive sample to decay.

Each isotope has a different half-life:

  • Carbon-14: 5,730 years (used in carbon dating)
  • Uranium-238: 4.5 billion years
  • Iodine-131: 8 days
  • Plutonium-239: 24,000 years

After 2 half-lives, only 25% remains. After 3, only 12.5%, and so on.

Uses and Dangers

Beneficial uses:

  • 🩺 Cancer treatment (radiotherapy)
  • 🪨 Carbon dating
  • ⚡ Nuclear power plants
  • 🚀 Spacecraft power (RTGs)
  • 📷 X-rays for medical imaging
  • 🍎 Food irradiation (kill bacteria)

Dangers:

  • ☠️ Can damage DNA → cancer
  • 🧬 Genetic mutations
  • 💀 Radiation sickness
  • 💥 Nuclear accidents (Chernobyl, Fukushima)

⚛️ Section Q: Nuclear Reactions

Nuclear Fission

SPLITTING a heavy nucleus into smaller ones, releasing huge energy.

Example: 235U + n → 141Ba + 92Kr + 3n + huge energy!

Used in:

  • Nuclear power plants (controlled fission)
  • Atomic bombs (uncontrolled chain reaction)

Nuclear Fusion

JOINING two light nuclei to form a heavier one, releasing even MORE energy.

Example: 4 ¹H → ⁴He + huge energy (happens in the Sun!)

Fusion powers the Sun and stars. We're trying to use it for clean energy on Earth (ITER project).

Why Mass → Energy?

Einstein's famous equation: E = mc²

A tiny bit of mass converts into HUGE energy (because c² is enormous: 9 × 10¹⁶).

1 gram of mass = 9 × 10¹³ J = enough to power a city for years!

🌌 Section R: Modern Physics Glimpses

Quarks — Even Smaller!

Protons and neutrons are NOT fundamental! They're made of even tinier particles called QUARKS.

  • A proton = 2 "up" quarks + 1 "down" quark
  • A neutron = 1 "up" + 2 "down" quarks

Quarks are held together by the STRONG NUCLEAR FORCE — one of the four fundamental forces.

The Four Fundamental Forces

Force Range Strength Action
GravitationalInfiniteWeakestPulls masses
ElectromagneticInfiniteStrongActs on charges
Strong nuclearVery short (~10⁻¹⁵ m)StrongestHolds nucleus together
Weak nuclearShortWeakCauses some decays

The Higgs Boson — "God Particle"

In 2012, the Higgs Boson was finally discovered at CERN (Switzerland) using the Large Hadron Collider. It's the particle that gives other particles their MASS!

This was one of the biggest scientific achievements of the 21st century.

🧮 Section S: Numerical Practice

P1. An atom has 17 protons and 18 neutrons. Find Z and A.

Z = 17; A = 17 + 18 = 35. Element: Chlorine

P2. Electronic configuration of an element with Z = 19?

K=2, L=8, M=8, N=1 → 2, 8, 8, 1 (Potassium)

P3. What is the maximum number of electrons in the L shell?

L is n=2. Max = 2 × 2² = 8 electrons

P4. Calculate the average atomic mass of an element with two isotopes: 80% of mass 35 and 20% of mass 37.

Avg = 0.8(35) + 0.2(37) = 28 + 7.4 = 35.4 amu

P5. If 100 g of a radioactive substance has a half-life of 10 years, how much remains after 30 years?

3 half-lives → divide by 2³ = 8

100/8 = 12.5 g

P6. An atom of Magnesium has Z = 12, A = 24. Find protons, neutrons, electrons.

Protons = 12, neutrons = 24 − 12 = 12, electrons = 12.

P7. Find the valency of an atom with electronic configuration 2, 8, 5.

Outermost shell has 5 e⁻. Atom can gain 3 e⁻ to complete octet. Valency = 3.

P8. Hydrogen has 3 isotopes: protium (mass 1), deuterium (mass 2), tritium (mass 3). What's same and different?

Same: Z = 1 (all have 1 proton, 1 electron). Same chemistry.

Different: Neutrons (0, 1, 2). Different mass, density, half-life.

P9. If an atom has 11 protons, 12 neutrons, 10 electrons — what's its charge?

11 (+) and 10 (−) → net charge = +1 (it's a Na⁺ ion).

P10. Identify isotopes/isobars: 14C, 14N, 12C.

14C and 12C → ISOTOPES (same Z = 6).

14C and 14N → ISOBARS (same A = 14).

🌟 Section T: Interesting Atomic Facts

  • 🌌 You are made of star stuff! All atoms heavier than hydrogen were forged in stars billions of years ago.
  • 💎 Diamond and graphite are both pure carbon, but different atomic arrangements give very different properties.
  • 🌬️ Helium atoms are so small they can leak through balloon walls.
  • 🥇 Gold (Au) gets its golden colour from quantum effects in its 6s electrons.
  • 🪨 The oldest things on Earth are zircon crystals — about 4.4 billion years old.
  • ☢️ A banana contains a tiny amount of radioactive potassium-40 — but it's harmless!
  • 🌞 Hydrogen makes up 75% of all matter in the universe.
  • 🧠 Your brain contains about 10²⁷ atoms.
  • 📏 If you removed all empty space from a person's atoms, they'd shrink to the size of a grain of sand!
  • 🚀 An atom of darmstadtium (Z=110) was created in 1994 — it exists for just milliseconds.

🌟 Final Recap

  • Atom = nucleus (protons + neutrons) + orbiting electrons
  • Z = protons; A = protons + neutrons
  • Shells: K(2), L(8), M(18), N(32); outermost ≤ 8
  • Models: Dalton → Thomson → Rutherford → Bohr → Quantum
  • Isotopes: same Z, different A
  • Cations lose e⁻; Anions gain e⁻
  • Octet rule explains why elements react

⚛️ Chapter 8 done! On to Matter and its States!

🇮🇳 Section U: Indian Contributions to Atomic Science

Maharshi Kanad (~600 BCE) — The First Atomist

Long before John Dalton, the Indian sage Maharshi Kanad proposed that all matter is made of tiny indivisible particles called "paramāṇu" (परमाणु).

He founded the Vaisheshika school of philosophy. His ideas include:

  • Matter is made of "anu" (particles)
  • "Paramāṇu" are eternal and indivisible
  • Particles combine to form everything we see
  • Two particles → "dvyaṇuka" (dimer), three → "tryaṇuka" (trimer)

His vision predates Greek atomism (Democritus, 460 BCE) and Western atomic theory (Dalton, 1803) by centuries.

Dr. Homi J. Bhabha (1909–1966) — Father of Indian Nuclear Programme

A brilliant physicist who founded the Tata Institute of Fundamental Research (TIFR) in 1945 and the Atomic Energy Establishment (now BARC) in 1954.

Key contributions:

  • Bhabha Scattering — electron-positron scattering theory
  • Cascade theory of electron showers (cosmic rays)
  • Established India's peaceful nuclear energy programme
  • Founded Apsara, India's first nuclear reactor (1956)

BARC (Bhabha Atomic Research Centre) in Mumbai is named after him.

Dr. A.P.J. Abdul Kalam (1931–2015) — Missile Man

India's 11th President and aerospace scientist. Played a key role in:

  • India's nuclear tests at Pokhran-II (1998)
  • Development of ballistic missiles (Agni, Prithvi)
  • Inspiring millions of Indian students in science

Sir C.V. Raman (1888–1970)

First Asian Nobel Prize winner in Science (Physics, 1930).

Discovered the Raman Effect — when light scatters off molecules, the scattered light's wavelength can change slightly. This reveals atomic and molecular structure!

Today, Raman spectroscopy is used to identify chemicals, study DNA, and even on Mars rovers.

🧪 Section V: Activities & Experiments

Activity 1: Build an Atom Model

Materials: Cardboard, modelling clay (3 colours), thin wire, scissors, glue.

Steps:

  1. Choose an element (e.g., Carbon: Z=6).
  2. Make 6 protons (red clay) and 6 neutrons (blue clay) — stick together as nucleus.
  3. Make 6 electrons (yellow clay tiny balls).
  4. Bend wire into 2 circles — small inner (K shell), bigger outer (L shell).
  5. Place 2 electrons on K shell, 4 electrons on L shell.
  6. Label everything!

Activity 2: Flame Test (with adult supervision)

Different elements glow in DIFFERENT colours when heated — because their electrons jump between specific shells!

  • Sodium (table salt): bright yellow-orange
  • Potassium: lilac/violet
  • Copper: blue-green
  • Calcium: orange-red
  • Lithium: crimson

This is why fireworks have different colours — each colour comes from a different metal!

Activity 3: Static Charge Demo

Rub a plastic comb on dry hair, then bring it near small paper bits.

The comb attracts the paper! Why? Rubbing transfers electrons, making the comb negatively charged. It pulls on the paper because of charge separation.

This is direct evidence that atoms contain CHARGED particles!

📋 Section W: Final MCQ Set

Q1. The maximum number of electrons in M shell is:

(a) 8 (b) 18 (c) 32 (d) 2 → (b) 18 (since 2n² = 2×3² = 18)

Q2. Who discovered the neutron?

(a) Thomson (b) Rutherford (c) Chadwick (d) Bohr → (c) Chadwick (1932)

Q3. An element X has Z=11. Its valency is:

Configuration: 2, 8, 1. Outer e⁻ = 1 → loses 1 e⁻ → valency = 1

Q4. Which is NOT a fundamental particle?

(a) electron (b) proton (c) neutron (d) atom → (d) atom (it's made of particles)

Q5. Isotopes of an element have:

(a) Same A different Z (b) Same Z different A (c) Same A and Z (d) None → (b)

Q6. The discovery of the atomic nucleus came from:

(a) Cathode rays (b) Alpha scattering (c) X-rays (d) Photons → (b) Rutherford's gold foil experiment

Q7. Charge on a proton is:

(a) +1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C (b) −1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C (c) 0 (d) +1 C → (a)

Q8. The atomic number of an element is equal to:

(a) Number of neutrons (b) Number of protons (c) Total nucleons (d) Mass → (b)

Q9. Electronic configuration 2, 8, 7 corresponds to:

(a) Na (b) Cl (c) F (d) Ar → (b) Chlorine (Z=17)

Q10. The Bohr model fails to explain:

(a) Hydrogen spectrum (b) Atomic stability (c) Multi-electron atoms (d) Electron orbits → (c) Multi-electron atoms

⚛️ You've now mastered the Atom! Onwards to bigger discoveries!

📘 Section X: Definitions Quick Reference

Term Definition
AtomSmallest unit of an element retaining its properties.
NucleusTiny dense centre containing protons & neutrons.
ProtonPositively charged particle in the nucleus.
NeutronNeutral particle in the nucleus.
ElectronNegatively charged particle orbiting the nucleus.
Z (atomic number)Number of protons in an atom.
A (mass number)Sum of protons and neutrons.
ShellEnergy level where electrons orbit (K, L, M, N…).
ValencyCombining capacity based on outer electrons.
IsotopeSame Z, different A.
IsobarSame A, different Z.
IsotoneSame number of neutrons.
CationPositively charged ion (lost e⁻).
AnionNegatively charged ion (gained e⁻).
Octet ruleAtoms tend to have 8 e⁻ in outermost shell.
RadioactivitySpontaneous emission from unstable nuclei.
Half-lifeTime for half the sample to decay.
FissionSplitting heavy nuclei.
FusionCombining light nuclei.
QuarkSubatomic particle inside proton/neutron.

🎯 Section Y: Exam Tips

  • 📝 Always draw a clear atom diagram with labels — examiners love it.
  • 🔢 Practise writing electronic configurations for the first 20 elements until automatic.
  • 🧠 Memorise: H, He, Li, Be, B, C, N, O, F, Ne, Na, Mg, Al, Si, P, S, Cl, Ar, K, Ca.
  • 📏 Remember the formula 2n² for max electrons per shell.
  • ⚠️ Outermost shell can NEVER hold more than 8 electrons.
  • 💡 If asked "why noble gases are inert" → say octet is complete.
  • 🧪 For isotope problems, write the symbol carefully (mass number on top).
  • 📚 Read questions twice — distinguish between "atomic number" and "mass number".

🏁 Chapter 8 complete — confidence built atom by atom!

🔍 Section Z: Common Mistakes to Avoid

  • ❌ Confusing atomic number (Z) with mass number (A). Z = protons; A = protons + neutrons.
  • ❌ Writing more than 8 electrons in the outermost shell of the first 20 elements.
  • ❌ Mixing up isotopes (same Z) and isobars (same A).
  • ❌ Forgetting that electrons in a neutral atom = protons.
  • ❌ Saying "atoms are the smallest particles" — they have sub-particles!
  • ❌ Drawing electrons inside the nucleus — they orbit around it.
  • ❌ Forgetting Bohr model fails for atoms beyond hydrogen.
  • ❌ Confusing cations (+) with anions (−). "Cat-ions are pawsitive!"
  • ❌ Forgetting hydrogen has 1 proton but its isotopes can have 0, 1, or 2 neutrons.
  • ❌ Calling electrons "tiny planets" — they don't orbit in fixed paths in modern theory.

🌐 Section AA: Real-World Atom Applications

  • 🏥 Medicine: PET scans, X-rays, MRI use atomic principles.
  • 💡 LEDs: Electrons jumping between energy levels emit specific colours.
  • ⚡ Nuclear power: 25% of low-carbon electricity worldwide.
  • 📱 Semiconductors: Silicon atom behaviour powers ALL electronics.
  • 🔬 Electron microscopes: See objects 1,000,000× smaller than light microscopes.
  • 🛰️ Atomic clocks: GPS depends on caesium atom vibrations.
  • 🌍 Carbon dating: Determines age of ancient artefacts using C-14.
  • 💎 Lasers: Use stimulated emission from excited atoms.

⚛️ The world runs on atomic science. You now understand the foundation!

📝 Section AB: Self-Assessment Checklist

Tick each item as you master it:

  • ☐ I can name the three subatomic particles and their charges.
  • ☐ I can explain Dalton's atomic theory and its limitations.
  • ☐ I can describe Rutherford's gold foil experiment and conclusions.
  • ☐ I can explain Bohr's model with shells.
  • ☐ I can calculate Z, A, p, n, e⁻ for any atom.
  • ☐ I can write electronic configurations for the first 20 elements.
  • ☐ I can define and give examples of isotopes, isobars, isotones.
  • ☐ I can explain how ions form and write Na⁺, Cl⁻, Mg²⁺, O²⁻.
  • ☐ I can describe how an ionic bond forms in NaCl.
  • ☐ I can describe the octet rule and use it for predictions.
  • ☐ I can identify alpha, beta, gamma radiation.
  • ☐ I can solve half-life problems.
  • ☐ I can explain fission vs fusion.
  • ☐ I can list at least 3 Indian contributions to atomic science.
  • ☐ I can solve at least 8 of the 10 numerical problems unaided.

💪 Encouragement Corner

Atoms are tricky because they're invisible — but with diagrams and practice, you've mastered them! Every electronic device, medical scan, and chemistry reaction in your life depends on what you just learned. Be proud!

🎉 Chapter 8 fully covered with depth and care!

🔭 Section AC: Looking Ahead

What you'll learn next about atoms:

  • 📊 Class 10: The Periodic Table — how atoms arrange themselves, periodic trends.
  • 🧪 Class 11: Atomic orbitals (s, p, d, f), quantum numbers, modern atomic theory.
  • ⚛️ Class 12: Nuclear physics, photoelectric effect, dual nature of matter.
  • 🌌 College: Quantum mechanics, particle physics, antimatter, dark matter mysteries.

Atoms are your gateway to ALL of chemistry and modern physics!

✨ The journey from atoms to molecules to the universe continues!

🎓 Mastery Achieved!

You've now explored the building blocks of EVERYTHING in the universe. From the tiny atom to massive stars — they're all made of the same fundamental particles you just learned about.

Keep exploring, keep questioning, and remember: every great scientist started exactly where you are now — curious and willing to learn!

🌟 The universe is yours to discover, one atom at a time. 🌟

🏆 Chapter 8 Complete!

✨ Final Words on the Atom

Every star, every breath, every thought — atoms.

You now understand the universe at its most fundamental scale!

Keep curious. Keep learning. Keep wondering.

The next chapters await — onwards to Matter, Sound, and beyond!

⚛️ Atom — mastered!

🚀 Next: Matter in Our Surroundings →

⚗ Chapter 9: Atomic Foundations of Matter

Textbook: Exploration (New NCERT 2024-25) | 22 Pages | Topics: Law of Conservation of Mass, Law of Constant Proportions, Dalton's Atomic Theory, Chemical Bonding (Ionic & Covalent), Writing Chemical Formulas, Molecular Mass, Formula Unit Mass

9.1 Law of Conservation of Mass (Lavoisier, 1789)

💡 The Law:

"Mass can neither be created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction."
Total mass of reactants = Total mass of products

🔬 Activity 9.1: Investigating Physical Change

  1. Place a clean beaker on a digital weighing balance and set to zero
  2. Pour 50 mL water + add a spatula of common salt
  3. Record the mass before dissolving
  4. Swirl until salt dissolves completely
  5. Observation: Mass of solution = Mass of water + Mass of salt

Conclusion: No change in mass during physical change (dissolution).

🔬 Activity 9.2: Investigating Chemical Change

Set-up 1 (Open system):
  • Baking soda (NaHCO₃) + Vinegar in an open conical flask
  • Brisk effervescence occurs (CO₂ gas produced)
  • Result: Final mass < Initial mass (CO₂ escaped!)
Set-up 2 (Closed system - balloon on flask):
  • Same reaction but balloon covers the flask mouth (traps CO₂)
  • Result: Final mass = Initial mass ✅

⚠ Key Insight: In Set-up 1, mass decreased because CO₂ gas escaped. In Set-up 2 (closed system), all products are contained, so mass is conserved. The law holds true only when NO substance enters or leaves the system.

🔬 Activity 9.3: Precipitation Reaction

Sodium sulfate + Barium chloride → Barium sulfate (white precipitate) + Sodium chloride

  • Weigh both solutions before mixing
  • Mix them - white precipitate (BaSO₄) forms
  • Weigh after reaction
  • Result: Mass before = Mass after ✅ (no gas escapes in this reaction)

9.2 Law of Constant Proportions (Proust)

💡 The Law:

"In a chemical compound, the elements are always combined in a definite proportion by mass, regardless of the source."

Examples:

CompoundElementsFixed Mass RatioSource doesn't matter
Water (H₂O)Hydrogen : Oxygen1 : 8 by massRiver, borewell, ocean - always same
Carbon dioxide (CO₂)Carbon : Oxygen3 : 8 by massFrom burning, breathing, fermentation
Ammonia (NH₃)Nitrogen : Hydrogen14 : 3 by massNatural or synthetic - always same

📌 Note: Both laws together formed the basis of Dalton's Atomic Theory.

9.3 Dalton's Atomic Theory

Postulates of Dalton's Atomic Theory:

  1. All matter is made up of very tiny indivisible particles called atoms
  2. Atoms of the same element are identical in mass and properties
  3. Atoms of different elements are different in mass and properties
  4. Atoms cannot be created or destroyed in a chemical reaction (explains Conservation of Mass)
  5. Atoms combine in simple, fixed whole-number ratios to form compounds (explains Constant Proportions)
  6. In a chemical reaction, atoms are rearranged — they are neither created nor destroyed

⚠ Limitations of Dalton's Theory (now known):

  • Atoms ARE divisible (into electrons, protons, neutrons) — discovered later
  • Atoms of same element CAN have different masses (isotopes)
  • Atoms of different elements CAN have same mass (isobars)

However, the theory correctly explained the laws of chemical combination and laid the foundation for modern chemistry.

9.4 How Atoms Combine — Chemical Bonding

Atoms with 8 electrons in their valence shell (or 2 for K-shell) are stable (octet rule). To achieve stability, atoms can:

MethodType of BondBetweenExample
Share electronsCovalent bondNon-metal + Non-metalH₂, O₂, H₂O, CO₂
Transfer electronsIonic bondMetal + Non-metalNaCl, MgO, CaCl₂

9.4.1 Covalent Bonding (Sharing of Electrons)

Types of Covalent Bonds:

Bond TypeElectron Pairs SharedRepresentationExample
Single bond1 pair (2 electrons)A — BH₂, Cl₂, HCl, H₂O
Double bond2 pairs (4 electrons)A = BO₂, CO₂
Triple bond3 pairs (6 electrons)A ≡ BN₂

9.4.2 Ionic Bonding (Transfer of Electrons)

Ions and Their Formation:

TypeHow FormedChargeExamples
Cation (+)Metal atom LOSES electronsPositiveNa⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Al³⁺, Mg²⁺
Anion (-)Non-metal atom GAINS electronsNegativeCl⁻, O²⁻, S²⁻, N³⁻

Common Polyatomic Ions (groups of atoms with charge):

NameFormulaValency
HydroxideOH⁻1
NitrateNO₃⁻1
HydrogencarbonateHCO₃⁻1
CarbonateCO₃²⁻2
SulfateSO₄²⁻2
AmmoniumNH₄⁺1
PhosphatePO₄³⁻3

Properties of Ionic vs Covalent Compounds:

PropertyIonic CompoundsCovalent Compounds
Melting/Boiling pointHigh (strong ionic bonds)Low (weak intermolecular forces)
State at room tempUsually solidSolid, liquid, or gas
Conductivity (solid)No (ions fixed in lattice)No
Conductivity (dissolved/molten)Yes (ions free to move)No
Solubility in waterGenerally solubleOften insoluble

9.5 Writing Chemical Formulas

9.5.1 Naming Covalent Compounds (Prefixes)

For covalent compounds between non-metals, use prefixes to indicate number of atoms:

PrefixNumberExampleName
mono-1COCarbon monoxide
di-2CO₂Carbon dioxide
tri-3PCl₃Phosphorus trichloride
tetra-4CCl₄Carbon tetrachloride
penta-5P₂O₅Diphosphorus pentoxide
hexa-6SF₆Sulfur hexafluoride

Rules: First element keeps its name (mono- usually omitted). Second element ends in -ide.

9.5.2 Writing Formulas of Ionic Compounds (Crossover Method)

Steps:

  1. Write symbols of the two ions with their charges
  2. Crossover the charges (exchange the numbers, drop the signs)
  3. Write as subscripts; simplify if possible

⚠ Important Rules:

  • For polyatomic ions (like OH⁻, SO₄²⁻), put brackets when subscript > 1: e.g., Al(OH)₃ not AlOH₃
  • If both charges are same after crossover, simplify: Mg²⁺ and O²⁻ gives MgO (not Mg₂O₂)
  • Cation name comes first, anion name second (ends in -ide)

9.6 Molecular Mass & Formula Unit Mass

Atomic Mass Unit (u):

1 u = 1/12 the mass of a Carbon-12 atom

ElementSymbolAtomic Mass
HydrogenH1 u
CarbonC12 u
NitrogenN14 u
OxygenO16 u
SodiumNa23 u
MagnesiumMg24 u
AluminiumAl27 u
SulfurS32 u
ChlorineCl35.5 u
PotassiumK39 u
CalciumCa40 u

Molecular Mass (for covalent compounds):

= Sum of atomic masses of ALL atoms in the molecule

CompoundFormulaCalculationMolecular Mass
WaterH₂O2(1) + 1618 u
Carbon dioxideCO₂12 + 2(16)44 u
Nitric acidHNO₃1 + 14 + 3(16)63 u
Sulfuric acidH₂SO₄2(1) + 32 + 4(16)98 u
GlucoseC₆H₁₂O₆6(12) + 12(1) + 6(16)180 u

Formula Unit Mass (for ionic compounds):

Ionic compounds don't form molecules — they form formula units. Calculation is the same method.

CompoundFormulaCalculationFormula Unit Mass
Sodium chlorideNaCl23 + 35.558.5 u
Calcium oxideCaO40 + 1656 u
Calcium nitrateCa(NO₃)₂40 + 2{14 + 3(16)}164 u
Potassium chlorideKCl39 + 35.574.5 u
Magnesium hydroxideMg(OH)₂24 + 2(16+1)58 u

Important Questions & Answers (Textbook + Extra)

Q1: State the Law of Conservation of Mass. Describe an experiment to verify it.

Ans: "Mass can neither be created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction." To verify: Take baking soda and vinegar in a conical flask. Cover with a balloon (closed system). Weigh before and after reaction. Despite gas production, total mass remains constant because balloon traps all CO₂.

Q2: Calculate the molecular mass of: (a) HNO₃ (b) Ca(OH)₂ (c) Al₂(SO₄)₃

(a) HNO₃ = 1 + 14 + 3(16) = 1 + 14 + 48 = 63 u

(b) Ca(OH)₂ = 40 + 2(16+1) = 40 + 34 = 74 u

(c) Al₂(SO₄)₃ = 2(27) + 3{32 + 4(16)} = 54 + 3(96) = 54 + 288 = 342 u

Q3: Differentiate between ionic and covalent compounds.

Ionic CompoundsCovalent Compounds
Formed by transfer of electronsFormed by sharing of electrons
Between metals and non-metalsBetween non-metals only
High melting/boiling pointLow melting/boiling point
Conduct electricity in solution/moltenDo not conduct electricity
Example: NaCl, MgO, CaCl₂Example: H₂O, CO₂, CH₄

Q4: Write formulas using crossover method: (a) Magnesium nitride (b) Aluminium sulfate (c) Sodium oxide

(a) Mg²⁺ and N³⁻ → crossover → Mg₃N₂

(b) Al³⁺ and SO₄²⁻ → crossover → Al₂(SO₄)₃

(c) Na⁺ and O²⁻ → crossover → Na₂O

Q5: Element A (electronic config 2,8,1) reacts with element B (config 2,8,7). What type of bond forms? Write the formula.

Ans: A has 1 valence electron (metal, like Na) — will LOSE 1 electron to form A⁺. B has 7 valence electrons (non-metal, like Cl) — will GAIN 1 electron to form B⁻. They form an ionic bond. Formula: AB (one A⁺ combines with one B⁻).

Q6: Why do ionic compounds conduct electricity in solution but not in solid state?

Ans: In solid state, ions are fixed in a rigid crystal lattice and cannot move. In dissolved/molten state, the ions become free to move and can carry electric current. Movement of charged particles (ions) constitutes electric current.

🌊 Section A: What is Matter?

Matter is anything that has mass and occupies space (has volume).

Look around — air, water, food, your body, books, the chair you're sitting on — all are matter!

Even invisible things like air and water vapour are matter.

What is NOT matter?

Things that have NO mass and take NO space:

  • 💡 Light
  • 🔊 Sound
  • 🌡️ Heat
  • 💭 Thoughts, emotions, ideas
  • ⚡ Electricity (it's flow of electrons — but electricity itself isn't matter)

These are all forms of energy, not matter.

🔬 Section B: Particle Nature of Matter

All matter is made of TINY PARTICLES (atoms or molecules). These particles are:

  • Extremely SMALL (cannot be seen even with a microscope)
  • Always in MOTION
  • Have SPACES between them
  • ATTRACT each other

Activity 1: Dissolving Sugar

Put a spoon of sugar in a glass of water. Stir. Sugar disappears!

Why? Tiny sugar particles fit into the SPACES between water particles. Taste the water — it's sweet, so sugar is still there, just spread out!

Activity 2: The Smell Test

Open a perfume bottle in one corner of a room. Soon, the smell reaches everywhere!

Why? Perfume particles spread out (DIFFUSION) — they move and mix with air particles.

Activity 3: Hot Tea

Dropping a tea bag in HOT water makes tea quickly. In COLD water, it takes forever!

Why? Higher temperature → particles move faster → faster diffusion.

Characteristics of Particles

Characteristic Evidence
Have spaces between themSugar dissolves in water without overflow
Continuously movingSmells spread, ink spreads in water
Attract each otherWater drops stay round; solids hold shape
Very smallA drop of ink can colour a bucket of water

❄️💧💨 Section C: Three States of Matter

Matter exists in three common states based on how its particles are arranged and behave:

1. Solid — fixed shape and volume (e.g., ice, wood, iron)

2. Liquid — fixed volume but no fixed shape (e.g., water, oil, milk)

3. Gas — no fixed shape or volume (e.g., air, oxygen, water vapour)

Visual Comparison

Detailed Comparison Table

Property Solid Liquid Gas
ShapeFixedTakes container's shapeTakes container's shape
VolumeFixedFixedNot fixed (fills container)
DensityHighMediumVery low
CompressibilityNegligibleVery littleHigh
FluidityDoes not flowFlows easilyFlows fastest
Force of attractionStrongestMediumNegligible
Particle motionVibrate in placeMove around freelyMove randomly, very fast
Spaces between particlesVery smallLarger than solidsVery large
Diffusion rateNegligibleSlowVery fast
ExamplesIron, wood, iceWater, oil, milkAir, oxygen, steam

🔥❄️ Section D: Change of State

Matter can change from one state to another by either:

  • 🔥 Adding heat (increasing temperature)
  • ❄️ Removing heat (decreasing temperature)
  • 📈 Changing pressure

The Six State Changes

Change From → To Heat Example
Melting (Fusion)Solid → LiquidAbsorbedIce → Water
FreezingLiquid → SolidReleasedWater → Ice
VaporisationLiquid → GasAbsorbedWater → Steam
CondensationGas → LiquidReleasedSteam → Water
SublimationSolid → GasAbsorbedNaphthalene, dry ice
DepositionGas → SolidReleasedFrost on cold surface

Melting Point

The temperature at which a SOLID changes to a LIQUID at atmospheric pressure.

Example: Melting point of ice = 0°C = 273.15 K

The melting point of a substance is an indication of the strength of forces of attraction between its particles.

Boiling Point

The temperature at which a LIQUID begins to boil at atmospheric pressure.

At boiling point, the liquid changes to vapour throughout its bulk.

Example: Boiling point of water = 100°C = 373.15 K

Latent Heat

Latent means "hidden". Heat absorbed during a state change without raising the temperature is called latent heat.

Latent Heat of Fusion: heat needed to convert 1 kg of solid to liquid at its melting point.

For ice: 3.34 × 10⁵ J/kg

Latent Heat of Vaporisation: heat needed to convert 1 kg of liquid to gas at its boiling point.

For water: 22.6 × 10⁵ J/kg

This is why steam burns are MORE dangerous than boiling water burns!

🌡️ Section E: Temperature Scales

Two common temperature scales:

  • Celsius (°C): Water freezes at 0°C, boils at 100°C
  • Kelvin (K): SI unit. Water freezes at 273.15 K, boils at 373.15 K

Conversion:

  • K = °C + 273
  • °C = K − 273

Examples:

  • 25°C = 298 K (room temperature)
  • 0 K = −273°C (absolute zero — coldest possible)
  • 100°C = 373 K (boiling water)

💨 Section F: Evaporation

Evaporation: the change of a liquid to vapour at any temperature BELOW its boiling point.

Wet clothes dry even when the temperature is 20°C — much below water's boiling point!

Why? Some particles always have enough energy to escape as vapour, even at room temperature.

Factors Affecting Evaporation

  1. Surface area — bigger area = faster evaporation. (That's why we spread out wet clothes!)
  2. Temperature — hotter = faster evaporation.
  3. Humidity — high humidity = slower evaporation (air is already full of moisture).
  4. Wind speed — faster wind blows water vapour away, speeding evaporation.

Evaporation Causes Cooling

When a liquid evaporates, it ABSORBS heat from its surroundings → surroundings get cooler.

Examples in daily life:

  • 🥵 Sweating cools your body — sweat evaporates and removes heat.
  • 🏺 Water stays cool in earthen pots (matka) — water evaporates through pores.
  • 💧 Putting a wet cloth on a fever patient's forehead cools them down.
  • 🌬️ Splashing water on the floor in summer cools the house.
  • ❄️ Acetone (nail polish remover) feels cold on the skin — evaporates fast.

🌫️ Section G: Plasma and BEC — More States!

Besides solid, liquid, and gas, there are MORE states of matter:

Plasma — The 4th State

Super-heated gas with ionised particles. Found in:

  • ☀️ The Sun and all stars
  • 💡 Fluorescent tubes and neon signs
  • ⚡ Lightning
  • 🌌 Most matter in the universe (99%!)

Bose-Einstein Condensate (BEC) — The 5th State

Created at temperatures near ABSOLUTE ZERO (−273°C). Particles behave like a single super-atom.

Predicted by Indian physicist Satyendra Nath Bose and Albert Einstein in 1924, but first created in 1995.

BOSON particles are named after Bose!

🧮 Section H: Numerical Problems

P1. Convert 50°C to Kelvin.

K = 50 + 273 = 323 K

P2. Convert 200 K to Celsius.

°C = 200 − 273 = −73°C

P3. The melting point of mercury is −39°C. What is it in Kelvin?

K = −39 + 273 = 234 K

P4. Why is the boiling point of water lower on mountain tops?

Atmospheric pressure is LOWER at high altitudes. Lower pressure → liquid molecules escape more easily → lower boiling point.

P5. If room temperature is 25°C, what is it in Kelvin?

K = 25 + 273 = 298 K

📊 Section I: Physical and Chemical Properties

Physical Properties

Properties that can be observed WITHOUT changing the substance into something new.

  • 🎨 Colour
  • 👃 Smell
  • 🪨 Hardness
  • ⚖️ Density
  • 🌡️ Melting point, boiling point
  • 💧 Solubility
  • 🔌 Electrical conductivity
  • 🔥 Thermal conductivity
  • ✨ Lustre (shine)
  • 🔨 Malleability (can be hammered into sheets)
  • 🧵 Ductility (can be drawn into wires)

Chemical Properties

Properties that describe how a substance behaves in CHEMICAL REACTIONS.

  • 🔥 Flammability (burns or not)
  • 💨 Reactivity with air (rusting, tarnishing)
  • 💧 Reactivity with water
  • 🧪 Reactivity with acids
  • ⚗️ Combustion behaviour
  • 📉 Decomposition pattern

🌊 Section J: Pressure and Matter

Gases can be COMPRESSED easily because their particles have lots of space between them.

When pressure is increased on a gas → particles come closer → gas can change to LIQUID.

Examples:

  • 🚀 LPG (Liquefied Petroleum Gas): gas compressed under high pressure to become liquid in cylinders
  • 🏥 Oxygen cylinders in hospitals
  • 🥤 Carbon dioxide in fizzy drinks
  • 🧊 Dry ice (solid CO₂)

Activity: Compressing Gas

Take a syringe with NO needle. Suck in some air, close the tip with your finger, push the piston.

You can compress the air! Now try the same with WATER — you can't compress it!

Conclusion: Gases are compressible; liquids and solids are not.

🍵 Section K: Diffusion in Detail

Diffusion is the intermixing of particles of one substance into another due to their constant random motion.

Diffusion in Gases (Fastest!)

  • Smell of agarbatti/incense spreads quickly across a room
  • Smell of food from kitchen reaches the dining room
  • Perfume scents spread rapidly

Diffusion in Liquids

  • Drop of ink in water — colour spreads slowly
  • Sugar dissolves in tea evenly
  • Dettol added to water spreads

Diffusion in Solids (Very Slow)

  • Welding two metal pieces — atoms diffuse at the joint
  • Carbon diffusion into iron makes steel
  • Salt diffuses through pickle vegetables over weeks

Why is diffusion important?

  • 🫁 Oxygen diffuses from lungs to blood
  • 🌳 Plants absorb water and minerals through diffusion
  • 🐟 Fish breathe — oxygen dissolves in water and diffuses into gills
  • 🌸 Pollination — flower scents attract bees via diffusion

🔬 Section L: How Heat Changes States

Heat is a form of ENERGY. When you heat a substance:

  1. Particles GAIN kinetic energy
  2. Particles MOVE FASTER
  3. Spaces between particles INCREASE
  4. Forces of attraction WEAKEN
  5. Substance may change state

Heating Curve of Water (Most Important!)

Notice: temperature stays CONSTANT during melting and boiling — heat is being used as LATENT HEAT to change state, not to raise temperature!

🌍 Section M: Water — A Special Substance

Water is unique because it exists in all 3 states naturally on Earth:

  • ❄️ Solid (ice in poles, mountains)
  • 💧 Liquid (oceans, rivers, lakes)
  • 💨 Gas (water vapour in atmosphere)

Anomalous Expansion of Water

Most substances CONTRACT when cooled. But water EXPANDS when frozen!

This is why:

  • 🧊 Ice floats on water
  • 🐟 Fish survive in frozen lakes — ice on top, liquid water beneath
  • 🌊 Pipes burst in winter when water freezes inside them
  • 🪨 Rocks crack in cold areas — water seeps in, freezes, expands

📝 Section N: Practice Questions

Q1. Why do we sweat more in summer?

Higher temperature makes us hotter. Sweating cools us because the sweat evaporates, absorbing heat from our body.

Q2. Why does a desert cooler cool better on a hot dry day?

Low humidity + high temperature → faster evaporation of water → more cooling effect.

Q3. Why do we wear cotton clothes in summer?

Cotton absorbs sweat easily and has small pores that let sweat evaporate quickly → keeps us cool.

Q4. Why does steam cause more severe burns than boiling water at the same temperature?

Steam has extra LATENT HEAT (released when it condenses back to water on the skin). This extra heat causes severe burns.

Q5. What is dry ice? Why is it called so?

Dry ice is solid carbon dioxide (CO₂). It's "dry" because it changes directly from solid to gas (sublimation) — no liquid phase, hence "dry".

Q6. Define melting point and boiling point.

Melting point: temp at which a solid changes to liquid. Boiling point: temp at which a liquid changes to gas.

Q7. What is the difference between evaporation and boiling?

Evaporation happens at ANY temperature on the SURFACE only. Boiling happens at a SPECIFIC temperature (boiling point) THROUGHOUT the liquid.

Q8. Why are gases easily compressible while solids are not?

Gas particles have HUGE spaces between them, so they can be pushed closer. Solid particles are already tightly packed.

📋 Section O: MCQs

M1. Which has highest compressibility?

(a) Solid (b) Liquid (c) Gas (d) All equal → (c) Gas

M2. 25°C in Kelvin?

(a) 298 K (b) 248 K (c) 25 K (d) 273 K → (a) 298 K

M3. Naphthalene balls disappear over time because of:

(a) Melting (b) Evaporation (c) Sublimation (d) Diffusion → (c) Sublimation

M4. The intermixing of two gases is called:

(a) Sublimation (b) Diffusion (c) Evaporation (d) Condensation → (b) Diffusion

M5. Which one is NOT matter?

(a) Water (b) Cloud (c) Smoke (d) Light → (d) Light

M6. The fifth state of matter is:

(a) Solid (b) Plasma (c) BEC (d) Vapour → (c) BEC

M7. Heat absorbed without temperature rise is:

(a) Specific heat (b) Latent heat (c) Kinetic energy (d) Heat capacity → (b) Latent heat

M8. The unit of temperature in SI is:

(a) °C (b) °F (c) K (d) R → (c) Kelvin (K)

🇮🇳 Section P: Indian Science Connect

S.N. Bose (1894–1974)

Indian physicist whose work led to the discovery of bosons (named after him) and Bose-Einstein statistics.

His collaboration with Einstein predicted the Bose-Einstein Condensate — the FIFTH state of matter!

Despite never winning a Nobel Prize, his name lives on in every particle physics textbook.

📘 Section Q: Glossary

Term Meaning
MatterAnything with mass and volume
DiffusionIntermixing of particles
EvaporationLiquid → gas below boiling point
SublimationSolid → gas directly
DepositionGas → solid directly
MeltingSolid → liquid
FreezingLiquid → solid
CondensationGas → liquid
Latent heatHidden heat during state change
Boiling pointTemp where liquid → gas
Melting pointTemp where solid → liquid
PlasmaSuper-heated ionised gas
BECUltra-cold quantum state
Absolute zero0 K = −273°C

💧 Matter mastery growing strong!

🌡️ Section R: Effect of Pressure on Boiling Point

Boiling point depends on PRESSURE:

  • 📈 Higher pressure → higher boiling point
  • 📉 Lower pressure → lower boiling point

Pressure Cooker Magic

In a pressure cooker, steam can't escape → pressure builds up → water boils at HIGHER temperature (about 120°C).

Food cooks FASTER because of this higher temperature!

Mountain Cooking Challenge

On Mount Everest, atmospheric pressure is much lower → water boils at only ~70°C.

This means rice and dal take much longer to cook on high mountains — they need a pressure cooker!

🔍 Section S: Activities to Try at Home

Activity 1: Compress a Sponge

Take a sponge. Squeeze it — it shrinks because of the AIR (gas) inside its pores being compressed!

This proves gases can be compressed easily.

Activity 2: Crystals on a String

Dissolve as much sugar as possible in hot water. Hang a string in it. As water evaporates over days, beautiful sugar crystals form on the string!

This shows how dissolved solid particles come together as water leaves.

Activity 3: Make a Mini Cloud

Boil water in a bottle. Cover the mouth with an ice cube. Watch as a mini cloud forms inside!

The water vapour rises, hits the cold air near the ice, and CONDENSES back to water droplets — just like real clouds form!

Activity 4: Make a Lava Lamp Effect

Fill a glass with oil and water. Add food colouring and an effervescent tablet.

Watch coloured bubbles rise and fall! The CO₂ released attaches to coloured water and floats up. When it pops at the top, water sinks back down.

🧪 Section T: More Numerical Practice

P6. Convert 373 K to Celsius.

°C = 373 − 273 = 100°C (boiling point of water!)

P7. Convert −40°C to Kelvin.

K = −40 + 273 = 233 K

P8. Why is steam at 100°C more dangerous than water at 100°C?

Steam has additional latent heat of vaporisation (22.6 × 10⁵ J/kg). When it touches skin, it condenses and releases this huge amount of heat plus the burn from the high temperature.

P9. Arrange the following in increasing order of particle motion: solid copper, water, oxygen gas, ice.

Ice ≈ solid copper (vibrate in place) < water (flow) < oxygen gas (move very fast)

P10. A solid has fixed volume but a liquid does not. True or false?

FALSE. Both solids and liquids have FIXED volume. Only the SHAPE of liquid changes.

🌍 Section U: Daily Life Applications

  • 🧊 Refrigerators: use evaporation cooling — refrigerant evaporates and absorbs heat from food.
  • 🚿 Sweating: body's natural cooling system.
  • 🏺 Earthen pots: water seeps through pores, evaporates, and cools the inside.
  • 🚀 Rockets: use compressed gases.
  • 🥶 Air conditioners: same principle as refrigerators.
  • 🍳 Pressure cookers: high pressure → higher boiling point → faster cooking.
  • 🥶 Ice packs for injuries: cold compress to reduce swelling.
  • 💨 Aerosol sprays: gas under pressure released as fine spray.
  • 💧 Distillation: separating mixtures using evaporation & condensation.

📋 Section V: Important Formulas

📌 K = °C + 273

📌 °C = K − 273

📌 Density = Mass ÷ Volume

📌 Latent heat absorbed = m × L (where m=mass, L=latent heat)

📌 Latent heat of fusion of ice = 3.34 × 10⁵ J/kg

📌 Latent heat of vaporisation of water = 22.6 × 10⁵ J/kg

📌 1 atmosphere = 1.01 × 10⁵ Pa (Pascal)

🌟 Section W: Fun Facts

  • 🌌 99% of all matter in the universe is in the PLASMA state (stars, lightning, fluorescent tubes).
  • ❄️ Solid water (ice) is LESS dense than liquid water — that's why ice floats!
  • 🌡️ The coldest temperature ever recorded in nature was −89°C in Antarctica.
  • 🔥 Lava can reach temperatures of 1200°C — hot enough to melt many metals.
  • 🌊 Helium is the ONLY substance that doesn't solidify at normal pressure, no matter how cold.
  • 🥶 Liquid nitrogen boils at −196°C — used to flash-freeze food and preserve cells.
  • 💧 Water expands 9% when freezing — that's why frozen bottles burst!
  • 🧊 Dry ice is colder than regular ice (−78°C vs 0°C).

🌊 Matter — understood in solid, liquid, gas, and beyond!

📝 Section X: Common Mistakes

  • ❌ Saying "smoke is not matter". Smoke is matter (particles of solid in gas).
  • ❌ Confusing evaporation with boiling. Evaporation: surface, any temp. Boiling: throughout, specific temp.
  • ❌ Forgetting that temperature stays constant during state change.
  • ❌ Thinking ice has same density as water — it's LESS dense.
  • ❌ Saying "gas has no mass". Gas DOES have mass, just very low density.
  • ❌ Mixing up sublimation and deposition (opposite processes).
  • ❌ Forgetting K = °C + 273 conversion.
  • ❌ Calling plasma "the third state" — it's the FOURTH.

🎯 Section Y: Exam Tips

  • 📊 Always draw and label the comparison diagram (solid-liquid-gas particle arrangements).
  • 🌡️ Memorise K = °C + 273 formula.
  • 📋 Make a comparison table for solid, liquid, gas — it's a frequent question.
  • 💡 Explain evaporation with at least 2 examples from daily life.
  • 🧪 Practice converting between Celsius and Kelvin.
  • 📝 Define melting point and boiling point precisely.
  • 🔬 Mention sublimation examples (naphthalene, dry ice, camphor).
  • 👨‍🔬 Remember S.N. Bose and the BEC state.

📚 Section Z: Self-Check Quiz

Tick when you understand:

  • ☐ I can define matter and list 5 characteristics of particles.
  • ☐ I can compare solids, liquids, gases in a table.
  • ☐ I can name and explain 6 state changes.
  • ☐ I can convert °C to K and back.
  • ☐ I can explain why evaporation causes cooling.
  • ☐ I can list 4 factors affecting evaporation.
  • ☐ I can give 5 daily-life examples of state changes.
  • ☐ I know about plasma and BEC.
  • ☐ I can solve numerical conversions.
  • ☐ I can explain the heating curve of water.

💧 Final Words on Matter

From the water you drink to the air you breathe — everything is matter. Now you understand how it behaves, why it changes states, and why steam cooks rice faster!

Next chapter: SOUND — the matter waves you hear every day!

🏁 Chapter 9 — Matter mastered!

🌐 Section AA: Matter in Cosmic Context

Where can different states of matter be found in the universe?

  • ⭐ Stars (Sun): Plasma — superheated ionised gas at millions of degrees.
  • 🪐 Gas giants (Jupiter): Mostly hydrogen and helium gas/liquid.
  • 🌍 Earth: Solids, liquids, gases — all 3 states naturally!
  • 🌑 Moon: Mostly solid rock; no atmosphere.
  • ☄️ Comets: Solid ice/rock that sublimates near the Sun.
  • 🌟 Nebulae: Massive clouds of gas and dust.
  • ⚫ Neutron stars: Ultra-dense matter — a teaspoon weighs millions of tonnes!
  • 🌌 Black holes: Matter compressed beyond comprehension.

🔬 Section AB: Modern Discoveries

Supercritical Fluids

At very high pressure and temperature, the difference between liquid and gas disappears! The substance becomes a "supercritical fluid" — used in industrial processes like decaffeinating coffee.

Quantum States

At extreme cold (near 0 K), matter behaves in bizarre ways — superconductors carry electricity with NO resistance, and superfluids flow without friction.

Time Crystals (2016)

A newly discovered state where atoms move in repeating patterns through TIME, not just space! Could revolutionise quantum computing.

🌳 Section AC: Matter Cycles in Nature

Water Cycle

  1. ☀️ Sun heats water → EVAPORATION (ocean → vapour)
  2. ☁️ Vapour rises and cools → CONDENSATION (forms clouds)
  3. 🌧️ Clouds release water → PRECIPITATION (rain, snow)
  4. 🌊 Water flows back to oceans

This cycle is a perfect example of state changes happening continuously in nature!

Other Natural Cycles

  • 🌫️ Morning dew — gas to liquid (condensation)
  • ❄️ Frost on grass — gas to solid (deposition)
  • 🌨️ Snow forming — liquid to solid
  • 🌫️ Fog forming — gas to liquid in air

🌊 Beautiful matter behaviours around us every moment!

🎓 Section AD: Long Answer Questions

L1. Describe an experiment to show that particles of matter have spaces between them.

Take 100 mL of water in a glass. Add 30 g of salt and stir. Salt dissolves but water level remains nearly the same! This proves salt particles fit into spaces between water particles.

L2. Explain how a liquid changes to gas with the help of a heating curve.

As heat is added, temperature rises until boiling point. Then temperature stays constant (latent heat absorbed) until ALL liquid becomes gas. Then temperature rises again as the gas heats up.

L3. Why does dew form in early morning?

At night, ground cools down. Water vapour in air touches the cold surface, loses heat, and CONDENSES into water droplets — that's dew!

L4. Compare the kinetic energy of particles in solids, liquids, and gases.

Solid: lowest KE (only vibrate). Liquid: higher KE (move around). Gas: highest KE (move very fast in all directions). Higher temperature increases KE.

L5. Why do gases exert pressure on the walls of their container?

Gas particles move randomly at high speed. They constantly collide with the walls of the container. These collisions exert force per unit area = PRESSURE.

L6. Why does our hand feel cold when we put acetone or nail polish remover on it?

Acetone has a very low boiling point. It evaporates quickly, absorbing heat from our skin → skin feels cold.

🌟 Section AE: Encouragement

Everything around you — your phone, your food, your toys, your body — is MATTER. You now understand the rules that govern how all of it behaves!

Every great chemist and physicist started by understanding matter. You're walking the same path. Keep wondering, keep experimenting at home (safely!), and ask LOTS of questions.

🌟 Curiosity is the most powerful tool a scientist has — and you have it in abundance!

🎯 Chapter 9 — Complete and confident!

🔎 Section AF: Worked Examples

Example 1: A liquid X melts at 50°C and boils at 200°C. What is X at room temperature (25°C)?

Since 25°C is BELOW its melting point of 50°C, X must be a SOLID at room temperature.

Example 2: A gas is cooled from 100°C until it becomes a liquid. Name the process.

This is CONDENSATION (gas to liquid).

Example 3: Why does food cook faster in a pressure cooker?

Higher pressure inside → higher boiling point of water (~120°C) → food cooks at higher temperature → faster cooking.

Example 4: What makes solids hold their shape?

STRONG forces of attraction between particles. Particles can only vibrate, not move around freely.

Example 5: Why does ice float on water?

Ice is LESS DENSE than water due to its unique crystal structure (anomalous expansion). Less dense things float on denser ones.

💧 Excellent progress! Ready for the next chapter!

🔭 Section AG: Looking Ahead

What you'll explore next about matter:

  • 📊 Class 10: Acids, bases, salts, metals & non-metals
  • 🧪 Class 11: Behaviour of gases (ideal gas law), thermodynamics
  • ⚛️ Class 12: Solutions, solid state chemistry, surface chemistry
  • 🌌 College: Quantum mechanics of matter, condensed matter physics, materials science

The journey of understanding matter continues for a lifetime!

🌟 You've Got This!

From understanding why ice floats to knowing why steam burns more than water — you've mastered the fundamental behaviour of matter. Onwards to SOUND!

🎓 Final Recap

  • Matter = anything with mass and volume
  • Three common states: solid, liquid, gas
  • Particles: tiny, moving, attracting, with spaces
  • State changes: melting, freezing, vaporisation, condensation, sublimation, deposition
  • Evaporation causes cooling
  • Plasma (4th) and BEC (5th) are extreme states
  • K = °C + 273

🌊 Chapter 9 — Mastered!

🔊 Next: Sound waves all around us!

🎉 Matter — fully understood!

✨ Onwards to the symphony of Sound!

🚀 Keep going strong!

🌟 You're doing great!

🏁 Matter chapter complete!

🔊 Sound is next!

🔊 Chapter 10: Sound Waves — Characteristics and Applications

Textbook: Exploration (New NCERT 2024-25) | 24 Pages | Topics: Production of Sound, Need for Medium, Longitudinal Waves, Compressions & Rarefactions, Wavelength, Frequency, Amplitude, Speed of Sound, Reflection (Echo), Ultrasound, SONAR, Human Ear

10.1 Production of Sound

💡 Key Concept:

Sound is produced by vibrating objects.
When vibration stops, sound stops.

🔬 Activity 10.1: Rubber Band on Box

  1. Stretch a rubber band across the open side of a cardboard box
  2. Pluck the rubber band → you hear sound AND see it vibrating
  3. Wait till vibration stops → sound also stops
  4. Change tension → different sound (pitch changes)

Conclusion: Sound is produced by vibration. No vibration = No sound.

🔬 Tuning Fork (Activity 10.2):

A tuning fork is a U-shaped metal bar with a stem. When you strike its prongs on a rubber pad:

  • Prongs vibrate rapidly → produce a steady sound of specific frequency
  • Touch vibrating prong to water surface → water splashes (proves vibration)
  • Touch vibrating prong to a hanging ping-pong ball → ball bounces away

Other sources: Vocal cords (voice), Drum membrane, Guitar strings, Loudspeaker cone, Temple bell

10.2 Sound Needs a Medium to Travel

Sound CANNOT travel through vacuum.
It needs a material medium (solid, liquid, or gas) to propagate.

Proof - Bell Jar Experiment:

  • Place an electric bell inside a sealed glass jar
  • Start the bell → you can hear it ringing
  • Gradually pump out air from the jar
  • Sound becomes fainter and fainter
  • When most air is removed → bell is still vibrating but NO sound heard!
  • Let air back in → sound returns

Conclusion: Sound needs air (medium) to travel. No medium = No sound propagation.

This is why astronauts cannot talk directly in space - they use radio waves (electromagnetic - don't need medium).

Sound travels through all three states:

MediumExperimentObservation
SolidScratch one end of table, ear on other endSound heard clearly through table
LiquidTap spoons underwater, listen with ear submergedSound travels through water
Gas (Air)Normal conversationSound travels through air

10.3 Sound is a Longitudinal Wave

How Sound Propagates:

  1. Vibrating object pushes nearby air particles → Compression (high density region)
  2. Object moves back → particles spread out → Rarefaction (low density region)
  3. Alternating C and R regions travel outward as a wave
  4. Particles vibrate back and forth (parallel to wave direction) → Longitudinal wave
  5. Individual particles do NOT travel - only the disturbance/energy travels forward

📌 Key difference: In a transverse wave (like light), particles vibrate perpendicular to wave direction. Sound is longitudinal.

10.4 Characteristics of a Sound Wave

Key Quantities:

QuantitySymbolDefinitionSI Unit
Wavelengthλ (lambda)Distance between 2 consecutive compressions (or 2 rarefactions)metre (m)
Frequencyν (nu) or fNumber of vibrations per secondHertz (Hz) = s⁻¹
Time PeriodTTime for one complete vibrationsecond (s)
AmplitudeAMaximum displacement from mean positionmetre (m)

T = 1/f     |     v = f × λ     (Speed = Frequency × Wavelength)

What determines what we hear?

PerceptionDepends OnRelationshipExample
Pitch (shrill vs bass)FrequencyHigher frequency = Higher pitch (shrill)Woman's voice (high pitch) vs Man's voice (low pitch)
Loudness (loud vs soft)AmplitudeLarger amplitude = Louder soundShouting (large A) vs Whispering (small A)
Quality / TimbreWaveform shapeDifferent overtone patternsSitar vs Guitar playing same note sound different

Loudness in Decibels (dB):

SoundLoudness (dB)Effect
Breathing / Rustling leaves10 dBBarely audible
Normal conversation60 dBComfortable
Busy traffic70 dBAnnoying
Factory / Loud music80-90 dBHarmful over time
Jet engine at close range120+ dBPain threshold, ear damage

⚠ Above 80 dB for prolonged time can cause hearing damage!

Tone vs Note:

ToneNote
Sound of single frequencyCombination of fundamental + overtones
Simple, pure soundRich, pleasant, musical sound
Tuning fork, whistleSitar, singing, piano

10.5 Speed of Sound

Speed of Sound = Frequency × Wavelength
v = ν × λ

MediumSpeed (m/s)Why?
Air (at 0°C)331 m/sParticles far apart, loose bonds
Air (at 22°C)344 m/s
Water (25°C)1498 m/sParticles closer than gas
Steel5960 m/sParticles very close, strong bonds

Speed in Solids > Liquids > Gases

Why? In solids, particles are closest together and have strongest bonds → vibrations pass fastest.

Temperature effect: Speed increases with temperature (warmer = particles move faster = faster transmission).

📝 Solved Examples:

Example 10.1: Calculate the wavelength of sound in air for frequencies (i) 20 Hz and (ii) 20,000 Hz. Speed of sound = 344 m/s.

λ = v/f

(i) λ = 344/20 = 17.2 m

(ii) λ = 344/20000 = 0.0172 m = 1.72 cm

Notice: Low frequency = very long wavelength (17.2 m!), High frequency = tiny wavelength (1.72 cm)

10.6 Audible Range & Beyond

RangeFrequencyHeard byUses
Infrasound< 20 HzElephants, whales, rhinosEarthquake detection, volcano monitoring
Audible (Human)20 Hz - 20,000 HzHumansSpeech, music, alarms
Ultrasound> 20,000 HzBats, dogs, dolphinsMedical imaging, SONAR, cleaning

Note: Hearing range decreases with age. Many adults cannot hear above 16,000 Hz.

10.7 Reflection of Sound — Echo & Reverberation

Echo:

An echo is a reflected sound heard distinctly after the original sound.

Minimum distance for echo = 17.2 m (from reflecting surface)
Total path = 2 × 17.2 = 34.4 m   |   Time gap needed: ≥ 0.1 seconds

How to calculate:

For echo to be heard separately, reflected sound must reach ear at least 0.1 s after original.

In 0.1 s, sound travels: 344 × 0.1 = 34.4 m (total there and back)

So minimum distance to wall = 34.4/2 = 17.2 m

Reverberation:

In a small room, reflected sound reaches ear in less than 0.1 s → mixes with original → called reverberation (sound seems to last longer/buzz).

Too much reverberation → unclear speech in halls. Solutions:

  • Sound-absorbing materials on walls (curtains, carpets, acoustic panels)
  • Proper hall design (curved ceilings, shaped walls)

Fun fact: The Gol Gumbaz in Bijapur, Karnataka has a famous "Whispering Gallery" where a whisper can be heard multiple times across the large dome due to multiple reflections!

📝 Solved Example:

A person claps near a cliff and hears the echo after 3 seconds. Speed of sound = 344 m/s. How far is the cliff?

Total distance traveled by sound = v × t = 344 × 3 = 1032 m

Distance to cliff = 1032 / 2 = 516 m

(Divide by 2 because sound travels TO the cliff and BACK)

10.8 Applications of Ultrasound & SONAR

Applications of Ultrasound:

ApplicationHow it Works
Medical imaging (Ultrasonography)Ultrasound waves bounce off organs/fetus → creates image on screen. Safe (no radiation unlike X-rays)
EchocardiographyUltrasound to examine heart structure and function
CleaningUltrasonic vibrations shake off dirt from jewelry, lenses, surgical instruments
Detecting cracksSend ultrasound through metal → cracks reflect waves → found without cutting open
Breaking kidney stonesFocused ultrasound shatters kidney stones (lithotripsy)

📝 Solved Example (SONAR):

A naval sonar signal sent into seawater returns after 0.90 s. Speed of sound in seawater = 1530 m/s. Find distance of underwater object.

d = (v × t) / 2 = (1530 × 0.90) / 2 = 1377 / 2 = 688.5 m

10.9 Structure of the Human Ear

Step-by-step: How we hear

  1. Pinna (outer ear flap) collects sound waves from surroundings and funnels them into ear canal
  2. Sound waves travel through ear canal and hit the eardrum (a thin membrane)
  3. Eardrum vibrates at the same frequency as the sound wave
  4. Three tiny bones (hammer, anvil, stirrup) amplify these vibrations by ~20 times
  5. Amplified vibrations reach the cochlea (fluid-filled, snail-shaped organ)
  6. Fluid vibrations bend tiny hair cells → generates electrical signals
  7. Auditory nerve carries signals to brain, which interprets them as sound

Important Questions & Answers

Q1: Why can we not hear sound on the Moon?

Ans: Moon has no atmosphere (no air/gas). Since sound is a mechanical wave that needs a material medium to propagate (through compressions and rarefactions of particles), it cannot travel in vacuum. Hence, no sound on the Moon.

Q2: A sound wave has frequency 500 Hz and speed 344 m/s. Find wavelength and time period.

λ = v/f = 344/500 = 0.688 m = 68.8 cm

T = 1/f = 1/500 = 0.002 s = 2 ms

Q3: Distinguish between loudness and pitch.

LoudnessPitch
Depends on amplitudeDepends on frequency
How loud/soft the sound isHow high/low (shrill/bass) the sound is
Measured in decibels (dB)Measured in Hertz (Hz)
Larger amplitude = louderHigher frequency = higher pitch
Example: Shouting vs whisperingExample: Flute (high) vs drum (low)

Q4: Why is the speed of sound greatest in solids and least in gases?

Ans: Speed of sound depends on how quickly vibrations can pass from one particle to the next. In solids, particles are closest together and have strongest inter-particle forces, so vibrations transfer fastest. In gases, particles are far apart with weak forces, making sound transmission slowest.

Q5: A person standing in a room 10 m from a wall claps. Will they hear an echo? Why?

Ans: No. For an echo, minimum distance to reflecting surface = 17.2 m. Here the wall is only 10 m away. The reflected sound will reach the ear in less than 0.1 s, mixing with the original sound (reverberation, not echo).

Q6: If 20 compressions pass a point in 4 seconds, what is the frequency?

Frequency = Number of vibrations / Time = 20/4 = 5 Hz

🔊 Section A: What is Sound?

Sound is a form of energy that produces the sensation of HEARING in our ears.

Sound is produced by the VIBRATION of objects.

Examples of vibrating objects producing sound:

  • 🎸 Guitar strings vibrate when plucked
  • 🥁 Drum membrane vibrates when struck
  • 🔔 Bell vibrates when rung
  • 🗣️ Your vocal cords vibrate when you speak
  • 🦗 Insect wings vibrate creating buzz

Activity 1: Feel the Vibration

Place your fingers on your throat and speak loudly. Feel the vibrations of your vocal cords!

Strike a tuning fork on a hard surface and bring it close to a ping-pong ball hanging by a string — the ball jumps! This proves vibrations.

🌊 Section B: How Sound Travels

Sound needs a MEDIUM (something to travel through) — air, water, or any solid.

Sound travels in the form of WAVES.

The Vibrating Source Creates Waves

When an object vibrates, it pushes air particles around it. These particles bump into nearby particles, creating a chain reaction — like dominoes!

This creates regions of:

  • Compression (C): particles pushed close together — HIGH pressure
  • Rarefaction (R): particles spread apart — LOW pressure

Sound Wave Diagram

🎵 Section C: Longitudinal vs Transverse Waves

Sound waves are LONGITUDINAL waves.

Two types of mechanical waves:

Property Longitudinal (Sound) Transverse (Light, Water Surface)
Particle motionParallel to wave directionPerpendicular to wave direction
ShapeCompressions and rarefactionsCrests and troughs
ExamplesSound, slinky pushLight, water waves
Medium needed?YesOnly mechanical ones

📏 Section D: Properties of Sound Waves

1. Wavelength (λ)

The distance between two consecutive compressions OR two consecutive rarefactions.

Unit: metre (m). Symbol: lambda (λ).

2. Frequency (f or ν)

The number of complete oscillations (waves) per SECOND.

Unit: Hertz (Hz). 1 Hz = 1 vibration per second.

Named after physicist Heinrich Hertz.

3. Time Period (T)

The time taken for ONE complete oscillation.

Unit: second (s).

Relationship: T = 1/f and f = 1/T

4. Amplitude (A)

The maximum displacement from the rest position.

Bigger amplitude = LOUDER sound.

5. Speed (v)

How fast the wave travels.

Unit: m/s.

Key formula: v = f × λ (speed = frequency × wavelength)

🚀 Section E: Speed of Sound in Different Media

Medium Temperature Speed (m/s)
Air (gas)0°C331
Air25°C346
Hydrogen0°C1284
Water (fresh)25°C1493
Sea water25°C1533
Glass25°C4540
Aluminium25°C5100
Iron25°C5950

Key observation: Sound travels FASTEST in SOLIDS, slower in liquids, slowest in gases!

Why? Particles are closely packed in solids, so vibrations transfer quickly.

🚫 Section F: Sound Cannot Travel in Vacuum

Sound needs particles to travel. In a VACUUM (no particles), there's nothing to vibrate!

Bell Jar Experiment (Robert Boyle, 1660)

An electric bell is placed inside a glass jar connected to a vacuum pump.

  • Air present: You can HEAR the bell ringing.
  • Vacuum pump removes air: Sound becomes FAINTER...
  • Complete vacuum: NO SOUND, even though you can SEE the bell ringing!

This proves sound CANNOT travel through vacuum.

Why no sound in space?

Space is mostly empty — no particles, no sound. That's why explosions in space are SILENT in real life (movies lie!).

🎼 Section G: Pitch, Loudness, and Quality

Pitch (depends on FREQUENCY)

Pitch is how SHRILL or DEEP a sound is.

  • High frequency → HIGH pitch (shrill, like a whistle)
  • Low frequency → LOW pitch (deep, like a drum)

Examples:

  • 👶 Baby's voice: high pitch
  • 👨 Man's voice: low pitch
  • 👩 Woman's voice: medium-high pitch
  • 🦁 Lion's roar: low pitch
  • 🐦 Bird's chirp: high pitch

Loudness (depends on AMPLITUDE)

Loudness is how INTENSE the sound feels.

  • Big amplitude → LOUD sound
  • Small amplitude → SOFT sound

Unit of loudness: decibel (dB).

Examples:

  • 🤫 Whisper: 30 dB
  • 🗣️ Normal conversation: 60 dB
  • 🚗 Traffic: 80 dB
  • 🎸 Rock concert: 110 dB
  • ✈️ Jet plane: 140 dB (painful!)

Above 80 dB for long time can damage hearing!

Quality / Timbre

Quality (timbre) distinguishes between two sounds of same pitch and loudness.

Why does a flute sound different from a guitar even when playing the same note?

Because they have DIFFERENT timbres (overtones and harmonics)!

📊 Section H: Audible Range of Sound

Humans can hear sounds with frequencies between 20 Hz and 20,000 Hz.

Three Categories

  • Infrasonic: BELOW 20 Hz. Too low for humans. Elephants, whales use these.
  • Audible: 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz. What we can hear.
  • Ultrasonic: ABOVE 20,000 Hz. Too high for humans. Bats, dolphins use these.

Animal Hearing Ranges

Animal Range (Hz)
Human20 – 20,000
Dog40 – 60,000
Cat100 – 65,000
Bat1,000 – 1,20,000
Dolphin75 – 1,50,000
Elephant5 – 12,000

🧮 Section I: Numerical Problems

P1. A sound wave has frequency 200 Hz and wavelength 1.5 m. Find its speed.

v = f × λ = 200 × 1.5 = 300 m/s

P2. Speed of sound in air = 340 m/s. Find wavelength if frequency is 1700 Hz.

λ = v/f = 340/1700 = 0.2 m

P3. Find time period if frequency is 50 Hz.

T = 1/f = 1/50 = 0.02 s

P4. A man hears thunder 5 seconds after seeing lightning. How far is the storm? (Speed of sound = 340 m/s)

Distance = speed × time = 340 × 5 = 1700 m

P5. Frequency = 400 Hz. Find time period.

T = 1/400 = 0.0025 s = 2.5 ms

🏔️ Section J: Reflection of Sound

Sound waves can BOUNCE BACK from a hard, smooth surface — just like light bounces off mirrors!

This is called reflection of sound.

Laws of Reflection (Same as light!)

  1. The angle of incidence = the angle of reflection
  2. Incident sound, reflected sound, and normal all lie in the same plane

Surfaces that reflect sound

  • 🪨 Rocks, cliffs, mountains
  • 🧱 Brick walls, concrete
  • 💎 Polished metal surfaces
  • 🪟 Glass windows

Surfaces that ABSORB sound

  • 🧣 Curtains, soft furnishings
  • 🛋️ Sofas, carpets
  • 👫 Crowds of people (clothing absorbs)
  • 📚 Books in a library

🗣️ Section K: Echo

An ECHO is the sound we hear when our voice REFLECTS BACK from a distant surface.

Condition for an Echo

For a CLEAR echo, the reflected sound must reach our ear at least 0.1 seconds AFTER the original sound.

Our brain holds a sound for ~0.1 second (persistence of hearing). If reflected sound comes back faster, it merges with original sound.

Minimum distance for an echo

Speed of sound in air ≈ 340 m/s

Total path (to wall and back) = 340 × 0.1 = 34 m

So the wall must be at least 17 metres away for an echo!

Where you'll experience echoes

  • 🏔️ Mountains and canyons
  • 🏛️ Empty large halls (Gol Gumbaz in Bijapur is famous!)
  • 🌊 Large empty caves
  • 🏚️ Empty rooms

🏛️ Section L: Reverberation

Reverberation is the PERSISTENCE of sound due to MULTIPLE reflections.

In large halls, sound bounces off walls, ceiling, floor — creating overlapping reflections that take time to die out.

How to reduce reverberation in auditoriums/concert halls

  • 🪟 Use sound-absorbing materials on walls and ceiling (foam, fibreboard)
  • 🛋️ Carpet the floor
  • 🪑 Use upholstered seats
  • 🎭 Curtains and drapes
  • 🔻 Special acoustic panels with grooves

Without reduction, music in a hall would sound like a muddled mess!

📡 Section M: Uses of Multiple Reflection of Sound

1. Megaphone / Loudhailer / Bullhorn

Funnel-shaped device that channels sound in one direction. Reflections inside the cone reinforce the sound.

Used by: traffic police, sports coaches, leaders addressing crowds.

2. Stethoscope

Used by doctors to listen to heartbeats and lung sounds.

Heart sounds reach the doctor's ears via multiple reflections inside the tube.

3. Soundboards behind speakers

Curved boards in concert halls/auditoriums reflect speaker's voice to reach the entire audience.

4. Curved ceilings in concert halls

Reflect sound evenly across the audience hall.

🌊 Section N: SONAR

SONAR = SOund Navigation And Ranging

SONAR uses ULTRASONIC waves to detect underwater objects.

How SONAR Works

  1. Ship transmits ultrasonic waves downward
  2. Waves travel through water
  3. Hit an object (submarine, fish school, ocean floor)
  4. Reflect back (echo)
  5. Detector on ship receives echo
  6. Time delay → calculate distance

Formula

Distance = (Speed × Time) ÷ 2
The "÷ 2" is because sound travels there AND back.

Applications of SONAR

  • 🚢 Naval ships detect enemy submarines
  • 🐟 Fishing boats locate schools of fish
  • 🌊 Mapping ocean floor (bathymetry)
  • 🚢 Detecting underwater obstacles (icebergs)
  • 🦦 Studying marine life
  • ⛏️ Locating wrecks and lost treasure

Example: A SONAR pulse returns in 4 seconds. If speed of sound in water = 1500 m/s, find the depth.

Distance = (v × t) ÷ 2 = (1500 × 4) ÷ 2 = 3000 m

🦇 Section O: Ultrasound and its Uses

Ultrasound: sound waves with frequency ABOVE 20,000 Hz.

Medical Uses

  • 🤰 Pregnancy scans — see the baby in the womb!
  • 🩺 Imaging internal organs (liver, kidney, gallbladder)
  • 💎 Breaking kidney stones (lithotripsy)
  • 🦷 Cleaning teeth at dentist's office
  • 🧠 Cleaning surgical instruments

Industrial Uses

  • 🔧 Detecting cracks in metal pipes and aircraft parts
  • 🧼 Cleaning intricate machinery
  • 📡 Welding plastics
  • 🔬 Quality control in factories

Animals That Use Ultrasound

  • 🦇 Bats: emit ultrasonic squeaks and use echoes for navigation (echolocation)
  • 🐬 Dolphins: communicate and hunt using ultrasound
  • 🐋 Whales: communicate over thousands of kilometres
  • 🦗 Moths: detect bats' ultrasonic calls

👂 Section P: The Human Ear

Our ears are remarkable sound detection organs! Three main parts:

1. Outer Ear

  • Pinna: visible ear that COLLECTS sound waves
  • Ear canal: tube that funnels sound to eardrum

2. Middle Ear

  • Eardrum (tympanic membrane): vibrates with incoming sound
  • Three tiny bones: hammer, anvil, stirrup — amplify vibrations 22 times!

3. Inner Ear

  • Cochlea: snail-shaped fluid-filled tube
  • Hair cells in cochlea convert vibrations to NERVE SIGNALS
  • Auditory nerve: carries signals to BRAIN

Ear Diagram

🧮 Section Q: More Numerical Problems

P6. SONAR sends a pulse and receives echo in 3 seconds. Speed of sound in water = 1500 m/s. Find depth.

d = (v × t)/2 = (1500 × 3)/2 = 2250 m

P7. A girl hears an echo from a cliff 0.6 seconds after shouting. How far is the cliff?

Distance = (340 × 0.6)/2 = 102 m

P8. Calculate frequency if wavelength = 2.5 m and speed = 340 m/s.

f = v/λ = 340/2.5 = 136 Hz

P9. An ultrasound has frequency 50,000 Hz and speed 1500 m/s in water. Find wavelength.

λ = v/f = 1500/50,000 = 0.03 m = 3 cm

P10. Why can't you hear sound underwater the same way as in air?

Sound travels FASTER in water (~1500 m/s vs 340 m/s in air). Also, water absorbs some frequencies differently, and our ears are designed for air.

📢 Section R: Noise Pollution

Noise pollution is unwanted or excessive sound that harms human health and the environment.

Sources of Noise Pollution

  • 🚗 Traffic (horns, engines)
  • 🏭 Industries and factories
  • 🚁 Aircraft, trains
  • 🎉 Loudspeakers at events
  • 🎆 Firecrackers
  • 🔨 Construction work
  • 📺 Loud music and TVs

Harmful Effects

  • 👂 Hearing loss (especially above 80 dB for long time)
  • 😰 Stress and anxiety
  • 😴 Sleep disturbance
  • 📉 Reduced concentration and productivity
  • ❤️ High blood pressure, heart problems
  • 🐦 Affects wildlife behaviour

How to Reduce Noise Pollution

  • 🌳 Plant trees along roads — they absorb sound
  • 🚫 Strict laws on horn usage and loudspeakers
  • 🔇 Install soundproofing in buildings
  • 🎧 Use earmuffs/earplugs in noisy areas
  • 🔧 Better-designed machines, mufflers on vehicles
  • 🏥 Designate silent zones near hospitals and schools

🎵 Section S: Musical Instruments and Sound Production

All musical instruments produce sound by VIBRATION!

String Instruments

Sound comes from vibrating strings.

  • 🎸 Guitar, sitar, veena: pluck strings
  • 🎻 Violin, sarangi: bow strings
  • 🎹 Piano: hammers hit strings

Wind Instruments

Sound comes from vibrating air columns.

  • 🎷 Saxophone, clarinet
  • 🎺 Trumpet, bugle
  • 🪈 Flute, bansuri
  • 🎷 Shehnai (Indian classical)

Percussion Instruments

Sound from vibrating membranes or solid bodies.

  • 🥁 Drum, tabla, mridangam, dholak
  • 🎯 Cymbals, ghatam
  • 🔔 Bells, xylophone

🚀 Section T: Supersonic Speeds and Sonic Boom

Mach number = Speed of object ÷ Speed of sound in air

  • Mach 1 = Speed of sound (~340 m/s = 1224 km/h)
  • Mach 2 = Twice the speed of sound
  • Below Mach 1 = subsonic
  • Above Mach 1 = supersonic
  • Above Mach 5 = hypersonic

Sonic Boom

When an aircraft exceeds the speed of sound, it creates a shock wave heard as a LOUD BOOM on the ground.

Concorde aircraft used to fly at Mach 2 — created sonic booms over oceans!

📋 Section U: MCQs

M1. Sound waves are:

(a) Transverse (b) Longitudinal (c) Electromagnetic (d) None → (b) Longitudinal

M2. Audible range for humans:

(a) 1-20 Hz (b) 20-20,000 Hz (c) 20-50,000 Hz (d) 1-1000 Hz → (b)

M3. Sound CANNOT travel through:

(a) Air (b) Water (c) Iron (d) Vacuum → (d) Vacuum

M4. Pitch of sound depends on:

(a) Amplitude (b) Frequency (c) Speed (d) Wavelength → (b) Frequency

M5. Loudness depends on:

(a) Amplitude (b) Frequency (c) Speed (d) Both A and B → (a) Amplitude

M6. Sound travels FASTEST in:

(a) Air (b) Water (c) Iron (d) Wood → (c) Iron (solids)

M7. Minimum distance for echo in air:

(a) 10 m (b) 17 m (c) 34 m (d) 100 m → (b) 17 m

M8. SONAR uses:

(a) Infrasound (b) Audible sound (c) Ultrasound (d) Light → (c) Ultrasound

M9. Bats use ____ for navigation.

(a) Eyes (b) Echolocation (ultrasound) (c) Smell (d) Touch → (b)

M10. Unit of frequency:

(a) Hz (b) m/s (c) N/m (d) Pa → (a) Hertz

🎯 Section V: Long Answer Questions

L1. Explain the production and propagation of sound with examples.

Sound is produced when an object vibrates. The vibrations push air particles, creating compressions and rarefactions that travel as waves. Example: striking a drum creates membrane vibrations → air vibrations → wave travels to ear.

L2. Why is sound called a longitudinal wave?

Because particles of medium vibrate PARALLEL (along) to the direction the wave travels — back-and-forth motion creating compressions and rarefactions.

L3. Describe the working of a SONAR system with an example.

SONAR sends ultrasonic waves into water. Waves bounce off objects (fish, ships, ocean floor) and return. By measuring the time delay and using d = (v×t)/2, distance is calculated. Used in: locating submarines, mapping ocean floor, finding fish schools.

L4. How does the human ear work?

1. Pinna collects sound. 2. Sound travels through ear canal to eardrum. 3. Eardrum vibrates. 4. Three tiny bones (hammer, anvil, stirrup) amplify vibrations. 5. Vibrations reach cochlea (fluid-filled). 6. Hair cells convert to nerve signals. 7. Auditory nerve sends to brain.

L5. Distinguish between pitch and loudness with examples.

Pitch depends on FREQUENCY — high pitch = shrill (whistle), low pitch = deep (drum). Loudness depends on AMPLITUDE — large amplitude = loud (shouting), small amplitude = soft (whisper).

L6. Describe an experiment to show sound needs a medium.

Bell jar experiment: electric bell inside vacuum jar. With air, bell is heard. As air is pumped out, sound fades. In vacuum, no sound heard even though bell is ringing. Proves sound needs medium.

📚 Section W: Glossary

Term Meaning
SoundForm of energy produced by vibration
Wavelength (λ)Distance between two consecutive compressions/rarefactions
Frequency (f)Oscillations per second; unit Hz
AmplitudeMax displacement from rest
Time period (T)Time for one oscillation
PitchHow shrill or deep (frequency)
LoudnessHow intense (amplitude); dB
EchoReflected sound heard after 0.1s
ReverberationPersistence due to multiple reflections
SONARSound Navigation And Ranging
UltrasoundFrequency above 20,000 Hz
InfrasoundFrequency below 20 Hz
DecibelUnit of loudness
EcholocationNavigation using sound echoes

🔊 Sound chapter — mastered!

🎓 Section X: Common Mistakes to Avoid

  • ❌ Saying sound travels in vacuum. It does NOT — sound needs a medium.
  • ❌ Confusing pitch with loudness. Pitch = frequency. Loudness = amplitude.
  • ❌ Thinking sound is a transverse wave. Sound is LONGITUDINAL.
  • ❌ Forgetting to divide by 2 in SONAR distance formula (sound travels there AND back).
  • ❌ Confusing echo with reverberation. Echo = one distinct reflection; reverberation = many overlapping reflections.
  • ❌ Saying audible range is 0-20,000 Hz. Lower limit is 20 Hz.
  • ❌ Thinking ultrasound is loud sound. Ultrasound = high FREQUENCY, not loudness.
